## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=Optical anisotropy of uniaxially drawn and silver-dispersed polyimide films | lines=65-147 | keywords=ElectricalDielectricProperties, MechanicalProperties, OpticalProperties, PhysicalProperties, ProcessingProperties
65: # Optical anisotropy of uniaxially drawn and silver-dispersed polyimide films
66: 
67: Takashi Sawada, $^{a)}$  Shinji Ando, $^{b)}$  and Shigekuni Sasaki
68: 
69: NTT Science and Core Technology Laboratory Group, 3-9-11 Midori-cho, Musashino-shi, Tokyo,
70: 
71: 180-8585, Japan
72: 
73: (Received 17 August 1998; accepted for publication 10 December 1998)
74: 
75: Distinct anisotropy in optical transmittance in the visible and near-infrared region for uniaxially drawn and silver-dispersed polyimide films was observed. The films were prepared in a one-step operation that involves thermal curing and simultaneous uniaxial drawing of poly(amic acid) (PAA) films, which were made by dissolving silver nitrate in the PAA solution at a 1:4 mol ratio. The polyimide molecular chains with a rod-like structure were oriented along the drawing direction during curing, and this orientation accompanied the generation of silver nanoparticles with elongated shapes. An anisotropy in the optical transmittance of 5:1 was obtained for a  $22 - \mu \mathrm{m}$ -thick film at  $850\mathrm{nm}$  with transmittance of  $68\%$  perpendicular to the drawing direction. The optical and mechanical properties of this film were retained after annealing at  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{h}$ . © 1999
76: 
77: American Institute of Physics. [S0003-6951(99)02107-5]
78: 
79: It has been reported that the polyimide films derived from pyromellitic dianhydride and  $2,2^{\prime}$ -bis(trifluoromethyl)- $4,4^{\prime}$ -diaminobiphenyl (PMDA/TFDB, Fig. 1), which has a rod-like structure, shows a large anisotropy in refractive indices in the film plane (in-plane birefringence:  $\Delta n$ ) by uniaxial drawing during thermal curing. $^{1}$  Comparing with conventional unfluorinated polyimides, this material shows higher transparency in the visible and near-infrared (IR) regions as well as lower dielectric constant, lower refractive indices, and lower water absorption. $^{2}$  The largest  $\Delta n$  of 0.21 was observed at  $1543~\mathrm{nm}$ , $^{3}$  and a thin optical half-waveplate at  $1555~\mathrm{nm}$ , only  $14.5~\mu \mathrm{m}$  thick, which is 6.3 times thinner than a zeroth-order quartz waveplate, was prepared using this polyimide. $^{4,5}$  The  $\Delta n$  of the waveplate was retained after annealing at  $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{h}$ , indicating that the chemical structure and the molecular ordering of the polyimide were maintained up to  $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
80: 
81: Polyimide films containing metal/metal oxide particles generated in situ have been studied extensively by Taylor and co-workers. They have also incorporated silver salts and organosilver complexes in aromatic polyimides and obtained highly reflective silver-containing polyimide films. We have recently prepared five kinds of metal [aluminum, copper, palladium, silver(Ag), and gold(Au)]-containing fluorinated polyimide films in order to develop thermally stable polymeric materials that exhibit optical functions and high transparency in the near-infrared region. Although all the films show much less transparency in the visible region than the host polyimide, their transmittance in the near-infrared region (at  $1550\mathrm{nm}$ ) is higher than  $80\%$  except for the Au-containing film.
82: 
83: Stookey and Araujo<sup>11</sup> first observed a large anisotropy in optical transmittance (polarizing property) in a stretched inorganic glass containing silver particles. They confirmed that the anisotropy originates from the differences in the selective
84: 
85: absorption wavelengths parallel and perpendicular to the longer axis of elongated metal particles and, thus, the wavelengths with high extinction ratio (that is the difference in transmittance between two polarizing directions) can be shifted by changing the size and the anisotropy of the silver particles. Using this principle, Gritz<sup>12</sup> fabricated high-efficiency glass polarizers with transmittance higher than  $90\%$  and contrast larger than 500:1. Baba and co-workers<sup>13-15</sup> investigated the optical anisotropy caused by the elongated silver islands embedded in glasses. In our study, in a one-step operation, we generated silver nanoparticles having elongated shapes in a thermally stable polymeric material. The preparation method is schematically shown in Fig. 2. As shown by Taylor and co-workers<sup>7-9</sup> and these authors,<sup>10</sup> silver salts and organosilver complexes are reduced to the metal state and precipitated as silver nanoparticles in polyimide films by thermal curing. We confirmed by transmission electron micrographs that the silver particles precipitated in a rod-like fluorinated polyimide had spherical shapes.<sup>10</sup> This indicates that the local molecular structure of polyimide does not affect the shape of the precipitated particles. However, when the polyimide molecular chains are highly oriented by uniaxial drawing during curing, the shapes of the silver particles are expected to be elongated along the drawing direction.
86: 
87: The fluorinated polyimide, PMDA/TFDB, was used as a matrix polymer in this study because of its properties described above. The preparation of  $N,N$ -dimethylacetamide solution of poly(amic acid) (PAA), the precursor of polyimide, has been described elsewhere. Silver nitrate  $(\mathrm{AgNO}_3)$
88: 
89: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
90: PMDA / TFDB  
91: FIG. 1. Structural formula for rod-like fluorinated polyimide.
92: 
93: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
94: FIG. 2. Method for preparing optically anisotropic polyimide films.
95: 
96: was used as a source material of silver particles because it has good solubility in PAA solution. After drying at  $110^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{h}$ ,  $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$  was dissolved in the PAA solution under nitrogen.  $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$  was added into the PAAs in a 1:4 mol ratio. The solutions were stirred for  $12\mathrm{h}$  at room temperature and spin coated onto a 4 in. silicon wafer, followed by drying in nitrogen at  $70^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{h}$ .
97: 
98: Figure 3 shows the absorption spectra of the silver-doped PAA and polyimide films cured on silica substrates at various temperatures for  $1\mathrm{h}$ . No optical anisotropy was observed in the film plane for all the films. An absorption peak is clearly observed at  $430~\mathrm{nm}$  for the film cured at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . This peak wavelength coincides with that of the surface plasmon resonance of Ag colloids reported in the literature.[16,17] This indicates that  $\mathrm{AgNO_3}$  incorporated into polyimide was reduced to Ag by the thermal treatment at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and, consequently, silver nanoparticles were generated. As the curing temperature increases, the absorption band at  $430~\mathrm{nm}$  grows and looks slightly displaced to longer wavelengths, suggesting that the silver particles increased in size. The different shape of the spectrum that was observed for the film cured at  $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  can be understood from its appearance; a reflective surface with silvery color was formed on the air-side surface.
99: 
100: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
101: FIG. 3. Changes in absorption spectra of fluorinated poly(amic acid) films added  $\mathrm{AgNO_3}$  for final curing temperatures of  $70 - 350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . At around  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the poly(amic acid) is thermally converted to polyimide, and Ag nanoparticles begin to coagulate.
102: 
103: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
104: FIG. 4. Optical transmittance spectra of  $22 - \mu \mathrm{m}$  -thick uniaxially drawn Ag-nanoparticle-dispersed and non-dispersed polyimide films for normally incident light polarized perpendicular  $(T_{\perp})$  and parallel  $(T_{\parallel})$  to the drawing direction. The heating rate was  $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ , and the load ranged from 5 to  $18\mathrm{g}$ .
105: 
106: This indicates that Ag particles were concentrated near the air-side surface. Since the formation of a reflective surface is undesirable for the use of films as an optically anisotropic film such as a polarizer, thermal curing below  $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  is preferable for that purpose.
107: 
108: For preparing uniaxially drawn silver-dispersed polyimide films, PAA films peeled from the substrate were cut into rectangles of  $15 \times 5 \mathrm{~mm}$  and loaded in a thermal mechanical analyzer (TMA: Sinku-Riko TM-7000). A constant load was applied along the longer axis of the film by the TMA during thermal curing. The film was thus heated to  $320^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$  at a heating rate of 5 and  $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{min}$ , kept at this temperature for  $1 \mathrm{~h}$  and then cooled to room temperature. The optical anisotropy of the films was measured by rotating a Glan-Thompson prism (polarizer) inserted into the light path of a spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-3500, wavelengths:  $600 - 1300 \mathrm{~nm}$ ). The average thicknesses of the polyimide films were  $22 \mu \mathrm{m}$ .
109: 
110: Figure 4 shows the transmittance spectra of the silver-dispersed polyimide films uniaxially drawn during curing at the heating rate of  $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$  with varying the load of the TMA from 5 to  $18\mathrm{g}$ . All films prepared at  $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$  were cut around  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The spectrum of the polyimide film prepared from undoped PAA is also shown for comparison. The nonsilver-dispersed film showed much higher transmittance in a wide range but no optical anisotropy. All the uniaxially drawn and silver-dispersed films, on the other hand, exhibited distinct anisotropy in optical transmittance in the visible and near-IR region. The transmittance parallel to the drawing direction is considerably lower than that perpendicular to it. This phenomenon can be explained by the same mechanism as that for stretched glasses containing silver particles. In addition, the extinction ratios increased as the load increased. An extinction ratio of  $7\mathrm{dB}$  (1:5 in transmittance ratio) was obtained at  $850~\mathrm{nm}$  for a film cured with a load of  $18\mathrm{g}$ .
111: 
112: Temperature dependence of elongation behavior of the films is shown in Fig. 5. Compared with the undoped PAA, the doped PAA films showed larger shrinkage between 100 and  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and sudden elongation above  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . In addition, the elongation increased as the load increased. The magnitude of the sudden elongation should be important for induc
113: 
114: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
115: FIG. 5. Elongation behaviors of  $\mathrm{AgNO_3}$ -doped and undoped poly(amic acid) films for various loads. The final curing temperature was  $320^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
116: 
117: ing a high degree of molecular orientation of polyimides, which is the main cause that generates silver particles with elongated shapes.
118: 
119: A high-resolution reflection image of an uniaxially drawn film (18 g in Fig. 5) observed by scanning electron micrograph (JEOL JSM-890) is shown in Fig. 6. Some of the particles have elongated shapes and their longer axes are oriented to the drawing direction. The sizes of these particles were estimated to be several tens of nm along the longer axes and several nm along the shorter axes. The difference in transmittance between the two polarization directions and the wavelength dispersion of optical anisotropy should be determined by size, aspect ratio, degree of orientation of the longer axis, and anisotropic distribution of the silver particles precipitated in a film. Hence, the wavelength dependence of the optical transmittance and anisotropy can be controlled by varying the curing conditions, the drawing conditions, and the kind of dopant.
120: 
121: In this study, the highest curing temperature of silver-dispersed polyimide films was  $310 - 340^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  lower than that used for inorganic glass matrices, $^{14}$  and the highest temperature was kept for  $1\mathrm{h}$  after the elongation of the films. It is surprising that the elongated shapes of the silver particles in
122: 
123: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
124: FIG. 6. High-resolution reflection image of Ag-nanoparticle-dispersed film observed using scanning electron micrography.
125: 
126: the polyimide films do not relax to spheres at the elongation temperature of  $320^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . This is due the low elongation temperature of  $\mathrm{Ag}$  particles and the high molecular order of the surrounding polyimide chains. In the conventional processes, metal nanoparticles are formed or embedded in a matrix glass and then elongated by uniaxial drawing with the glass plate. However, in the present method, the precipitation and elongation of silver nanoparticles take place in one step. The fact that an absorption peak of silver nanoparticles was observed even for an isotropic film cured at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and that it grew considerably at higher temperatures, suggests the following mechanism: (a) silver nanoparticles, which are likely to have spherical shapes, begin to precipitate from polyimide around  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; (b) the precipitation become vigorous, and the particles begin to coagulate or sinter between 250 and  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; and (c) spherical silver particles are elongated along the drawing direction between 300 and  $320^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  while the polyimide film significantly elongates. Uniaxially oriented polyimide molecules provide an anisotropic environment for the growth of silver nanoparticles. The melting temperature of bulk silver is higher than  $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; however, it has been reported that sintering of silver nanoparticles begins at  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
127: 
128: The silver-dispersed elongated polyimide films showed good flexibility and tractability although they were cleavable along the drawing direction. The thickness of the films ( $\sim 22 \mu \mathrm{m}$ ) is sufficiently thin for decreasing the excess loss caused when films are inserted into a groove.[4,5] Furthermore, no change was observed in the transmittance spectrum, the optical anisotropy, and the flexibility after annealing at  $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$  for  $1 \mathrm{~h}$ . The uniaxially drawn and silver-dispersed polyimide films affords thin, flexible, and thermally stable films that exhibit anisotropy in optical transmittance in the visible and near-infrared region.
129:

## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussions | lines=94-101 | keywords=ThermalProperties
94: Figure 3. Thermal and thermo-oxidative stabilities of the CPI films; (a) DMA curves; (b) TGA curves in air; (c) TGA curves in  $\mathrm{N}_2$ .
95: 
96: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)
97: 
98: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)
99: 
100: Compared with the boron-free film (CPI-0), incorporating carborane into the polyimide backbone structure greatly enhanced polymer thermal and thermo-oxidative stabilities (Figure 3b,c). Both thermal and thermo-oxidative stability were improved with increasing boron-loadings. The initial thermal decomposition temperature (<1.0 wt.% of the original weight) were detected over  $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  in air,  $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  higher than that of CPI-0. The  $T_{5}$  in  $\mathbf{N}_2$  were measured in the range of  $595 - 601^{\circ}\mathrm{C}, 13 - 19^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  higher than CPI-0, and apparently did not change with the boron-loadings. However, the  $T_{5}$  in air increased from  $602^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  (CPI-5) to  $632^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  (CPI-50) with increasing boron-loadings. At  $50\mathrm{mol}.\%$  of carborane loading (CPI-50), the  $T_{10}$  was measured at  $657^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  (in  $\mathbf{N}_2$ ) and  $749^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  (in air), respectively, and the char yield at  $750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  was as high as  $87.9\%$  (in  $\mathbf{N}_2$ ) and  $90.0\%$  (in air), indicating obvious improvement in thermo-oxidative stability. Moreover, the TGA analysis manifested that some weight gains were observed for the CPI films beginning at about  $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  in air, which was probably attributed to the oxidation of boron atoms in the carborane cage to produce a boron oxide layer on the film surface, which could retard the further erosion of inner polymers [39].
101:

## excerpt 2 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussions | lines=128-148 | keywords=MechanicalProperties, ThermalProperties
128: # 3.4. Mechanism of Thermo-Oxidative Resistance
129: 
130: The extraordinary thermo-oxidative stabilities of the CPI films might be attributed to the formation of the boron oxide protective layer on the film surface, preventing the direct contact of inner polymer with oxygen molecules and resulting in the reduced decomposition rate of the inner polymer. Hence, the sheltered inner polymer could still provide the film with certain mechanical strength and flexibility even after thermo-oxidative aging at  $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The thermal degradation kinetics in air was analyzed via TGA with the Flynn-Wall-Ozawa method [40,41], based on the following Equation (2), which is a derivate from Equation (1):
131: 
132: $$
133: \log \beta = \log \frac {A E _ {a}}{R g (\alpha)} - 2. 3 1 5 - \frac {0 . 4 5 7 E _ {a}}{R T}, \tag {1}
134: $$
135: 
136: $$
137: \frac {\mathrm {d} \log \beta}{\mathrm {d} (1 / T)} = \frac {0 . 4 5 7 E _ {a}}{R}, \tag {2}
138: $$
139: 
140: where  $\beta$  is the heating rate (K min $^{-1}$ ),  $R$  is the gas constant (8.314 J moL $^{-1}$  K $^{-1}$ ),  $E_{a}$  is the activation energy (kJ moL $^{-1}$ ),  $T$  is the absolute temperature (K), and  $g(\alpha)$  is the function of conversion rate  $\alpha$ .
141: 
142: TGA of the CPI films with various boron-loadings (CPI-0, CPI-20, CPI-50) were performed in air at different heating rates. Figure 6 compares the DTG (Derivative Thermogravimetry) curves of CPI films with different carborane loadings. The two intersection points of the two tangents of the decomposition peak and baseline were set as initial degradation temperature  $(T_{i})$  and terminal degradation temperature  $(T_{t})$ . The initial weight residue was regarded as the weight residue at the initial degradation temperature and the same as the terminal weight residue. Then, the section between the initial and terminal weight residue was equally divided into 10 pieces, and each weight residue represented a certain conversion rate  $\alpha$ . According to the conversion rate  $\alpha$  and heating rate  $\beta$ ,  $T_{\alpha}$  (temperature at conversion rate  $\alpha$ ) could be directly determined in the corresponding TGA curves. Subsequently, plots of  $\lg \beta$  against  $1000 / T$  were linearly fitted to a series of straight lines in which the slopes were used to compute the activation energy  $E_{a}$  at the corresponding conversion rate  $\alpha$ , as
143: 
144: exhibited in Figure 7a. All of the generated lines were in excellent linear dependence with  $\mathbb{R}^2 >0.98$ , as summarized in Table S3.
145: 
146: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
147: Figure 6. DTG curves of (a) CPI-0, (b) CPI-20, and (c) CPI-50 under various heating rates.

Figure 6 compares the DTG (Derivative Thermogravimetry) curves of CPI films with different carborane loadings. The two intersection points of the two tangents of the decomposition peak and baseline were set as initial degradation temperature $(T_{i})$ and terminal degradation temperature $(T_{t})$ . The initial weight residue was regarded as the weight residue at the initial degradation temperature and the same as the terminal weight residue. Then, the section between the initial and terminal weight residue was equally divided into 10 pieces, and each weight residue represented a certain conversion rate $\alpha$ . According to the conversion rate $\alpha$ and heating rate $\beta$ , $T_{\alpha}$ (temperature at conversion rate $\alpha$ ) could be directly determined in the corresponding TGA curves. 100: Compared with the boron-free film (CPI-0), incorporating carborane into the polyimide backbone structure greatly enhanced polymer thermal and thermo-oxidative stabilities (Figure 3b,c). Both thermal and thermo-oxidative stability were improved with increasing boron-loadings. The initial thermal decomposition temperature (<1.0 wt.% of the original weight) were detected over  $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  in air,  $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  higher than that of CPI-0. The  $T_{5}$  in  $\mathbf{N}_2$  were measured in the range of  $595 - 601^{\circ}\mathrm{C}, 13 - 19^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  higher than CPI-0, and apparently did not change with the boron-loadings. However, the  $T_{5}$  in air increased from  $602^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  (CPI-5) to  $632^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  (CPI-50) with increasing boron-loadings. At  $50\mathrm{mol}.\%$  of carborane loading (CPI-50), the  $T_{10}$  was measured at  $657^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  (in  $\mathbf{N}_2$ ) and  $749^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  (in air), respectively, and the char yield at  $750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  was as high as  $87.9\%$  (in  $\mathbf{N}_2$ ) and  $90.0\%$  (in air), indicating obvious improvement in thermo-oxidative stability. Moreover, the TGA analysis manifested that some weight gains were observed for the CPI films beginning at about  $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  in air, which was probably attributed to the oxidation of boron atoms in the carborane cage to produce a boron oxide layer on the film surface, which could retard the further erosion of inner polymers [39].

## excerpt 2 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussions | lines=128-136 | keywords=MechanicalProperties; family=CPI
128: # 3.4. Mechanism of Thermo-Oxidative Resistance
129: 
130: The extraordinary thermo-oxidative stabilities of the CPI films might be attributed to the formation of the boron oxide protective layer on the film surface, preventing the direct contact of inner polymer with oxygen molecules and resulting in the reduced decomposition rate of the inner polymer. Hence, the sheltered inner polymer could still provide the film with certain mechanical strength and flexibility even after thermo-oxidative aging at  $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The thermal degradation kinetics in air was analyzed via TGA with the Flynn-Wall-Ozawa method [40,41], based on the following Equation (2), which is a derivate from Equation (1):
131: 
132: $$
133: \log \beta = \log \frac {A E _ {a}}{R g (\alpha)} - 2. 3 1 5 - \frac {0 . 4 5 7 E _ {a}}{R T}, \tag {1}
134: $$
135: 
136: $$

Then, the section between the initial and terminal weight residue was equally divided into 10 pieces, and each weight residue represented a certain conversion rate $\alpha$ . According to the conversion rate $\alpha$ and heating rate $\beta$ , $T_{\alpha}$ (temperature at conversion rate $\alpha$ ) could be directly determined in the corresponding TGA curves. Subsequently, plots of $\lg \beta$ against $1000 / T$ were linearly fitted to a series of straight lines in which the slopes were used to compute the activation energy $E_{a}$ at the corresponding conversion rate $\alpha$ , as 143: ## excerpt 7 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussions | lines=159-161 | keywords=ProcessingProperties; anchors=CPI-50; family=CPI, CPI-50 159: 160: To further investigate the mechanism, three representatives with different carbon-loadings (CPI-0, CPI-20, and CPI-50) were isothermally aged by TGA in air at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $150\mathrm{min}$ (Figure 8a). Apparently, the thermo-oxidative stability was greatly improved by increasing the carborane loadings. Since the boron-free CPI film (CPI-0) was completely decomposed into volatiles rapidly, whereas the CPI-20 and CPI-50 film still maintained more than $29\%$ and $76\%$ weight residue after $150\mathrm{min}$ of thermo-oxidative degradation at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. In addition, the boron-free CPI film (CPI-0) was easier to decompose with a degradation peak more intense than the carborane-containing films (CPI-20 and CPI-50). Combined with DTG curves (Figure 8b), a second sharp weight loss peak immediately followed the first for CPI-0 in air. However, only one decomposition peak was observed for CPI-50 in 161:

Materials and Methods | lines=122-130 | keywords=OpticalProperties 122: The infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) experiments were carried out on FT-IR Bruker Vertex 70 instrument. 123: 124: Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was experimented on a Mettler Toledo DSC 1 (Mettler Toledo, Greifensee, Switzerland) instrument. The polymer specimens were incorporated in some aluminum pans and subjected to a heating rate of $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\min^{-1}$ (under $\mathbf{N}_2$ medium). 125: 126: Optical microscopy of the laser-exposed films was performed on a ADL 601 P Bresser device (Bresser, Rhede, Germany) in ambient temperature. 127: 128: Illuminance and colorimetry studies were undertaken on CL-70F device (Konica Minolta, INC., Tokyo, Japan). 129: 130: Refractometry and birefringence analyses were undertaken on DR-M4 (Atago Co., Ltd., Saitama, Japan) instrument under variable wavelength. [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=176-183 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=SPIN25; family=SPIN25 176: The thermal characteristics are essential for the utilization of these polymer materials in optical storage. The DSC experiments allowed the evaluation of the glass transition temperature (Tg) of each sample (see Supplementary Material, Figures S1-S5). The flexibility of the main chain is enhanced by the presence of the $\mathrm{H}_3\mathrm{C} - \mathrm{C} - \mathrm{CH}_3$ and $-\mathrm{O}-$ bridges that affect the internal rotation barrier and, implicitly, the magnitude of the Tg. According to Table 3, the inserted amount of azomonomer introduced in the supramolecular systems is slightly influencing the intermolecular interactions, and this is reflected in the sample thermal behavior. Hence, a higher quantity of the azochromophore is diminishing the interactions among the chains features, and through this, the Tg is slowly reduced with regard to the sample without azo dye (Figure S1). It is known that a bigger glass transition temperature is indicative of a higher chain rigidity, which, in turn, is linked to a larger residual birefringence [39]. However, in such situations, the maximum generated 177: 178: birefringence (by various external factors) is lower, presuming a constant chromophore concentration. Thus, the reduction in $\mathrm{Tg}$ by the presence of more AzoCN is beneficial for inducing a larger birefringence to the PI-based supramolecular systems. In any case, the $\mathrm{Tg}$ values are above $160^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which makes the examined PI-based supramolecular systems suitable for the optical storage uses. Moreover, based on the experimentally registered $\mathrm{Tg}$ of the samples, it is possible to extract information on the volumetric coefficient of thermal expansion (noted here as $\alpha_{\mathrm{v}}$ ), as described in the work of Bicerano [40]. The obtained values are listed in Table 3, where it can be noted that the value of this parameter slightly changes upon enhancing the amount of the azo derivative in the sample. Thus, the small increase in $\alpha_{\mathrm{v}}$ reveals a propensity for variation in the size of the sample at temperatures reaching the vitrification point. 179: 180: Table 3. Figure 7 illustrates the spectral distribution of the visible radiations emitted by the LED, which are changed due to the presence of each PI/AzoCN system. The peak max parameter reveals the biggest wavelength optically emitted from the light source in the presence of the samples [43]. It can be observed that the peak max slightly increases with the addition of the azochromophore. In addition, the magnitude of the recorded illuminance is gradually reduced upon more azo dye incorporation. After laser irradiation, the illuminance values are enhanced, while the peak max values are slightly reduced with regard to the non-irradiated films. This might be due to the surface structuring which diminishes the light reflection into the incident medium and favors its propagation through the sample optical medium. 202: 203: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 204: 205: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 206: 207: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ## excerpt 3 | block=b0004 | section=3. 270: 271: The data of refractive index dispersion at energies under the interband absorption edge can be attributed to the fundamental electronic excitation spectrum. The $\mathrm{E_0}$ parameter offers relevant information on the band structure of the polymeric material, while $\mathrm{E_d}$ is connected to the mean potency of interband photosensitive transitions, including structural variations [47]. The results reveal that the introduction of AzoCN in the system determines the diminishment of the strength of the interband transition and the average excitation for electronic transitions (quantified by $\mathrm{E_0}$ ). This aspect is further accentuated by the laser treatment of the samples. The changes in strength of the interband optical transitions reveal the dye-induced modification of the microstructural properties, which are affecting the electron/hole excitations in the energy-dependent fundamental electronic excitation spectrum. In addition to this, as was suggested by Tanaka [48], an estimative value of the optical band gap $(\mathrm{E_g})$ can be obtained from the oscillator energy. The attained $\mathrm{E_g}$ values for the samples before and after UV irradiation are shown in Table 4. The increasing amount of azochromophore has the effect of reducing the band gap of the studied systems. This is consistent with the report of Abdelrazek [44] for sulfone-based polymers doped with another azo dye. It seems that the presence of AzoCN favors the occurrence of localized states close to conduction or valance bands owing to the vacancies, defects and interstitials. This strongly influences the optical band gap energy but does not display a pronounced effect on the oscillator energy. Aside from the azo-component addition, laser exposure enhances the appearance of localized states and lowers the forbidden gap with regard to the non-irradiated counterpart samples. 272: 273: Furthermore, the WDD approach is useful for extracting additional optical parameters, reflecting the linear and non-linear optical properties of the studied systems, such as the zero frequency refractive index $(\mathrm{n}_0)$ , linear optical susceptibility $(\zeta(1))$ , third-order optical susceptibility $(\zeta(3))$ and the nonlinear refractive index $(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{NL}})$ , as shown in Equations (2)-(4): 274: 275: $$ ## excerpt 4 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=306-311 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=PI/AzoCN; family=PI/AzoCN 306: # 3.5.3. Birefringence 307: 308: Birefringence appears to be the key optical parameter of a material that is able to record data in an optical storage device [41]. In the case of writeable media, the 'bit' occurs in the recording layer that is exposed to a laser beam of elevated power during several tens of seconds. Therefore, the writing mechanism in an optical storage system involves an optically generated birefringence. Figure 12 presents the birefringence data of the samples. As noticed, the pristine samples display an inherent birefringence, which is gradually increased by the incorporation of AzoCN. Birefringence was also reported in other polymer/azo dye systems [49]. The power from the laser pulse produces certain modifications in the PI/AzoCN recording layer that are reflected in a larger birefringence (coarse bars from Figure 12). The orientation of the PI-based films is more pronounced as the azo-dye content is larger. In this case, there are more photosensitive molecules in the samples that can suffer light-triggered orientation, hence higher birefringence. The induction of such stable birefringence denotes the good ability of the highly AzoCN-doped samples to store information upon laser irradiation, as demanded by optical storage applications. 309: 310: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 311: Figure 12. Birefringence of the PI-based supramolecular systems with AzoCN content before and after laser exposure. The bar containing a pattern represents the laser-exposed samples.

[POLYMER_ANCHOR] 身份标识: doi:10.3390/polym15041056_SPIN75_01 名称: SPIN75 别名: 聚合物分类编码: PI/AzoCN 聚合物分类名称: Polyimide supramolecular system 样本形态: Film [RESULT_PROPERTY_HINTS] OpticalProperties [PROPERTY_METHODS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0003 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=122-130 | keywords=OpticalProperties 122: The infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) experiments were carried out on FT-IR Bruker Vertex 70 instrument. 123: 124: Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was experimented on a Mettler Toledo DSC 1 (Mettler Toledo, Greifensee, Switzerland) instrument. The polymer specimens were incorporated in some aluminum pans and subjected to a heating rate of $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\min^{-1}$ (under $\mathbf{N}_2$ medium). 125: 126: Optical microscopy of the laser-exposed films was performed on a ADL 601 P Bresser device (Bresser, Rhede, Germany) in ambient temperature. 127: 128: Illuminance and colorimetry studies were undertaken on CL-70F device (Konica Minolta, INC., Tokyo, Japan). 129: 130: Refractometry and birefringence analyses were undertaken on DR-M4 (Atago Co., Ltd., Saitama, Japan) instrument under variable wavelength. [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=176-183 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=SPIN75; family=SPIN75 176: The thermal characteristics are essential for the utilization of these polymer materials in optical storage. The DSC experiments allowed the evaluation of the glass transition temperature (Tg) of each sample (see Supplementary Material, Figures S1-S5). The flexibility of the main chain is enhanced by the presence of the $\mathrm{H}_3\mathrm{C} - \mathrm{C} - \mathrm{CH}_3$ and $-\mathrm{O}-$ bridges that affect the internal rotation barrier and, implicitly, the magnitude of the Tg. According to Table 3, the inserted amount of azomonomer introduced in the supramolecular systems is slightly influencing the intermolecular interactions, and this is reflected in the sample thermal behavior. Hence, a higher quantity of the azochromophore is diminishing the interactions among the chains features, and through this, the Tg is slowly reduced with regard to the sample without azo dye (Figure S1). It is known that a bigger glass transition temperature is indicative of a higher chain rigidity, which, in turn, is linked to a larger residual birefringence [39]. However, in such situations, the maximum generated 177: 178: birefringence (by various external factors) is lower, presuming a constant chromophore concentration. Thus, the reduction in $\mathrm{Tg}$ by the presence of more AzoCN is beneficial for inducing a larger birefringence to the PI-based supramolecular systems. In any case, the $\mathrm{Tg}$ values are above $160^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which makes the examined PI-based supramolecular systems suitable for the optical storage uses. Moreover, based on the experimentally registered $\mathrm{Tg}$ of the samples, it is possible to extract information on the volumetric coefficient of thermal expansion (noted here as $\alpha_{\mathrm{v}}$ ), as described in the work of Bicerano [40]. The obtained values are listed in Table 3, where it can be noted that the value of this parameter slightly changes upon enhancing the amount of the azo derivative in the sample. Thus, the small increase in $\alpha_{\mathrm{v}}$ reveals a propensity for variation in the size of the sample at temperatures reaching the vitrification point. 179: 180: Table 3. Thermal properties of the analyzed PI-based supramolecular systems: glass transition temperature (Tg) and thermal expansion $(\alpha_{\mathrm{v}})$ . 181: 182:

The peak max parameter reveals the biggest wavelength optically emitted from the light source in the presence of the samples [43]. It can be observed that the peak max slightly increases with the addition of the azochromophore. In addition, the magnitude of the recorded illuminance is gradually reduced upon more azo dye incorporation. After laser irradiation, the illuminance values are enhanced, while the peak max values are slightly reduced with regard to the non-irradiated films. This might be due to the surface structuring which diminishes the light reflection into the incident medium and favors its propagation through the sample optical medium. 202: 203: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 204: 205: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 206: 207: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ## excerpt 3 | block=b0004 | section=3. 270: 271: The data of refractive index dispersion at energies under the interband absorption edge can be attributed to the fundamental electronic excitation spectrum. The $\mathrm{E_0}$ parameter offers relevant information on the band structure of the polymeric material, while $\mathrm{E_d}$ is connected to the mean potency of interband photosensitive transitions, including structural variations [47]. The results reveal that the introduction of AzoCN in the system determines the diminishment of the strength of the interband transition and the average excitation for electronic transitions (quantified by $\mathrm{E_0}$ ). This aspect is further accentuated by the laser treatment of the samples. The changes in strength of the interband optical transitions reveal the dye-induced modification of the microstructural properties, which are affecting the electron/hole excitations in the energy-dependent fundamental electronic excitation spectrum. In addition to this, as was suggested by Tanaka [48], an estimative value of the optical band gap $(\mathrm{E_g})$ can be obtained from the oscillator energy. The attained $\mathrm{E_g}$ values for the samples before and after UV irradiation are shown in Table 4. The increasing amount of azochromophore has the effect of reducing the band gap of the studied systems. This is consistent with the report of Abdelrazek [44] for sulfone-based polymers doped with another azo dye. It seems that the presence of AzoCN favors the occurrence of localized states close to conduction or valance bands owing to the vacancies, defects and interstitials. This strongly influences the optical band gap energy but does not display a pronounced effect on the oscillator energy. Aside from the azo-component addition, laser exposure enhances the appearance of localized states and lowers the forbidden gap with regard to the non-irradiated counterpart samples. 272: 273: Furthermore, the WDD approach is useful for extracting additional optical parameters, reflecting the linear and non-linear optical properties of the studied systems, such as the zero frequency refractive index $(\mathrm{n}_0)$ , linear optical susceptibility $(\zeta(1))$ , third-order optical susceptibility $(\zeta(3))$ and the nonlinear refractive index $(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{NL}})$ , as shown in Equations (2)-(4): 274: 275: $$ ## excerpt 4 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=306-311 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=PI/AzoCN; family=PI/AzoCN 306: # 3.5.3. Birefringence 307: 308: Birefringence appears to be the key optical parameter of a material that is able to record data in an optical storage device [41]. In the case of writeable media, the 'bit' occurs in the recording layer that is exposed to a laser beam of elevated power during several tens of seconds. Therefore, the writing mechanism in an optical storage system involves an optically generated birefringence. Figure 12 presents the birefringence data of the samples. As noticed, the pristine samples display an inherent birefringence, which is gradually increased by the incorporation of AzoCN. Birefringence was also reported in other polymer/azo dye systems [49]. The power from the laser pulse produces certain modifications in the PI/AzoCN recording layer that are reflected in a larger birefringence (coarse bars from Figure 12). The orientation of the PI-based films is more pronounced as the azo-dye content is larger. In this case, there are more photosensitive molecules in the samples that can suffer light-triggered orientation, hence higher birefringence. The induction of such stable birefringence denotes the good ability of the highly AzoCN-doped samples to store information upon laser irradiation, as demanded by optical storage applications. 309: 310: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 311: Figure 12. Birefringence of the PI-based supramolecular systems with AzoCN content before and after laser exposure. The bar containing a pattern represents the laser-exposed samples.

Materials and Methods | lines=122-130 | keywords=OpticalProperties 122: The infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) experiments were carried out on FT-IR Bruker Vertex 70 instrument. 123: 124: Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was experimented on a Mettler Toledo DSC 1 (Mettler Toledo, Greifensee, Switzerland) instrument. The polymer specimens were incorporated in some aluminum pans and subjected to a heating rate of $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\min^{-1}$ (under $\mathbf{N}_2$ medium). 125: 126: Optical microscopy of the laser-exposed films was performed on a ADL 601 P Bresser device (Bresser, Rhede, Germany) in ambient temperature. 127: 128: Illuminance and colorimetry studies were undertaken on CL-70F device (Konica Minolta, INC., Tokyo, Japan). 129: 130: Refractometry and birefringence analyses were undertaken on DR-M4 (Atago Co., Ltd., Saitama, Japan) instrument under variable wavelength. [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=199-207 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=PI/AzoCN; family=PI/AzoCN, PI 199: # 3.5.1. Illuminance and Color Properties 200: 201: The variation of the azo-derivative content in the PI systems produces changes in the appearance of the prepared films. To monitor this, tests of the illuminance and color features of the samples (under a LED light source) were performed. Figure 7 illustrates the spectral distribution of the visible radiations emitted by the LED, which are changed due to the presence of each PI/AzoCN system. The peak max parameter reveals the biggest wavelength optically emitted from the light source in the presence of the samples [43]. It can be observed that the peak max slightly increases with the addition of the azochromophore. In addition, the magnitude of the recorded illuminance is gradually reduced upon more azo dye incorporation. After laser irradiation, the illuminance values are enhanced, while the peak max values are slightly reduced with regard to the non-irradiated films. This might be due to the surface structuring which diminishes the light reflection into the incident medium and favors its propagation through the sample optical medium. 202: 203: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 204: 205: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 206: 207: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ## excerpt 2 | block=b0004 | section=3. 270: 271: The data of refractive index dispersion at energies under the interband absorption edge can be attributed to the fundamental electronic excitation spectrum. The $\mathrm{E_0}$ parameter offers relevant information on the band structure of the polymeric material, while $\mathrm{E_d}$ is connected to the mean potency of interband photosensitive transitions, including structural variations [47]. The results reveal that the introduction of AzoCN in the system determines the diminishment of the strength of the interband transition and the average excitation for electronic transitions (quantified by $\mathrm{E_0}$ ). This aspect is further accentuated by the laser treatment of the samples. The changes in strength of the interband optical transitions reveal the dye-induced modification of the microstructural properties, which are affecting the electron/hole excitations in the energy-dependent fundamental electronic excitation spectrum. In addition to this, as was suggested by Tanaka [48], an estimative value of the optical band gap $(\mathrm{E_g})$ can be obtained from the oscillator energy. The attained $\mathrm{E_g}$ values for the samples before and after UV irradiation are shown in Table 4. The increasing amount of azochromophore has the effect of reducing the band gap of the studied systems. This is consistent with the report of Abdelrazek [44] for sulfone-based polymers doped with another azo dye. It seems that the presence of AzoCN favors the occurrence of localized states close to conduction or valance bands owing to the vacancies, defects and interstitials. This strongly influences the optical band gap energy but does not display a pronounced effect on the oscillator energy. Aside from the azo-component addition, laser exposure enhances the appearance of localized states and lowers the forbidden gap with regard to the non-irradiated counterpart samples. 272: 273: Furthermore, the WDD approach is useful for extracting additional optical parameters, reflecting the linear and non-linear optical properties of the studied systems, such as the zero frequency refractive index $(\mathrm{n}_0)$ , linear optical susceptibility $(\zeta(1))$ , third-order optical susceptibility $(\zeta(3))$ and the nonlinear refractive index $(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{NL}})$ , as shown in Equations (2)-(4): 274: 275: $$ ## excerpt 3 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=306-311 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=PI/AzoCN; family=PI/AzoCN 306: # 3.5.3. Birefringence 307: 308: Birefringence appears to be the key optical parameter of a material that is able to record data in an optical storage device [41]. In the case of writeable media, the 'bit' occurs in the recording layer that is exposed to a laser beam of elevated power during several tens of seconds. Therefore, the writing mechanism in an optical storage system involves an optically generated birefringence. Figure 12 presents the birefringence data of the samples. As noticed, the pristine samples display an inherent birefringence, which is gradually increased by the incorporation of AzoCN. Birefringence was also reported in other polymer/azo dye systems [49]. The power from the laser pulse produces certain modifications in the PI/AzoCN recording layer that are reflected in a larger birefringence (coarse bars from Figure 12). The orientation of the PI-based films is more pronounced as the azo-dye content is larger. In this case, there are more photosensitive molecules in the samples that can suffer light-triggered orientation, hence higher birefringence. The induction of such stable birefringence denotes the good ability of the highly AzoCN-doped samples to store information upon laser irradiation, as demanded by optical storage applications. 309: 310: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 311: Figure 12. Birefringence of the PI-based supramolecular systems with AzoCN content before and after laser exposure. The bar containing a pattern represents the laser-exposed samples.

chain_input.text:
[POLYMER_ANCHOR] 身份标识: doi:10.17576/jsm-2020-4912-25_pure SPI_01 名称: pure SPI 别名: 聚合物分类编码: SPI 聚合物分类名称: sulfonated polyimide 样本形态: Membrane [RESULT_PROPERTY_HINTS] ElectricalDielectricProperties [PROPERTY_METHODS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0012 | section=EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN | lines=94-106 | keywords=ElectricalDielectricProperties 94: # EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN 95: 96: In order to obtain the highest fuel cell membrane performance in terms of physicochemical properties, the synthesis parameters of the composite membrane were optimized using Central Composite Design (CCD), which was one of the methods in response surface methodology (RSM). For this work, three factors or independent variables such as loading of filler (A), annealing temperature (B) and type of filler (C) had been selected as the physicochemical properties of a membrane were highly dependent on the membrane synthesis parameters. At the beginning of the optimization, their effects towards the outputs of the optimization, which were the water uptake, proton conductivity and ion exchange capacity, and their interactions had been investigated based on RSM. The design of the experiment and responses were tabulated in Table 1. Each factor was varied at 5 levels as showed in Table 2. 97: 98: TABLE 1. [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0010 | section=PROTON CONDUCTIVITY | lines=78-86 | keywords=ElectricalDielectricProperties; anchors=pure SPI; family=SPI, pure SPI 78: # PROTON CONDUCTIVITY 79: 80: Proton conductivities of pure SPI, SPI/RHA and SPI/sRHA composite membranes in full hydration form were measured in the lateral direction by AC impedance method using Multi-channel Potentiostat/Galvanostat (WonATech, Korea). Before carrying out the test, the ascast membranes were soaked in $2\mathrm{M}$ methanol solution for $1\mathrm{h}$ at $60^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in order to remove the DMSO residue. Then, the membranes were immersed in $1.0\mathrm{N}$ hydrochloric acid for $12\mathrm{h}$ in room temperate. This was the proton exchange treatment step. Next, the membranes in proton form were then immersed in deionized water for $2\mathrm{h}$ . Finally, a modified four-probe AC impedance method was used to measure the resistance within each of the composite membranes. The proton conductivity of the membranes was calculated from the following equation: 81: 82: $$ 83: \sigma = \frac {L}{R A} \tag {3} 84: $$ 85: 86: where $\sigma$ was the proton conductivity in $\mathrm{Scm^{-1}}$ ; $L$ was the two electrodes distance; $R$ was the membrane resistance; and $A$ was the membrane cross-sectional area.

## excerpt 1 | block=b0010 | section=PROTON CONDUCTIVITY | lines=78-86 | keywords=ElectricalDielectricProperties
78: # PROTON CONDUCTIVITY
79: 
80: Proton conductivities of pure SPI, SPI/RHA and SPI/sRHA composite membranes in full hydration form were measured in the lateral direction by AC impedance method using Multi-channel Potentiostat/Galvanostat (WonATech, Korea). Before carrying out the test, the ascast membranes were soaked in  $2\mathrm{M}$  methanol solution for  $1\mathrm{h}$  at  $60^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  in order to remove the DMSO residue. Then, the membranes were immersed in  $1.0\mathrm{N}$  hydrochloric acid for  $12\mathrm{h}$  in room temperate. This was the proton exchange treatment step. Next, the membranes in proton form were then immersed in deionized water for  $2\mathrm{h}$ . Finally, a modified four-probe AC impedance method was used to measure the resistance within each of the composite membranes. The proton conductivity of the membranes was calculated from the following equation:
81: 
82: $$
83: \sigma = \frac {L}{R A} \tag {3}
84: $$
85: 
86: where  $\sigma$  was the proton conductivity in  $\mathrm{Scm^{-1}}$ ;  $L$  was the two electrodes distance;  $R$  was the membrane resistance; and  $A$  was the membrane cross-sectional area.

## excerpt 2 | block=b0022 | section=EFFECTS OF FACTORS ON PROTON CONDUCTIVITY | lines=268-285 | keywords=ElectricalDielectricProperties
268: # EFFECTS OF FACTORS ON PROTON CONDUCTIVITY
269: 
270: In Figure 8, both fillers showed a slightly different effect on the membrane proton conductivity. At constant annealing temperature, the proton conductivity of SPI/RHA composite membranes increased from 5 to 12.5 wt.  $\%$  whereas SPI/sRHA composite membranes increased from 7.5 to 15 wt.  $\%$ . Both types of filler experienced a drop in proton conductivity at an additional increase in filler loading. sRHA filler possessed a better distribution
271: 
272: of particles in the SPI polymer matrix compared to RHA filler. RHA tended to agglomerate with each other when the loading of filler exceeded 12.5 wt.  $\%$ . Hence, an additional raise in filler loading led to more aggregations. High aggregation of filler particle caused the reduction of effective proton channels present in the composite membranes produced. sRHA had extra benefit compared to RHA as sRHA contained higher sulfonic acid group concentration.

282: 
283: EFFECTS OF FACTORS ON ION EXCHANGE CAPACITY IEC was defined as the number of the sulfonic acid group per  $1\mathrm{g}$  of the membrane. Figure 9 shows that increased filler loading had led to a drop in IEC performance for both types of fillers. This was mainly due to the loss of sulfonic group in the polymer matrix when filler RHA or sRHA was incorporated into the membranes. Addition of filler into the polymer matrix had replaced out a part of the sulfonic acid groups from the composite membranes produced. Hence, IEC performance of the composite
284: 
285: membranes reduced along with increased filler content. However, when comparing Figure 9(a) and Figure 9(b), it demonstrated that at the same filler loading, sRHA had a higher value of IEC compared to RHA. This was because sRHA had active participation in providing free acid groups to the membrane compared to RHA as sulfonation of rice husk ash added sulfonic acid groups into rice husk ash and hence, adding benefits to sRHA. Hence, composite membranes with sRHA had displayed higher IEC performance compared to that of RHA.

Materials and Methods | lines=75-84 | keywords=RheologicalProperties; family=METI 75: # 2.2. Characterization and Methods 76: 77: The inherent viscosity of METI resins was measured at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ using an Ubbelohde viscometer (As-one, Osaka, Japan), and the absolute viscosity was measured at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ using a DV2-TRVCP viscometer (Brookfield, Middleboro, MA, USA). Molecular weights of METI resins, including number-average molecular weight $(\mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{n}})$ and weight-average molecular weight $(\mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{w}})$ , were determined using an LC-20AD-type gel permeation chromatography (GPC, Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan) system. Solubility tests were characterized as follows: $1.0\mathrm{g}$ of METI resins was mixed with $9.0\mathrm{g}$ of solvent (solid content $10\mathrm{wt}\%$ ) 78: 79: and stirred at room temperature for $24\mathrm{h}$ . Its solubility was visually judged and divided into three grades: completely dissolved $(++)$ , partially dissolved $(+ -)$ , and insoluble $(-)$ , where complete dissolution means that the resin is uniformly dispersed in the solution, transparent, without phase separation, precipitation, or gel formation; insolubilization indicates no change in the appearance of the resin. 80: 81: Attenuated total reflection-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra of PI NFMs were measured by a Bruker Tensor-27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA), wavenumber range: $4000 - 400\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ . Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were measured with a D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Corporation, Tokyo, Japan), Cu-Kα1 radiation, operating at $40\mathrm{kV}$ and $200\mathrm{mA}$ . Field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) imaging was performed using a JSM-6700F (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of $15\mathrm{kV}$ , and Pt/Pd was sputtered on each film before measurement. Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV-Vis) was performed at room temperature using a U-3210 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) for testing, and the PI fibrous membrane was dried at $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $10\mathrm{h}$ before the test to remove the adsorbed moisture. 82: 83: Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of PI NFMs was tested by a TA-Q50 thermal analysis system (Perkin-Elmer, Waltham, MA, USA) at a test temperature range of $30 - 760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a heating rate of $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ ; the test environment was nitrogen and the gas flow rate was $20\mathrm{mL / min}$ . Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was tested with a DSC 214 calorimetric differential scanner (NETZSCH, Selb, Germany); the test temperature range was $30 - 400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the heating rate was $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ , and the test environment was nitrogen with a gas flow of $20\mathrm{mL / min}$ . The rheological evaluation was carried out on an AR2000 rheometer (TA Instrument, Newcastle, DE, USA). METI powder was pressed to obtain a sample disk (diameter: $25\mathrm{mm}$ , thickness: $1.5 \pm 0.2\mathrm{mm}$ ). During the measurement, METI discs were loaded into parallel plates. The top parallel plate oscillated at a fixed angular frequency of $0.5\mathrm{Hz}$ and a fixed strain of $1.0\%$ ; the data acquisition range was $100 - 440^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and the heating rate was $4^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ . The single-lap shear strength (LSS) test was performed on an Instron 5567 tensile machine (Instron, Boston, MA, USA). The elongation speed was $2\mathrm{mm / min}$ . 84: ## excerpt 2 | block=b0003 | section=2. Preparation of METI NFMs 98: 99: Taking METI-20K NFM as an example, the detailed preparation process of METI NFMs is as follows: The completely dried METI-20K resin was dissolved in DMAc solution, and the solid content of the solution was controlled at $33\mathrm{wt}\%$ to obtain a solution with an absolute viscosity of $5000\mathrm{mPa}$ s. The obtained METI-20K solution was uniformly injected into a $5\mathrm{mL}$ syringe using a spinneret with an inner diameter of $0.21\mathrm{mm}$ . The METI-20K solution was extruded from the spinneret with a syringe pump at a rate of $0.2\mathrm{mL/h}$ . The positive voltage between the injector and the collector was $16\mathrm{kV}$ and the negative voltage was $-3\mathrm{kV}$ . The distance between the spinneret and the grounded drum collector (diameter: $10\mathrm{cm}$ ; length: $30\mathrm{cm}$ ) was $15\mathrm{cm}$ . The relative humidity of the electrospinning device was controlled at $30\pm 5\%$ , and the speed of the collector was set at $2\mathrm{rpm}$ . METI-20K fibers were densely wound and deposited on aluminum foil. The resulting METI-20K NFM was vacuum-dried at $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $1\mathrm{h}$ to remove residual solvent. 100: 101: METI-5K and METI-10K NFMs were prepared following the same procedure as above, but starting with either a METI-5K or METI-10K solution with a solid content of $47.5\mathrm{wt}\%$ or $38\mathrm{wt}\%$ (absolute viscosity: $\sim 5000\mathrm{mPa}$ s), respectively, in the electrospinning solution. 102: [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. There are clear differences in the solubility of the METI resins with different molecular weights in the same DMAc solvent. It can be seen that the viscosity of the METI resins gradually decreased with the decreasing molecular weights at the same solid content. In order to afford PI NFMs with fine micromorphologies, the METI solutions with the absolute viscosities of $5000\mathrm{mPa}$ were prepared for the following electrospinning fabrications. According to the figure, different solid contents that were $47.5\mathrm{wt}\%$ for METI-5K, $38.0\mathrm{wt}\%$ for METI-10K, and $33.0\mathrm{wt}\%$ for METI-20K were chosen. 131: ## excerpt 3 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=136-138 | keywords=RheologicalProperties; anchors=METI-5K; family=METI, METI-5K 136: 137: A series of METI NFMs were fabricated according to the electrospinning procedures shown in Figure 3 with the parameters shown in Section 2.4. It can be seen from the SEM images of the METI NFMs, shown in Figure 4, that the NFMs with fine micromorphology were successfully prepared. The flexible and tough fibrous membranes were composed of tangled nanofibers with average diameters $(\mathrm{d_{av}})$ from $644~\mathrm{nm}$ (METI-20K) to $1471~\mathrm{nm}$ (METI-5K). At the same viscosity, with a decrease in the designed molecular weights, the $\mathrm{d_{av}}$ values gradually increased. This might be attributed to the higher solid content of the resin solution with lower molecular weights at the same viscosity, resulting in higher resin content in the spinning solution sprayed from the spinneret per unit of time. Macroscopically, the $\mathrm{d_{av}}$ values became larger. 138: ## excerpt 4 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=179-181 | keywords=ThermalProperties; anchors=METI-5K; family=METI, METI-5K 179: 180: The curing process of the METI NFMs was further investigated by DSC measurements and the results are shown in Figure 7. As shown in Figure 7a, the METI polymers showed a clear two-stage transition process in the first heating scan. In the first stage, the polymers began melting to form endothermic peaks at $230.2^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $244.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and $253.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for METI-5K, METI-10K, and METI-20K samples, respectively. Then, with the increase in temperature, the second stages occurred at $276.1^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $301.9^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and $323.6^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for the three METI NFMs, respectively. Apparently, the second transitions were due to the alkynyl crosslinking reactions. It is worth noticing that current METI polymers exhibited crosslinking temperatures $(T_{\mathrm{c}})$ in the range of $280 - 320^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which is nearly $70^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ lower than that of the conventional phenylethynyl $(C_6H_4 - C\equiv C - C_6H_4)$ crosslinker [36]. This would undoubtedly expand the applications of "low-temperature" curable alkynyl-terminated PIs. In addition, the $T_{\mathrm{c}}$ values of the METI NFMs increased gradually with an increase in the molecular weights of the polymers, that is, with a decrease in the alkynyl contents of the METI NFMs. Meanwhile, the endothermic peak intensities of the METI polymers also gradually weakened from METI-5K to METI-20K. The second DSC run was recorded and is shown in Figure 7b, in which the exothermic peaks of the thermally crosslinking reactions of METI NFMs all disappeared, indicating complete crosslinking in the polymers. Meanwhile, clear glass transitions were detected. The glass transition temperatures $(T_{\mathrm{g}})$ of the three thermally crosslinked METI NFMs were $270.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for METI-5K, $272.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for METI-10K, and $273.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for METI-20K. On one hand, the existence of clear glass transitions in the crosslinked polymers meant that the degrees of the crosslinking reactions for the current METI polymers were relatively low, resulting in some extent of thermoplastic features for the cured METI polymers. On the other hand, the $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ values of the three METI polymers were a bit lower than that of the PI-ref $(T_{\mathrm{g}} = 288.7^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ ; however, the molecular weights of the METI polymers had little effect on the molecular segments' motion at elevated temperatures. 181: ## excerpt 6 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=188-190 | keywords=RheologicalProperties; anchors=METI-5K; family=METI, METI-5K 188: 189: Generally, the ideal bonding process for the adherends requires the adhesive to have a certain melting flowability to achieve good wettability and infiltrability between the adherends. The determination of complex viscosities and $\mathrm{T_c}$ of the adhesives play important roles for achieving good adhesion [37]. Thus, rheological behaviors of the METI NFMs were evaluated. As shown in Figure 8a, all METIs exhibited good melting flowability, which was mainly attributed to the controlled molecular weights and also the decreased intermolecular interactions due to the incorporation of flexible ether bonds and bulky phthalide groups into the polymer molecular chains. When METI-5K, METI-10K, and METI-20K samples reached the minimum complex viscosities $(\eta_{\mathrm{min}})$ , the corresponding temperatures $(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{min}})$ were $268.8^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (1413.81 Pa s), $318.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (11,035.2 Pa s), and $344.1^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (16,187.9 Pa s), respectively. At the same time, the $\eta_{\mathrm{min}}$ values gradually decreased with ta decrease in the molecular weights of the polymers, indicating that the decreased molecular weights were beneficial for the improvement of the melting flowability of the polymers. 190: ## excerpt 7 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=195-197 | keywords=RheologicalProperties; anchors=METI-5K; family=METI, METI-5K 195: 196: The rheological behaviors of the METI NFMs were further compared with the phenylethynyl-terminated (PETI) analogous materials [26] and the results are shown in Figure 8b. On one hand, the $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{min}}$ values of the METIs were apparently lower than those of the PETI counterparts with the same designed molecular weights. For example, the METI-5K exhibited a $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{min}}$ value of $268.8^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which was $89.6^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ lower than that of the PETI-5K sample $(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{min}} = 358.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ . From another point, the temperature required for achieving the same melting viscosity for the METI sample was also significantly lower than that of the 197: ## excerpt 8 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=207-209 | keywords=RheologicalProperties; anchors=METI-5K; family=METI, METI-5K 207: 208: For the METI-5K sample (Figure 9), when the hot-pressing temperature reached about $290^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the two NFM layers were almost completely fused into one uniform film structure; while for the METI-10K and METI-20K samples, the temperatures were $310^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $330^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. The results were consistent with the rheological test results shown in Figure 8. Compared with our previous work [26], the melting temperatures of METI NFMs were greatly decreased, which was mainly attributed to the lower crosslinking temperature of flexible propynyl groups compared with the rigid phenylethynyl end-cappers. The processing temperatures for the final bonding procedures were then set as follows: METI-5K: $290^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; METI-10K: $310^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; and METI-20K: $330^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . 209: ## excerpt 9 | block=b0004 | section=3. Field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) imaging was performed using a JSM-6700F (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of $15\mathrm{kV}$ , and Pt/Pd was sputtered on each film before measurement. Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV-Vis) was performed at room temperature using a U-3210 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) for testing, and the PI fibrous membrane was dried at $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $10\mathrm{h}$ before the test to remove the adsorbed moisture. 82: 83: Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of PI NFMs was tested by a TA-Q50 thermal analysis system (Perkin-Elmer, Waltham, MA, USA) at a test temperature range of $30 - 760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a heating rate of $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ ; the test environment was nitrogen and the gas flow rate was $20\mathrm{mL / min}$ . Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was tested with a DSC 214 calorimetric differential scanner (NETZSCH, Selb, Germany); the test temperature range was $30 - 400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the heating rate was $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ , and the test environment was nitrogen with a gas flow of $20\mathrm{mL / min}$ . The rheological evaluation was carried out on an AR2000 rheometer (TA Instrument, Newcastle, DE, USA). METI powder was pressed to obtain a sample disk (diameter: $25\mathrm{mm}$ , thickness: $1.5 \pm 0.2\mathrm{mm}$ ). During the measurement, METI discs were loaded into parallel plates. The top parallel plate oscillated at a fixed angular frequency of $0.5\mathrm{Hz}$ and a fixed strain of $1.0\%$ ; the data acquisition range was $100 - 440^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and the heating rate was $4^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ . The single-lap shear strength (LSS) test was performed on an Instron 5567 tensile machine (Instron, Boston, MA, USA). The elongation speed was $2\mathrm{mm / min}$ . 84: ## excerpt 2 | block=b0003 | section=2.
