129: ## excerpt 2 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=130-150 | keywords=MechanicalProperties, ProcessingProperties, RheologicalProperties, ThermalProperties 130: # 3.2. Thermal Foaming Processability 131: 132: The thermal foaming process of NPO-2 was monitored using a hot stage microscope with a heating rate of $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ . The temperature was increased to $320^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the corresponding morphologies of the NPO powder at different temperatures are shown in Figure 4. The morphology of the NPO powder remained unchanged at temperatures below $280^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and some volume shrinkage was observed at $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . At $320^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the NPO powder was completely melted and bubbles began forming. The foam bubbles gradually expanded from 0.5 to $10\mathrm{min}$ , implying that there was adequate strength maintaining the bubbles' intactness. The strength of the melt could be attributed to the crosslinking reaction of NPO during the foaming process [27]. In the foaming process, two types of foaming agents are produced: the byproducts of the imidization reaction, such as $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{OH}$ , and the cyclopentadiene yielded by the thermal decomposition of the NA end-capping groups [35,36]. 133: 134: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 135: Figure 4. Foaming process of NPO-2 with increasing temperature. 136: 137: Figure 5 compares the effect of BIA content on the NPO complex melt viscosities at $150 - 400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The NPO powders began melting at temperatures higher than $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; the melt viscosities decreased with the increase in temperature. After the minimum value, the melt viscosities were markedly increased due to the thermal crosslinking of the end-capping groups with a further increase in temperature, and the minimum melt viscosity was observed at $300 - 330^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Table 2 summarizes the minimum melt viscosity and the corresponding temperatures of the NPO powders with different BIA contents. Obviously, the minimum melt viscosity increased with the increase in BIA content in the polymer backbones. For instance, NPO-0 showed a minimum melt viscosity of $233\mathrm{Pa}\cdot \mathrm{s}$ at $317.3^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , lower than that of NPO-1 (826 Pa·s at $324.1^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ), NPO-2 (5134 Pa·s at $328.9^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ), and NPO-3 (45,490 Pa·s at $304.6^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ). 138: 139: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 140: Figure 5. Rheological behaviors of melting for NPOs with different BIA contents. 141: 142: Table 2. Minimum melt viscosity of powders NPO with different BIA contents and the corresponding temperature. 143: 144:
NPOMinimum Melt Viscosity (Pa·s)Temperature (°C)n(BIA): n(BIA+PDA)
NPO-0233317.30%
NPO-1826324.110%
NPO-25134328.920%
NPO-345,490304.630%
145: 146: Closed-cell rigid PI foams—with different molar ratios of BIA/(BIA + PDA) (0% to 30%)—were successfully prepared by thermal foaming of the NPOs. However, when the molar ratio of BIA/(BIA + PDA) exceeded 40%, the NPOs could not be thermally foamed to yield PI foams—attributed to the lack of melt fluidity. Figure 6 compares the micro morphologies of the PI foams. The distribution of the cell sizes of the PI foams ranged from 147: 148: 50 to $600\mu \mathrm{m}$ , and most of the cell walls were intact without any broken holes or defects. The cell sizes and uniformity were significantly affected by the BIA content. The average cell diameter decreased with an increase in BIA content, along with the cell size uniformity. Obviously, PIF-3 showed an uneven cell structure. 149: 150: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ## excerpt 3 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=208-217 | keywords=MechanicalProperties, PhysicalProperties, ThermalProperties; family=PIF, PIF-2 208: # 3.4. Mechanical Properties of the Rigid PI Foams 209: 210: Table 4 summarizes the mechanical properties of the PI foams. PIF-2 possessed the highest strengths, including a compression strength of $1.1\mathrm{MPa}$ and tensile strength of $0.9\mathrm{MPa}$ , and the highest elongation at breakage of $6.6\%$ . The elongation at breakage increased from $3.0\%$ for PIF-0 to $5.4\%$ for PIF-1, and to $6.4\%$ for PIF-3 and $6.6\%$ (the highest) for PIF-2, implying that increasing the BIA content enhances the toughness of the foams. PIF-3 showed an obvious deterioration in mechanical properties when the BIA content was increased to $30\%$ , accompanied by an irregular cell structure and lower closed-cell rate. 211: 212: Table 4. Mechanical properties of PI foams with different BIA content and different calcd ${\mathrm{M}}_{\mathrm{w}}$ . 213: 214:
PIFsCompressive PropertiesTensile Properties
Strength (MPa)Modulus (MPa)Strength (MPa)Modulus (MPa)Elongation at Breakage (%)
PIF-01.031.00.621.53.0
PIF-11.035.70.615.35.4
PIF-21.124.70.917.06.6
PIF-30.69.80.611.26.4
PIF-2-10001.327.80.626.12.7
PIF-2-12501.128.21.028.64.0
PIF-2-15001.124.70.917.06.6
PIF-2-17501.435.90.615.74.8
PIF-2-20001.022.50.611.96.5
215: 216: The PI foam mechanical properties were also influenced by $\mathrm{M_w}$ (Table 4). All PI foams showed a preferable compressive strength of $\geq 1.0 \, \mathrm{MPa}$ . Increasing the $\mathrm{M_w}$ can lead to a decrease in tensile strength and enhanced toughness. This might be attributed to the non-uniformity of the cell structure and the decrease in crosslinking density, respectively. Overall, the mechanical properties of rigid PI foams were simultaneously affected by the chemical structure and cell structure of the foams. Generally, toughness can be improved by increasing the BIA content and the $\mathrm{M_w}$ of the precursors. An intact and uniform cell structure is essential to ensure the preferred mechanical properties of foam materials. 217:

chain_input.text:
[POLYMER_ANCHOR] 身份标识: doi:10.3390/polym12030510_BPDA-PDA PI fiber heated to 400℃ at 1℃/min_01 名称: BPDA-PDA PI fiber heated to 400℃ at 1℃/min 别名: 聚合物分类编码: PI 聚合物分类名称: aromatic polyimide fiber 样本形态: Fiber [RESULT_PROPERTY_HINTS] MechanicalProperties [PROPERTY_METHODS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0003 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=64-69 | keywords=MechanicalProperties; family=PI 64: # 2.4. Characterization 65: 66: The amidization process of the PAA fibers was characterized on the basis of the curve trends detected by TGA. The amidization degrees of fiber samples was calculated from the FTIR spectra obtained through a FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Vertex 70, Billerica, MA, USA) in an attenuated total reflection mode. The bands at 1552 and $1380~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ , which respectively represent the absorption of benzene ring and imide N-C stretching of PI, were chosen to estimate the amidization degrees of the fibers. DMA (SDTA861e, Mettler Toledo, Schwerzenbach, Zürich, Switzerland) was carried out on the PAA fibers in tension mode at a frequency of $1\mathrm{Hz}$ . A series of heating rates (1, 2, 3, 3.5, 4, 5, 8, 10, 16 and $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ ) were adopted for DMA characterization. The densities of the PI fibers were measured by a buoyancy method [20]. The XRD (SmartLab, Rigaku, Akishima-shi, Tokyo, Japan) patterns were obtained along and perpendicular to the fiber axis. The PI fibers were twisted on the particular holder designed by our laboratory, enabling the fiber to be characterized quantitatively. The area and location of certain diffraction peaks were studied and analyzed with the Origin 8.0 (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA) and PeakFit 4.0 software (Systat Software, San Jose, CA, USA). The 2D wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WAXD) patterns of the PI fiber obtained from the Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility are shown in Figure 1. The most isolated (004) streaks on the 2D WAXD patterns were determined, and Hermans' orientation factor $(f_{\mathrm{c}})$ was calculated to characterize the preferred orientation of crystals in the fiber [20]. The tensile properties of all of the PI fibers were characterized by an extensometer (XQ-1C, New Fiber Instrument, Shanghai, China) with a cross-head speed of $5\mathrm{mm / min}$ . Each sample was tested 30 times, and the average value was adopted as the final result. 67: 68: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 69: Figure 1. 2D WAXD patterns of PI fiber (fiber treated at $40^{\circ}C / \mathrm{min}$ from room temperature to $400^{\circ}C$ as an example). The steaks of (004) and (0010) and the streaks of (110), (200) and (210) are on the meridian and equatorial directions of the PI fiber, respectively. [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results | lines=152-160 | keywords=MechanicalProperties; family=PI 152: # 3.6. Isothermal Annealing after Treatment 153: 154: Although increasing the heating rate can effectively promote the crystallinity of PI fibers and remarkably enhance their respective tensile moduli, the tensile strength does not increase, and instead slightly decreases due to the high residual stress under high heating rates. A thermal annealing process is necessary to reduce the residual stress. Here, the PI fiber obtained at $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{min} - 400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is annealed at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under different times. The annealed fiber is characterized by XRD and tensile testing for further study. 155: 156: Table 2 lists the diffraction angles, full-width-at-half-maximum values, axial d-spacings and crystal sizes of the annealed PI fibers. As annealing time increases from 0 to $25\mathrm{~s}$ , the axial d-spacing and crystal size both remarkably decrease from 31.87 and $181.08\AA$ to 31.73 and $154.80\AA$ . This result proves that when the PI fiber is annealed, the crystal lattice becomes highly compact, indicating that the residual stress in the fiber has been greatly reduced. Moreover, it should be noticed that the above process is only carried out in less than $25\mathrm{~s}$ , which is a rather short time. 157: 158: Table 2. D-spacing and crystal size calculated using the (004) diffraction for PI fibers annealed at ${400}^{ \circ }\mathrm{C}$ under different times. 159: 160:
Annealing Time (s)2-Theta (Degrees)FWHM (Degrees)d-spacing (Angstrom)Crystal Size (Angstrom)
011.110.4431.87181.08
511.120.4431.84179.42
1011.140.4731.79167.97
2511.160.5131.73154.80
6011.160.5231.73151.82
12011.160.5131.73154.80
30011.160.5231.73151.82

The PI fibers were twisted on the particular holder designed by our laboratory, enabling the fiber to be characterized quantitatively. The area and location of certain diffraction peaks were studied and analyzed with the Origin 8.0 (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA) and PeakFit 4.0 software (Systat Software, San Jose, CA, USA). The 2D wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WAXD) patterns of the PI fiber obtained from the Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility are shown in Figure 1. The most isolated (004) streaks on the 2D WAXD patterns were determined, and Hermans' orientation factor $(f_{\mathrm{c}})$ was calculated to characterize the preferred orientation of crystals in the fiber [20]. The tensile properties of all of the PI fibers were characterized by an extensometer (XQ-1C, New Fiber Instrument, Shanghai, China) with a cross-head speed of $5\mathrm{mm / min}$ . Each sample was tested 30 times, and the average value was adopted as the final result. 67: 68: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 69: Figure 1. 2D WAXD patterns of PI fiber (fiber treated at $40^{\circ}C / \mathrm{min}$ from room temperature to $400^{\circ}C$ as an example). The steaks of (004) and (0010) and the streaks of (110), (200) and (210) are on the meridian and equatorial directions of the PI fiber, respectively. [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results | lines=152-160 | keywords=MechanicalProperties; family=PI 152: # 3.6. Isothermal Annealing after Treatment 153: 154: Although increasing the heating rate can effectively promote the crystallinity of PI fibers and remarkably enhance their respective tensile moduli, the tensile strength does not increase, and instead slightly decreases due to the high residual stress under high heating rates. A thermal annealing process is necessary to reduce the residual stress. Here, the PI fiber obtained at $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{min} - 400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is annealed at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under different times. The annealed fiber is characterized by XRD and tensile testing for further study. 155: 156: Table 2 lists the diffraction angles, full-width-at-half-maximum values, axial d-spacings and crystal sizes of the annealed PI fibers. As annealing time increases from 0 to $25\mathrm{~s}$ , the axial d-spacing and crystal size both remarkably decrease from 31.87 and $181.08\AA$ to 31.73 and $154.80\AA$ . This result proves that when the PI fiber is annealed, the crystal lattice becomes highly compact, indicating that the residual stress in the fiber has been greatly reduced. Moreover, it should be noticed that the above process is only carried out in less than $25\mathrm{~s}$ , which is a rather short time. 157: 158: Table 2. This result proves that when the PI fiber is annealed, the crystal lattice becomes highly compact, indicating that the residual stress in the fiber has been greatly reduced. Moreover, it should be noticed that the above process is only carried out in less than $25\mathrm{~s}$ , which is a rather short time. 157: 158: Table 2. chain_input.text:
[POLYMER_ANCHOR] 身份标识: doi:10.3390/polym12030510_BPDA-PDA PI fiber heated to 350℃ at 2℃/min_01 名称: BPDA-PDA PI fiber heated to 350℃ at 2℃/min 别名: 聚合物分类编码: PI 聚合物分类名称: aromatic polyimide fiber 样本形态: Fiber [RESULT_PROPERTY_HINTS] MechanicalProperties [PROPERTY_METHODS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0003 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=64-69 | keywords=MechanicalProperties; family=PI 64: # 2.4. Characterization 65: 66: The amidization process of the PAA fibers was characterized on the basis of the curve trends detected by TGA. The amidization degrees of fiber samples was calculated from the FTIR spectra obtained through a FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Vertex 70, Billerica, MA, USA) in an attenuated total reflection mode. The bands at 1552 and $1380~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ , which respectively represent the absorption of benzene ring and imide N-C stretching of PI, were chosen to estimate the amidization degrees of the fibers. DMA (SDTA861e, Mettler Toledo, Schwerzenbach, Zürich, Switzerland) was carried out on the PAA fibers in tension mode at a frequency of $1\mathrm{Hz}$ . A series of heating rates (1, 2, 3, 3.5, 4, 5, 8, 10, 16 and $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ ) were adopted for DMA characterization. The densities of the PI fibers were measured by a buoyancy method [20]. The XRD (SmartLab, Rigaku, Akishima-shi, Tokyo, Japan) patterns were obtained along and perpendicular to the fiber axis. The PI fibers were twisted on the particular holder designed by our laboratory, enabling the fiber to be characterized quantitatively. The area and location of certain diffraction peaks were studied and analyzed with the Origin 8.0 (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA) and PeakFit 4.0 software (Systat Software, San Jose, CA, USA). The 2D wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WAXD) patterns of the PI fiber obtained from the Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility are shown in Figure 1. The most isolated (004) streaks on the 2D WAXD patterns were determined, and Hermans' orientation factor $(f_{\mathrm{c}})$ was calculated to characterize the preferred orientation of crystals in the fiber [20]. The tensile properties of all of the PI fibers were characterized by an extensometer (XQ-1C, New Fiber Instrument, Shanghai, China) with a cross-head speed of $5\mathrm{mm / min}$ . Each sample was tested 30 times, and the average value was adopted as the final result. 67: 68: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 69: Figure 1. 2D WAXD patterns of PI fiber (fiber treated at $40^{\circ}C / \mathrm{min}$ from room temperature to $400^{\circ}C$ as an example). The steaks of (004) and (0010) and the streaks of (110), (200) and (210) are on the meridian and equatorial directions of the PI fiber, respectively. [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results | lines=152-160 | keywords=MechanicalProperties; family=PI 152: # 3.6. Isothermal Annealing after Treatment 153: 154: Although increasing the heating rate can effectively promote the crystallinity of PI fibers and remarkably enhance their respective tensile moduli, the tensile strength does not increase, and instead slightly decreases due to the high residual stress under high heating rates. A thermal annealing process is necessary to reduce the residual stress. Here, the PI fiber obtained at $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{min} - 400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is annealed at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under different times. The annealed fiber is characterized by XRD and tensile testing for further study. 155: 156: Table 2 lists the diffraction angles, full-width-at-half-maximum values, axial d-spacings and crystal sizes of the annealed PI fibers. As annealing time increases from 0 to $25\mathrm{~s}$ , the axial d-spacing and crystal size both remarkably decrease from 31.87 and $181.08\AA$ to 31.73 and $154.80\AA$ . This result proves that when the PI fiber is annealed, the crystal lattice becomes highly compact, indicating that the residual stress in the fiber has been greatly reduced. Moreover, it should be noticed that the above process is only carried out in less than $25\mathrm{~s}$ , which is a rather short time. 157: 158: Table 2. D-spacing and crystal size calculated using the (004) diffraction for PI fibers annealed at ${400}^{ \circ }\mathrm{C}$ under different times. 159: 160:

The amidization degrees of fiber samples was calculated from the FTIR spectra obtained through a FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Vertex 70, Billerica, MA, USA) in an attenuated total reflection mode. The bands at 1552 and $1380~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ , which respectively represent the absorption of benzene ring and imide N-C stretching of PI, were chosen to estimate the amidization degrees of the fibers. DMA (SDTA861e, Mettler Toledo, Schwerzenbach, Zürich, Switzerland) was carried out on the PAA fibers in tension mode at a frequency of $1\mathrm{Hz}$ . A series of heating rates (1, 2, 3, 3.5, 4, 5, 8, 10, 16 and $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ ) were adopted for DMA characterization. The densities of the PI fibers were measured by a buoyancy method [20]. The XRD (SmartLab, Rigaku, Akishima-shi, Tokyo, Japan) patterns were obtained along and perpendicular to the fiber axis. The PI fibers were twisted on the particular holder designed by our laboratory, enabling the fiber to be characterized quantitatively. The area and location of certain diffraction peaks were studied and analyzed with the Origin 8.0 (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA) and PeakFit 4.0 software (Systat Software, San Jose, CA, USA). The 2D wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WAXD) patterns of the PI fiber obtained from the Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility are shown in Figure 1. The most isolated (004) streaks on the 2D WAXD patterns were determined, and Hermans' orientation factor $(f_{\mathrm{c}})$ was calculated to characterize the preferred orientation of crystals in the fiber [20]. The tensile properties of all of the PI fibers were characterized by an extensometer (XQ-1C, New Fiber Instrument, Shanghai, China) with a cross-head speed of $5\mathrm{mm / min}$ . Each sample was tested 30 times, and the average value was adopted as the final result. 67: 68: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 69: Figure 1. 2D WAXD patterns of PI fiber (fiber treated at $40^{\circ}C / \mathrm{min}$ from room temperature to $400^{\circ}C$ as an example). The steaks of (004) and (0010) and the streaks of (110), (200) and (210) are on the meridian and equatorial directions of the PI fiber, respectively. [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results | lines=152-160 | keywords=MechanicalProperties; family=PI 152: # 3.6. Isothermal Annealing after Treatment 153: 154: Although increasing the heating rate can effectively promote the crystallinity of PI fibers and remarkably enhance their respective tensile moduli, the tensile strength does not increase, and instead slightly decreases due to the high residual stress under high heating rates. A thermal annealing process is necessary to reduce the residual stress. Here, the PI fiber obtained at $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{min} - 400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is annealed at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under different times. The annealed fiber is characterized by XRD and tensile testing for further study. 155: 156: Table 2 lists the diffraction angles, full-width-at-half-maximum values, axial d-spacings and crystal sizes of the annealed PI fibers. As annealing time increases from 0 to $25\mathrm{~s}$ , the axial d-spacing and crystal size both remarkably decrease from 31.87 and $181.08\AA$ to 31.73 and $154.80\AA$ . This result proves that when the PI fiber is annealed, the crystal lattice becomes highly compact, indicating that the residual stress in the fiber has been greatly reduced. Moreover, it should be noticed that the above process is only carried out in less than $25\mathrm{~s}$ , which is a rather short time. 157: 158: Table 2. D-spacing and crystal size calculated using the (004) diffraction for PI fibers annealed at ${400}^{ \circ }\mathrm{C}$ under different times. 159: 160:

Isothermal Annealing after Treatment 153: 154: Although increasing the heating rate can effectively promote the crystallinity of PI fibers and remarkably enhance their respective tensile moduli, the tensile strength does not increase, and instead slightly decreases due to the high residual stress under high heating rates. A thermal annealing process is necessary to reduce the residual stress. Here, the PI fiber obtained at $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{min} - 400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is annealed at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under different times. The annealed fiber is characterized by XRD and tensile testing for further study. 155: 156: Table 2 lists the diffraction angles, full-width-at-half-maximum values, axial d-spacings and crystal sizes of the annealed PI fibers. As annealing time increases from 0 to $25\mathrm{~s}$ , the axial d-spacing and crystal size both remarkably decrease from 31.87 and $181.08\AA$ to 31.73 and $154.80\AA$ . This result proves that when the PI fiber is annealed, the crystal lattice becomes highly compact, indicating that the residual stress in the fiber has been greatly reduced. Moreover, it should be noticed that the above process is only carried out in less than $25\mathrm{~s}$ , which is a rather short time. 157: 158: Table 2. chain_input.text:
[POLYMER_ANCHOR] 身份标识: doi:10.3390/polym12030510_BPDA-PDA PI fiber heated to 300℃ at 40℃/min_01 名称: BPDA-PDA PI fiber heated to 300℃ at 40℃/min 别名: 聚合物分类编码: PI 聚合物分类名称: aromatic polyimide fiber 样本形态: Fiber [RESULT_PROPERTY_HINTS] MechanicalProperties [PROPERTY_METHODS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0003 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=64-69 | keywords=MechanicalProperties; family=PI 64: # 2.4. Characterization 65: 66: The amidization process of the PAA fibers was characterized on the basis of the curve trends detected by TGA. The amidization degrees of fiber samples was calculated from the FTIR spectra obtained through a FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Vertex 70, Billerica, MA, USA) in an attenuated total reflection mode. The bands at 1552 and $1380~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ , which respectively represent the absorption of benzene ring and imide N-C stretching of PI, were chosen to estimate the amidization degrees of the fibers. DMA (SDTA861e, Mettler Toledo, Schwerzenbach, Zürich, Switzerland) was carried out on the PAA fibers in tension mode at a frequency of $1\mathrm{Hz}$ . A series of heating rates (1, 2, 3, 3.5, 4, 5, 8, 10, 16 and $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ ) were adopted for DMA characterization. The densities of the PI fibers were measured by a buoyancy method [20]. The XRD (SmartLab, Rigaku, Akishima-shi, Tokyo, Japan) patterns were obtained along and perpendicular to the fiber axis. The PI fibers were twisted on the particular holder designed by our laboratory, enabling the fiber to be characterized quantitatively. The area and location of certain diffraction peaks were studied and analyzed with the Origin 8.0 (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA) and PeakFit 4.0 software (Systat Software, San Jose, CA, USA). The 2D wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WAXD) patterns of the PI fiber obtained from the Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility are shown in Figure 1. The most isolated (004) streaks on the 2D WAXD patterns were determined, and Hermans' orientation factor $(f_{\mathrm{c}})$ was calculated to characterize the preferred orientation of crystals in the fiber [20]. The tensile properties of all of the PI fibers were characterized by an extensometer (XQ-1C, New Fiber Instrument, Shanghai, China) with a cross-head speed of $5\mathrm{mm / min}$ . Each sample was tested 30 times, and the average value was adopted as the final result. 67: 68: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 69: Figure 1. 2D WAXD patterns of PI fiber (fiber treated at $40^{\circ}C / \mathrm{min}$ from room temperature to $400^{\circ}C$ as an example). The steaks of (004) and (0010) and the streaks of (110), (200) and (210) are on the meridian and equatorial directions of the PI fiber, respectively. As annealing time increases from 0 to $25\mathrm{~s}$ , the axial d-spacing and crystal size both remarkably decrease from 31.87 and $181.08\AA$ to 31.73 and $154.80\AA$ . This result proves that when the PI fiber is annealed, the crystal lattice becomes highly compact, indicating that the residual stress in the fiber has been greatly reduced. Moreover, it should be noticed that the above process is only carried out in less than $25\mathrm{~s}$ , which is a rather short time. 157: 158: Table 2. chain_input.text:
[POLYMER_ANCHOR] 身份标识: doi:10.3390/polym12030510_Annealed BPDA-PDA PI fiber (80℃/min to 400℃, 60s annealing at 400℃)_01 名称: Annealed BPDA-PDA PI fiber (80℃/min to 400℃, 60s annealing at 400℃) 别名: 聚合物分类编码: PI 聚合物分类名称: aromatic polyimide fiber 样本形态: Fiber [RESULT_PROPERTY_HINTS] MechanicalProperties [PROPERTY_METHODS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0003 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=64-69 | keywords=MechanicalProperties; family=PI 64: # 2.4. Characterization 65: 66: The amidization process of the PAA fibers was characterized on the basis of the curve trends detected by TGA. The amidization degrees of fiber samples was calculated from the FTIR spectra obtained through a FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Vertex 70, Billerica, MA, USA) in an attenuated total reflection mode. The bands at 1552 and $1380~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ , which respectively represent the absorption of benzene ring and imide N-C stretching of PI, were chosen to estimate the amidization degrees of the fibers. DMA (SDTA861e, Mettler Toledo, Schwerzenbach, Zürich, Switzerland) was carried out on the PAA fibers in tension mode at a frequency of $1\mathrm{Hz}$ . A series of heating rates (1, 2, 3, 3.5, 4, 5, 8, 10, 16 and $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ ) were adopted for DMA characterization. The densities of the PI fibers were measured by a buoyancy method [20]. The XRD (SmartLab, Rigaku, Akishima-shi, Tokyo, Japan) patterns were obtained along and perpendicular to the fiber axis. The PI fibers were twisted on the particular holder designed by our laboratory, enabling the fiber to be characterized quantitatively. The area and location of certain diffraction peaks were studied and analyzed with the Origin 8.0 (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA) and PeakFit 4.0 software (Systat Software, San Jose, CA, USA). The 2D wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WAXD) patterns of the PI fiber obtained from the Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility are shown in Figure 1. The most isolated (004) streaks on the 2D WAXD patterns were determined, and Hermans' orientation factor $(f_{\mathrm{c}})$ was calculated to characterize the preferred orientation of crystals in the fiber [20]. The tensile properties of all of the PI fibers were characterized by an extensometer (XQ-1C, New Fiber Instrument, Shanghai, China) with a cross-head speed of $5\mathrm{mm / min}$ . Each sample was tested 30 times, and the average value was adopted as the final result. 67: 68: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 69: Figure 1. 2D WAXD patterns of PI fiber (fiber treated at $40^{\circ}C / \mathrm{min}$ from room temperature to $400^{\circ}C$ as an example). The steaks of (004) and (0010) and the streaks of (110), (200) and (210) are on the meridian and equatorial directions of the PI fiber, respectively. [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results | lines=152-160 | keywords=MechanicalProperties; family=PI 152: # 3.6. Isothermal Annealing after Treatment 153: 154: Although increasing the heating rate can effectively promote the crystallinity of PI fibers and remarkably enhance their respective tensile moduli, the tensile strength does not increase, and instead slightly decreases due to the high residual stress under high heating rates. A thermal annealing process is necessary to reduce the residual stress. Here, the PI fiber obtained at $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{min} - 400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is annealed at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under different times. The annealed fiber is characterized by XRD and tensile testing for further study. 155: 156: Table 2 lists the diffraction angles, full-width-at-half-maximum values, axial d-spacings and crystal sizes of the annealed PI fibers. As annealing time increases from 0 to $25\mathrm{~s}$ , the axial d-spacing and crystal size both remarkably decrease from 31.87 and $181.08\AA$ to 31.73 and $154.80\AA$ . This result proves that when the PI fiber is annealed, the crystal lattice becomes highly compact, indicating that the residual stress in the fiber has been greatly reduced. Moreover, it should be noticed that the above process is only carried out in less than $25\mathrm{~s}$ , which is a rather short time. 157: 158: Table 2. ## excerpt 1 | block=b0003 | section=Experimental | lines=45-47 | keywords=RheologicalProperties
45: # Instruments
46: 
47: NMR spectra were obtained on a Bruker 300 MHz spectrophotometer. The FT-IR (KBr) spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer RXI spectrophotometer  $(2\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w}\%)$  in KBr, resolution  $4\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ , scan no. 6). Thermal analysis (TGA-DTA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) were carried out on a Linseis STA PT 1000 and a DSC PL instruments (nitrogen atmosphere, scan rate  $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ ), respectively. DSC measurements were performed after heating the polymer samples to  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and then cooling to room temperature; this removed any adsorbed water on the polymer. Elemental analyses of the samples were performed on an Elemental Vario TEL instrument. Inherent viscosities were measured by an Ostwald viscometer at  $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  in DMF. The mass spectrum (electron impact,  $70\mathrm{eV}$ ) was obtained on an Agilent Technologies 5973Network MSD mass spectrometer. Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WXRD) measurements were performed at room temperature on a Simence D500 X-ray diffractometer (Germany) using Ni-filtered  $\mathrm{Cu - K}_{\alpha}$  radiation. The scanning rate was  $1^{\circ} / \mathrm{min}$  over a range of  $2\theta = 2 - 10^{\circ}$ . Scanning Electron Microphotographs (SEM) were obtained on a LEO 1430VP instrument.

[POLYMER_ANCHOR]
身份标识: doi:10.3390/polym13223903_neat 6FDA-ODA polyamic acid film_01
名称: neat 6FDA-ODA polyamic acid film
别名: 
聚合物分类编码: PAA
聚合物分类名称: polyamic acid
样本形态: Film

[RESULT_PROPERTY_HINTS]
ElectricalDielectricProperties

[PROPERTY_METHODS]
## excerpt 1 | block=b0003 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=70-73 | keywords=ElectricalDielectricProperties; family=PAA
70: # 2.3. Instrumentation
71: 
72: Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) with a Mettler STARE was used to determine the decomposition temperatures  $(\mathrm{T_d}s)$  of the PAA and PIs. The  $\mathrm{T_d}$  of the polymer was defined as the temperature at which a  $5\%$  weight loss occurred. UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded using an Agilent 8453 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analysis was conducted using a Bio-Rad FTS-60A infrared spectrometer in attenuated total reflection mode. A reflection cell with an incidence angle of  $60^{\circ}$  was used. An average of 128 scans were collected for each film sample over the range of  $400 - 4000~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ . The resolution was  $4\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ . The dielectric constants of the PI films were measured, using an Agilent LCR meter (ASTM D150), at room temperature while changing the frequency from  $10\mathrm{Hz}$  to  $10^{6}\mathrm{Hz}$  using the scanning mode. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was conducted in the contact mode with silicon tips using an AFM 5100N from the Hitachi High-Tech Sci. Co. Tensile tests of the PI films were performed at a tensile speed of  $10\mathrm{mm}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$  on dog-bone specimens  $(50\mathrm{mm} \times 5\mathrm{mm} \times 40\mu \mathrm{m})$  using a Shimadzu AGS-100G (Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan). DMA analysis of the thick PI films was performed using a DMA Q800 (TA Co., Detroit, MI, USA)at  $1.0\mathrm{Hz}$  and a heating rate of  $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\cdot \mathrm{min}^{-1}$  from room temperature to  $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
73:

[PROPERTY_RESULTS]
## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=144-147 | keywords=ElectricalDielectricProperties; family=PAA
144: # 3.5. Dielectric Properties of Neat PI and Crosslinked PI Free-Standing Thick Films
145: 
146: Next, the capacitance values of the 6FDA-ODA PI and crosslinked PI free-standing films were measured in order to compare their dielectric properties. It is generally known that the dielectric properties of a polymer such as 6FDA-ODA PI change when the polymer repeating unit contains fluorine, which is attributed to a relatively low polarizability and large free volume [23,24]. In general, when a PI film is used as an LC AL, the dielectric constant of PI affects the degree of accumulation of static charges formed on the surface after the rubbing process and also affects the time the residual charges remain on the surface. It is known that the low charge accumulation of a PI film made of PAA can minimize the image sticking problem in LCDs. The effect of crosslinking on the dielectric properties is clearly observed in Figure S9. When the dielectric constant and dielectric loss factor were specifically analyzed according to the frequency of the PI film sample, it was observed that the crosslinked PI film had a lower dielectric constant ( $\varepsilon = 3.69$  at  $10\mathrm{kHz}$ ) than the neat PI film ( $\varepsilon = 4.02$  at  $10\mathrm{kHz}$ ). In addition, the crosslinked PI film exhibited very stable dielectric properties at this frequency compared to the neat PI film. This phenomenon is due to the synergistic effect of the large free volume and loosening of the molecular packing due to the formation of partial crosslinking bridges between the PI chains. [PROPERTY_METHODS]
## excerpt 1 | block=b0003 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=70-73 | keywords=ElectricalDielectricProperties; family=PAA
70: # 2.3. Instrumentation
71: 
72: Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) with a Mettler STARE was used to determine the decomposition temperatures  $(\mathrm{T_d}s)$  of the PAA and PIs. The  $\mathrm{T_d}$  of the polymer was defined as the temperature at which a  $5\%$  weight loss occurred. UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded using an Agilent 8453 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analysis was conducted using a Bio-Rad FTS-60A infrared spectrometer in attenuated total reflection mode. A reflection cell with an incidence angle of  $60^{\circ}$  was used. An average of 128 scans were collected for each film sample over the range of  $400 - 4000~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ . The resolution was  $4\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ . The dielectric constants of the PI films were measured, using an Agilent LCR meter (ASTM D150), at room temperature while changing the frequency from  $10\mathrm{Hz}$  to  $10^{6}\mathrm{Hz}$  using the scanning mode. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was conducted in the contact mode with silicon tips using an AFM 5100N from the Hitachi High-Tech Sci. Co. Tensile tests of the PI films were performed at a tensile speed of  $10\mathrm{mm}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$  on dog-bone specimens  $(50\mathrm{mm} \times 5\mathrm{mm} \times 40\mu \mathrm{m})$  using a Shimadzu AGS-100G (Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan). DMA analysis of the thick PI films was performed using a DMA Q800 (TA Co., Detroit, MI, USA)at  $1.0\mathrm{Hz}$  and a heating rate of  $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\cdot \mathrm{min}^{-1}$  from room temperature to  $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
73:

[PROPERTY_RESULTS]
## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=144-147 | keywords=ElectricalDielectricProperties; family=PAA
144: # 3.5. Dielectric Properties of Neat PI and Crosslinked PI Free-Standing Thick Films
145: 
146: Next, the capacitance values of the 6FDA-ODA PI and crosslinked PI free-standing films were measured in order to compare their dielectric properties. It is generally known that the dielectric properties of a polymer such as 6FDA-ODA PI change when the polymer repeating unit contains fluorine, which is attributed to a relatively low polarizability and large free volume [23,24]. In general, when a PI film is used as an LC AL, the dielectric constant of PI affects the degree of accumulation of static charges formed on the surface after the rubbing process and also affects the time the residual charges remain on the surface. It is known that the low charge accumulation of a PI film made of PAA can minimize the image sticking problem in LCDs. The effect of crosslinking on the dielectric properties is clearly observed in Figure S9. When the dielectric constant and dielectric loss factor were specifically analyzed according to the frequency of the PI film sample, it was observed that the crosslinked PI film had a lower dielectric constant ( $\varepsilon = 3.69$  at  $10\mathrm{kHz}$ ) than the neat PI film ( $\varepsilon = 4.02$  at  $10\mathrm{kHz}$ ). In addition, the crosslinked PI film exhibited very stable dielectric properties at this frequency compared to the neat PI film. This phenomenon is due to the synergistic effect of the large free volume and loosening of the molecular packing due to the formation of partial crosslinking bridges between the PI chains. Finally, it can be inferred that the formation of a cross-linked structure between the PI chains can change the internal structure of the film, leading to a change in the dielectric properties of the film.

Synthesis of BPDA-PDA PAA solution and fabrication of PI film. 69: 70: For preparing PI/silica and PI/GPMS-modified silica composites, the $30\mathrm{wt}\%$ of pre-prepared silica sol (TSi) was added drop by drop into PAA solution as shown in Figure 2 to avoid local inhomogeneities under nitrogen flow. Then composite PI-TSi/PI-TSi-GPMS containing BPDA-PDA PAA solution films was cast onto the glass plate and the films were imidized under the same heating protocol. Finally, after curing at different temperatures, free-standing composite films were successfully obtained. Sample codes, physical appearance, optical transmittance, and $5\mathrm{wt}\%$ ( $T_{\mathrm{d}5}$ ) of thermal decomposition of the various samples are shown in Table 1. The film thickness of all the films was about $200\mu \mathrm{m}$ . The spectroscopy, optical transmittance, microstructure, thermal and physicochemical properties of the final composites are systematically discussed in Figures 3-8. 71: 72: Table 1. Thermal analysis of (a) pure PI, (b) PI-TSi, and (c) PI-TSi-GPMS50 composites. 102: 103: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 104: Figure 8. Effect of silica and GPMS-modified silica on the dielectric constant of polyimide. 105: ## excerpt 3 | block=b0003 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=110-112 | keywords=ElectricalDielectricProperties 110: # 2.4. Characterization and Properties Evaluation 111: 112: The solid-state NMR (Nuclear Magnetic Resonance) spectra were recorded on the same NMR instrument equipped with solid-state probe using the following experimental conditions: $^{13}\mathrm{C}$ CP/MAS NMR, 6 usec prescan delay time, 2000 usec contact pulse, $5\mathrm{kHz}$ MAS rate, and 2000 scans; $^{29}\mathrm{Si}$ CP/MAS NMR, 30.0 usec delay time, 1200 usec contact pulse, $5\mathrm{kHz}$ MAS rate, and 2500 scans. Then, a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) (JEOL) was used for observing the fractured cross-section of composite films. The fractured surfaces of the samples were obtained by dipping them into liquid nitrogen for $10\mathrm{min}$ and then broken into two pieces by radial impact force. The exposed fracture surfaces were gold-coated for 20 sec prior to SEM observation. UV spectra for the reported samples were recorded using a UV-Visible photo-spectrometer (UV-3101PC). Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra data were collected using a Perkin-Elmer spectrometer using ATR (Attenuated Total Reflection) mode. Thermogravimetric analysis was carried using under nitrogen atmosphere on TA instrument (TGA Q500) by maintaining a heating rate of $15^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ from 50 to $950^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The dielectric constants (DC) of the films were recorded on a TA instrument (DEA 2970) analyzer with a heating rate of $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ . [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=131-133 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=PI; family=PI, Polyimide 131: 132: Figure 5 shows the optical transmittance $(\mathrm{T}\%)$ of pure PI and composites at $638~\mathrm{nm}$ . The pure PI was highly transparent which was supported by the value of optical transmittance $(\mathrm{T}\%)$ $(89\%)$ at $638~\mathrm{nm}$ . In contrast, for the composite film, the opaque PI-TSi composite film showed only $45\%$ transmittance, indicating higher light scattering due to the increased particle sizes of silica as shown in Figure 4b. The physical observations of the films are also reported in Table 1. The optical transmittance $(\mathrm{T}\%)$ in Table 1 and Figure 5 was found to be increased for PI-TSi-GPMS50, and PI-TSi-GPMS75 composites indicated the effects of increasing the amount of GPMS in the TEOS mixture that allow favorably good dispersion of silica particles in the polymer phase. Such compatibilized composite films are brought by the GPMS that offers enough bonds/interactions between the organic and inorganic phases. These two factors would lead to the reduction of the average particle size of silica and good dispersion of such reduced silica particles in the polymer matrix. However, the overall phenomenon observed could be interpreted in terms of increasing domain size of silica that result in high scattering. Similar results were reported previously for the polyimide/silica-based composites [34,38,39]. 133: ## excerpt 2 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=130-133 | keywords=OpticalProperties; family=PI 130: # 3.3. Optical Transmittance 131: 132: Figure 5 shows the optical transmittance $(\mathrm{T}\%)$ of pure PI and composites at $638~\mathrm{nm}$ . The pure PI was highly transparent which was supported by the value of optical transmittance $(\mathrm{T}\%)$ $(89\%)$ at $638~\mathrm{nm}$ . In contrast, for the composite film, the opaque PI-TSi composite film showed only $45\%$ transmittance, indicating higher light scattering due to the increased particle sizes of silica as shown in Figure 4b. The physical observations of the films are also reported in Table 1. The optical transmittance $(\mathrm{T}\%)$ in Table 1 and Figure 5 was found to be increased for PI-TSi-GPMS50, and PI-TSi-GPMS75 composites indicated the effects of increasing the amount of GPMS in the TEOS mixture that allow favorably good dispersion of silica particles in the polymer phase. Such compatibilized composite films are brought by the GPMS that offers enough bonds/interactions between the organic and inorganic phases. These two factors would lead to the reduction of the average particle size of silica and good dispersion of such reduced silica particles in the polymer matrix. However, the overall phenomenon observed could be interpreted in terms of increasing domain size of silica that result in high scattering. Similar results were reported previously for the polyimide/silica-based composites [34,38,39]. 133:

62: ## excerpt 2 | block=b0003 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=67-71 | keywords=MechanicalProperties, RheologicalProperties 67: # 2.4. Characterization and Measurements 68: 69: The number-average molecular weights $(M_{\mathrm{n}})$ and the polydispersities $(M_{\mathrm{w}} / M_{\mathrm{n}})$ of these oligoimides were measured by a gel permeation chromatography (GPC) system equipped with a model 1515 HPLC pump (Waters, Milford, MA, USA), three Waters Styragel columns (HT-2 × 2 and HT-4) and a Waters 2414 refractive index detector. NMP with $0.02\mathrm{M}$ LiBr was used as eluent at a flow rate of $0.8~\mathrm{mL / min}$ . The sample concentration was $5\mathrm{mg / mL}$ and monodisperse polystyrene standards were used for the calibration. The chemical structure of oligoimides were evaluated by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) on a Perkin-Elmer 782 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Hong Kong, China) with pressed KBr pellets. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were detected by a Ragaku D/MAX-2500 X-ray powder diffraction (PANalytical B.V., Almelo, The Netherlands) with Cu-K $\alpha$ incident radiation ( $\lambda = 1.5418\AA$ ). Rheological measurements were tested by an AR2000 rheometer of TA Instrument (New Castle, DE, USA). The specimen discs with a diameter of $25\mathrm{mm}$ and a thickness of $1.3 - 1.5\mathrm{mm}$ were prepared by press molding the oligoimide powder. In the measurement, the specimen discs were loaded in the parallel plates, in which the top parallel plate was oscillated at a fixed angular frequency of $5\mathrm{rad / s}$ and a fixed strain of $0.1\%$ . To study the dynamic rheological behavior of the oligoimides, the specimen discs were scanned from 200 to $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at a rate of $4^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ . The isothermal viscosity of the oligoimides were determined measured at the $260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $2\mathrm{h}$ . Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) (New Castle, DE, USA) and dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) (New Castle, DE, USA) in nitrogen atmosphere were carried out by TA Q100 at a rate of $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ and TA Q800 at a rate of $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ , respectively. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (New Castle, DE, USA) in the air was performed on a TA Q50 (New Castle, DE, USA) a rate of $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ atmosphere. Instron 5567 tensile tester (Norwood, MA, USA) was used to assess the mechanical 70: 71: properties of the cured polyimides. The tensile and flexural properties were carried out in accordance with GB/T 1040.2-2006 and GB/T 9341-2008, respectively. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (VG Instruments, Altrincham, Cheshire, England) was determined by an ESCALab 220i-XL with an Al Kα excitation source. Change correction was carried out by the C 1s core line setting the hydrocarbon signal to $284.6\mathrm{eV}$ . [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=97-99 | keywords=RheologicalProperties; anchors=PIS-A0; family=PIS-A0 97: 98: due to the softening of backbone, which then decreased gradually until the temperature enhanced to $330^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . As the scanning temperature was over $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the dynamic viscosity increased abruptly due to the crosslinking of phenylethynyl group. Moreover, it can be seen that PIS-A oligoimides showed the lower minimum melt viscosity with the increase of siloxane content, which is $0.55\mathrm{Pa}\cdot \mathrm{s}$ for PIS-A0 and $0.12\mathrm{Pa}\cdot \mathrm{s}$ for PIA-A30, respectively. Comparing the oligoimides with different dianhydride structure, it is found that their minimum melt viscosity dropped in the order of PIS-T10 $>$ PIS-A10 $>$ PIS-O10. The PIS-O10 oligoimide derived from a-ODPA containing flexible ether linkage gave the minimum melt viscosity as low as $0.09\mathrm{Pa}\cdot \mathrm{s}$ at $333^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . It is worth to mention that both of PIS-A30 and PIS-O10 oligoimides revealed the melt viscosity no more than 1 Pa·s in the temperature range of $260 - 280^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , implying the broader processing window. 99: ## excerpt 2 | block=b0004 | section=3. 106: ## excerpt 3 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=120-122 | keywords=MechanicalProperties, PhysicalProperties; anchors=PIS-A0; family=PIS-A0 120: 121: These PEPA-terminated oligoimides were molded using the hot press at $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $2\mathrm{~h}$ to gain the cured polyimide sheet. Table 3 summarized the mechanical properties of the cured polyimides at room temperature. High-quality polyimide sheets can be obtained except PIS-A30 because it is not tough enough to fabricate the testing specimen. This may be related to the relatively low molecular weight of PIS-A30 oligoimide, resulting in the brittleness of cured polyimide resin [28,29]. Brostow et al. formulated a quantitative definition of brittleness in terms of elongation at break [30,31]. They think there is an inverse relationship between the brittleness and elongation at break. Our results are also consistent with their research. The cured polyimides prepared from these oligoimides with higher siloxane content exhibited a slight decrease in toughness because of the relatively lower molecular weight and higher crosslinking density. For example, PIS-A0 exhibited good mechanical properties with the tensile strength of $70.48\mathrm{MPa}$ , flexural strength of $121.52\mathrm{MPa}$ , and elongation at breakage of $3.90\%$ , while those for PIS-A20 decreased to $62.28\mathrm{MPa}$ , $99.37\mathrm{MPa}$ and $2.77\%$ , respectively. Comparing the cured polyimides based on different isomeric dianhydrides with same siloxane content, it is found that they exhibited the moderate tensile and flexural strengths in the ranges of 54.69–62.82 and 102.78–114.07 MPa, respectively. The cured PIS-T10 gave the highest tensile and flexural modulus because of the rigidity of dianhydride. 122: ## excerpt 4 | block=b0004 | section=3. 126: 127: The thermal properties of these cured polyimide resins were detected through DMA and TGA. The DMA curves of these cured polyimide resins are shown in Figure 7, in which the storage modulus curves firstly decreased gradually and then dropped rapidly with the temperature increasing. In the tanδ curves, the weak subglass relaxation peaks owing to $2,2^{\prime}$ -disubstitutes of benzidine moiety with $-\mathrm{CF}_3$ were observed around $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ [32], meanwhile, the sharp glass relaxation peaks related to the movement of polyimide backbone were also detected at $323 - 442^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . It is noticed that these cured polyimide resins exhibited an obvious decrease in $T_{\mathrm{gs}}$ with the increase of siloxane content. From the DMA data summarized in Table 4, it is found that the difference in $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ between PIS-A0 and PIS-A30 is as high as $119^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The cured PIS-T10 resin derived from a-BTDA showed the relatively higher $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ of $437^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ as compared with the cured PIS-A10 and PIS-O10 resins with similar siloxane content. Chuang et al. reported that the planar $\mathfrak{sp}^2$ bonding in carbonyl group $(\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{O})$ is more rigid than the $\mathfrak{sp}^3$ tetrahedral bonding linkage [33]. They also suggested that the rotational barrier of a-BTDA may be higher than that of a-BPDA because carbonyl group in a-BTDA imparts greater rigidity to the polymer backbone [16]. Therefore, the good thermal stability of cured PIS-T10 may be owing to the carbonyl group in the isomeric dianhydride, which increases the energy for the crankshaft motion of cured polyimide resin and provides the high thermal stability as a result. 128: ## excerpt 5 | block=b0004 | section=3. 135: ## excerpt 6 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=137-139 | keywords=ProcessingProperties; anchors=PIS-A0; family=PIS-A0 137: 138: The thermal oxidative stability of these cured polyimide resins was evaluated by TGA measurement in air atmosphere and illustrated in Figure 8. These cured polyimide resins exhibited excellent thermal oxidative stability. They showed no obvious weight loss before $450.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and exhibited the temperature at $5\%$ and $10\%$ loss of the original weight ( $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ ) exceeding $539.7^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $576.7^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. For cured PIS-A resins, there is a gradual decline in $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values according with the siloxane content increase. For instance, PIS-A0 revealed the $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values of 576.7 and $602.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively, while PIS-A30 gave the lower $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values of 549.2 and $589.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. This may be associated with the early thermoxidative degradation of Si-C bond in siloxane moiety due to its lower bond dissociation energy than the others [34]. However, it is noteworthy that the cured polyimide resins exhibited a higher char residue at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ along with the increasing siloxane content. Their unexpected thermal degradation behavior is probably related to the siloxane oxidation, which leads to a higher char residue. When comparing the TGA results of cured polyimide resins with different isomeric dianhydrides, it is found that $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values for these resins decreased in the order of PIS-A10 > PIS-T10 > PIS-O10. It is known that the bond dissociation energy of Ar-Ar (450–460 kJ/mol) is greater than that of Ar-CO-Ar (396 kJ/mol) and Ar-O-Ar (330 kJ/mol). As a result, the PIS-A10 displayed relatively higher thermal oxidative stability than the others [35,36]. 139:

NMP with $0.02\mathrm{M}$ LiBr was used as eluent at a flow rate of $0.8~\mathrm{mL / min}$ . The sample concentration was $5\mathrm{mg / mL}$ and monodisperse polystyrene standards were used for the calibration. The chemical structure of oligoimides were evaluated by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) on a Perkin-Elmer 782 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Hong Kong, China) with pressed KBr pellets. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were detected by a Ragaku D/MAX-2500 X-ray powder diffraction (PANalytical B.V., Almelo, The Netherlands) with Cu-K $\alpha$ incident radiation ( $\lambda = 1.5418\AA$ ). Rheological measurements were tested by an AR2000 rheometer of TA Instrument (New Castle, DE, USA). The specimen discs with a diameter of $25\mathrm{mm}$ and a thickness of $1.3 - 1.5\mathrm{mm}$ were prepared by press molding the oligoimide powder. In the measurement, the specimen discs were loaded in the parallel plates, in which the top parallel plate was oscillated at a fixed angular frequency of $5\mathrm{rad / s}$ and a fixed strain of $0.1\%$ . To study the dynamic rheological behavior of the oligoimides, the specimen discs were scanned from 200 to $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at a rate of $4^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ . The isothermal viscosity of the oligoimides were determined measured at the $260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $2\mathrm{h}$ . Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) (New Castle, DE, USA) and dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) (New Castle, DE, USA) in nitrogen atmosphere were carried out by TA Q100 at a rate of $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ and TA Q800 at a rate of $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ , respectively. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (New Castle, DE, USA) in the air was performed on a TA Q50 (New Castle, DE, USA) a rate of $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ atmosphere. Instron 5567 tensile tester (Norwood, MA, USA) was used to assess the mechanical 70: 71: properties of the cured polyimides. The tensile and flexural properties were carried out in accordance with GB/T 1040.2-2006 and GB/T 9341-2008, respectively. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (VG Instruments, Altrincham, Cheshire, England) was determined by an ESCALab 220i-XL with an Al Kα excitation source. Change correction was carried out by the C 1s core line setting the hydrocarbon signal to $284.6\mathrm{eV}$ . [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Comparing the oligoimides with different dianhydride structure, it is found that their minimum melt viscosity dropped in the order of PIS-T10 $>$ PIS-A10 $>$ PIS-O10. 126: 127: The thermal properties of these cured polyimide resins were detected through DMA and TGA. The DMA curves of these cured polyimide resins are shown in Figure 7, in which the storage modulus curves firstly decreased gradually and then dropped rapidly with the temperature increasing. In the tanδ curves, the weak subglass relaxation peaks owing to $2,2^{\prime}$ -disubstitutes of benzidine moiety with $-\mathrm{CF}_3$ were observed around $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ [32], meanwhile, the sharp glass relaxation peaks related to the movement of polyimide backbone were also detected at $323 - 442^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . It is noticed that these cured polyimide resins exhibited an obvious decrease in $T_{\mathrm{gs}}$ with the increase of siloxane content. From the DMA data summarized in Table 4, it is found that the difference in $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ between PIS-A0 and PIS-A30 is as high as $119^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The cured PIS-T10 resin derived from a-BTDA showed the relatively higher $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ of $437^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ as compared with the cured PIS-A10 and PIS-O10 resins with similar siloxane content. Chuang et al. reported that the planar $\mathfrak{sp}^2$ bonding in carbonyl group $(\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{O})$ is more rigid than the $\mathfrak{sp}^3$ tetrahedral bonding linkage [33]. They also suggested that the rotational barrier of a-BTDA may be higher than that of a-BPDA because carbonyl group in a-BTDA imparts greater rigidity to the polymer backbone [16]. Therefore, the good thermal stability of cured PIS-T10 may be owing to the carbonyl group in the isomeric dianhydride, which increases the energy for the crankshaft motion of cured polyimide resin and provides the high thermal stability as a result. 128: ## excerpt 4 | block=b0004 | section=3. 135: ## excerpt 5 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=137-139 | keywords=ProcessingProperties; anchors=PIS-A10; family=PIS-A10 137: 138: The thermal oxidative stability of these cured polyimide resins was evaluated by TGA measurement in air atmosphere and illustrated in Figure 8. These cured polyimide resins exhibited excellent thermal oxidative stability. They showed no obvious weight loss before $450.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and exhibited the temperature at $5\%$ and $10\%$ loss of the original weight ( $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ ) exceeding $539.7^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $576.7^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. For cured PIS-A resins, there is a gradual decline in $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values according with the siloxane content increase. For instance, PIS-A0 revealed the $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values of 576.7 and $602.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively, while PIS-A30 gave the lower $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values of 549.2 and $589.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. This may be associated with the early thermoxidative degradation of Si-C bond in siloxane moiety due to its lower bond dissociation energy than the others [34]. However, it is noteworthy that the cured polyimide resins exhibited a higher char residue at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ along with the increasing siloxane content. Their unexpected thermal degradation behavior is probably related to the siloxane oxidation, which leads to a higher char residue. When comparing the TGA results of cured polyimide resins with different isomeric dianhydrides, it is found that $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values for these resins decreased in the order of PIS-A10 > PIS-T10 > PIS-O10. It is known that the bond dissociation energy of Ar-Ar (450–460 kJ/mol) is greater than that of Ar-CO-Ar (396 kJ/mol) and Ar-O-Ar (330 kJ/mol). As a result, the PIS-A10 displayed relatively higher thermal oxidative stability than the others [35,36]. 139: ## excerpt 6 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=120-122 | keywords=MechanicalProperties, PhysicalProperties; anchors=PIS-A20; family=PIS-A20 120: 121: These PEPA-terminated oligoimides were molded using the hot press at $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $2\mathrm{~h}$ to gain the cured polyimide sheet. Table 3 summarized the mechanical properties of the cured polyimides at room temperature. High-quality polyimide sheets can be obtained except PIS-A30 because it is not tough enough to fabricate the testing specimen. This may be related to the relatively low molecular weight of PIS-A30 oligoimide, resulting in the brittleness of cured polyimide resin [28,29]. Brostow et al. formulated a quantitative definition of brittleness in terms of elongation at break [30,31]. They think there is an inverse relationship between the brittleness and elongation at break. Our results are also consistent with their research. The cured polyimides prepared from these oligoimides with higher siloxane content exhibited a slight decrease in toughness because of the relatively lower molecular weight and higher crosslinking density. For example, PIS-A0 exhibited good mechanical properties with the tensile strength of $70.48\mathrm{MPa}$ , flexural strength of $121.52\mathrm{MPa}$ , and elongation at breakage of $3.90\%$ , while those for PIS-A20 decreased to $62.28\mathrm{MPa}$ , $99.37\mathrm{MPa}$ and $2.77\%$ , respectively. Comparing the cured polyimides based on different isomeric dianhydrides with same siloxane content, it is found that they exhibited the moderate tensile and flexural strengths in the ranges of 54.69–62.82 and 102.78–114.07 MPa, respectively. The cured PIS-T10 gave the highest tensile and flexural modulus because of the rigidity of dianhydride. 122: ## excerpt 3 | block=b0004 | section=3. From the DMA data summarized in Table 4, it is found that the difference in $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ between PIS-A0 and PIS-A30 is as high as $119^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The cured PIS-T10 resin derived from a-BTDA showed the relatively higher $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ of $437^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ as compared with the cured PIS-A10 and PIS-O10 resins with similar siloxane content. Chuang et al. reported that the planar $\mathfrak{sp}^2$ bonding in carbonyl group $(\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{O})$ is more rigid than the $\mathfrak{sp}^3$ tetrahedral bonding linkage [33]. They also suggested that the rotational barrier of a-BTDA may be higher than that of a-BPDA because carbonyl group in a-BTDA imparts greater rigidity to the polymer backbone [16]. Therefore, the good thermal stability of cured PIS-T10 may be owing to the carbonyl group in the isomeric dianhydride, which increases the energy for the crankshaft motion of cured polyimide resin and provides the high thermal stability as a result. 128: ## excerpt 4 | block=b0004 | section=3. 135: ## excerpt 5 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=137-139 | keywords=ProcessingProperties; anchors=PIS-A30; family=PIS-A30 137: 138: The thermal oxidative stability of these cured polyimide resins was evaluated by TGA measurement in air atmosphere and illustrated in Figure 8. These cured polyimide resins exhibited excellent thermal oxidative stability. They showed no obvious weight loss before $450.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and exhibited the temperature at $5\%$ and $10\%$ loss of the original weight ( $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ ) exceeding $539.7^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $576.7^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. For cured PIS-A resins, there is a gradual decline in $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values according with the siloxane content increase. For instance, PIS-A0 revealed the $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values of 576.7 and $602.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively, while PIS-A30 gave the lower $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values of 549.2 and $589.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. This may be associated with the early thermoxidative degradation of Si-C bond in siloxane moiety due to its lower bond dissociation energy than the others [34]. However, it is noteworthy that the cured polyimide resins exhibited a higher char residue at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ along with the increasing siloxane content. Their unexpected thermal degradation behavior is probably related to the siloxane oxidation, which leads to a higher char residue. When comparing the TGA results of cured polyimide resins with different isomeric dianhydrides, it is found that $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values for these resins decreased in the order of PIS-A10 > PIS-T10 > PIS-O10. It is known that the bond dissociation energy of Ar-Ar (450–460 kJ/mol) is greater than that of Ar-CO-Ar (396 kJ/mol) and Ar-O-Ar (330 kJ/mol). As a result, the PIS-A10 displayed relatively higher thermal oxidative stability than the others [35,36]. 139:

62: ## excerpt 2 | block=b0003 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=67-71 | keywords=MechanicalProperties, RheologicalProperties 67: # 2.4. Characterization and Measurements 68: 69: The number-average molecular weights $(M_{\mathrm{n}})$ and the polydispersities $(M_{\mathrm{w}} / M_{\mathrm{n}})$ of these oligoimides were measured by a gel permeation chromatography (GPC) system equipped with a model 1515 HPLC pump (Waters, Milford, MA, USA), three Waters Styragel columns (HT-2 × 2 and HT-4) and a Waters 2414 refractive index detector. NMP with $0.02\mathrm{M}$ LiBr was used as eluent at a flow rate of $0.8~\mathrm{mL / min}$ . The sample concentration was $5\mathrm{mg / mL}$ and monodisperse polystyrene standards were used for the calibration. The chemical structure of oligoimides were evaluated by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) on a Perkin-Elmer 782 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Hong Kong, China) with pressed KBr pellets. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were detected by a Ragaku D/MAX-2500 X-ray powder diffraction (PANalytical B.V., Almelo, The Netherlands) with Cu-K $\alpha$ incident radiation ( $\lambda = 1.5418\AA$ ). Rheological measurements were tested by an AR2000 rheometer of TA Instrument (New Castle, DE, USA). The specimen discs with a diameter of $25\mathrm{mm}$ and a thickness of $1.3 - 1.5\mathrm{mm}$ were prepared by press molding the oligoimide powder. In the measurement, the specimen discs were loaded in the parallel plates, in which the top parallel plate was oscillated at a fixed angular frequency of $5\mathrm{rad / s}$ and a fixed strain of $0.1\%$ . To study the dynamic rheological behavior of the oligoimides, the specimen discs were scanned from 200 to $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at a rate of $4^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ . The isothermal viscosity of the oligoimides were determined measured at the $260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $2\mathrm{h}$ . Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) (New Castle, DE, USA) and dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) (New Castle, DE, USA) in nitrogen atmosphere were carried out by TA Q100 at a rate of $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ and TA Q800 at a rate of $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ , respectively. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (New Castle, DE, USA) in the air was performed on a TA Q50 (New Castle, DE, USA) a rate of $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ atmosphere. Instron 5567 tensile tester (Norwood, MA, USA) was used to assess the mechanical 70: 71: properties of the cured polyimides. The tensile and flexural properties were carried out in accordance with GB/T 1040.2-2006 and GB/T 9341-2008, respectively. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (VG Instruments, Altrincham, Cheshire, England) was determined by an ESCALab 220i-XL with an Al Kα excitation source. Change correction was carried out by the C 1s core line setting the hydrocarbon signal to $284.6\mathrm{eV}$ . [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=97-99 | keywords=RheologicalProperties; anchors=PIS-T10; family=PIS-T10 97: 98: due to the softening of backbone, which then decreased gradually until the temperature enhanced to $330^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . As the scanning temperature was over $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the dynamic viscosity increased abruptly due to the crosslinking of phenylethynyl group. Moreover, it can be seen that PIS-A oligoimides showed the lower minimum melt viscosity with the increase of siloxane content, which is $0.55\mathrm{Pa}\cdot \mathrm{s}$ for PIS-A0 and $0.12\mathrm{Pa}\cdot \mathrm{s}$ for PIA-A30, respectively. Comparing the oligoimides with different dianhydride structure, it is found that their minimum melt viscosity dropped in the order of PIS-T10 $>$ PIS-A10 $>$ PIS-O10. The PIS-O10 oligoimide derived from a-ODPA containing flexible ether linkage gave the minimum melt viscosity as low as $0.09\mathrm{Pa}\cdot \mathrm{s}$ at $333^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . It is worth to mention that both of PIS-A30 and PIS-O10 oligoimides revealed the melt viscosity no more than 1 Pa·s in the temperature range of $260 - 280^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , implying the broader processing window. 99: ## excerpt 2 | block=b0004 | section=3. 106: ## excerpt 3 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=120-122 | keywords=MechanicalProperties, PhysicalProperties; anchors=PIS-T10; family=PIS-T10 120: 121: These PEPA-terminated oligoimides were molded using the hot press at $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $2\mathrm{~h}$ to gain the cured polyimide sheet. Table 3 summarized the mechanical properties of the cured polyimides at room temperature. High-quality polyimide sheets can be obtained except PIS-A30 because it is not tough enough to fabricate the testing specimen. This may be related to the relatively low molecular weight of PIS-A30 oligoimide, resulting in the brittleness of cured polyimide resin [28,29]. Brostow et al. formulated a quantitative definition of brittleness in terms of elongation at break [30,31]. They think there is an inverse relationship between the brittleness and elongation at break. Our results are also consistent with their research. The cured polyimides prepared from these oligoimides with higher siloxane content exhibited a slight decrease in toughness because of the relatively lower molecular weight and higher crosslinking density. For example, PIS-A0 exhibited good mechanical properties with the tensile strength of $70.48\mathrm{MPa}$ , flexural strength of $121.52\mathrm{MPa}$ , and elongation at breakage of $3.90\%$ , while those for PIS-A20 decreased to $62.28\mathrm{MPa}$ , $99.37\mathrm{MPa}$ and $2.77\%$ , respectively. Comparing the cured polyimides based on different isomeric dianhydrides with same siloxane content, it is found that they exhibited the moderate tensile and flexural strengths in the ranges of 54.69–62.82 and 102.78–114.07 MPa, respectively. The cured PIS-T10 gave the highest tensile and flexural modulus because of the rigidity of dianhydride. 122: ## excerpt 4 | block=b0004 | section=3. Chuang et al. reported that the planar $\mathfrak{sp}^2$ bonding in carbonyl group $(\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{O})$ is more rigid than the $\mathfrak{sp}^3$ tetrahedral bonding linkage [33]. They also suggested that the rotational barrier of a-BTDA may be higher than that of a-BPDA because carbonyl group in a-BTDA imparts greater rigidity to the polymer backbone [16]. Therefore, the good thermal stability of cured PIS-T10 may be owing to the carbonyl group in the isomeric dianhydride, which increases the energy for the crankshaft motion of cured polyimide resin and provides the high thermal stability as a result. 128: ## excerpt 4 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=137-139 | keywords=ProcessingProperties; anchors=PIS-O10; family=PIS-O10 137: 138: The thermal oxidative stability of these cured polyimide resins was evaluated by TGA measurement in air atmosphere and illustrated in Figure 8. These cured polyimide resins exhibited excellent thermal oxidative stability. They showed no obvious weight loss before $450.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and exhibited the temperature at $5\%$ and $10\%$ loss of the original weight ( $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ ) exceeding $539.7^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $576.7^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. For cured PIS-A resins, there is a gradual decline in $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values according with the siloxane content increase. For instance, PIS-A0 revealed the $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values of 576.7 and $602.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively, while PIS-A30 gave the lower $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values of 549.2 and $589.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. This may be associated with the early thermoxidative degradation of Si-C bond in siloxane moiety due to its lower bond dissociation energy than the others [34]. However, it is noteworthy that the cured polyimide resins exhibited a higher char residue at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ along with the increasing siloxane content. Their unexpected thermal degradation behavior is probably related to the siloxane oxidation, which leads to a higher char residue. When comparing the TGA results of cured polyimide resins with different isomeric dianhydrides, it is found that $T_{5}$ and $T_{10}$ values for these resins decreased in the order of PIS-A10 > PIS-T10 > PIS-O10. It is known that the bond dissociation energy of Ar-Ar (450–460 kJ/mol) is greater than that of Ar-CO-Ar (396 kJ/mol) and Ar-O-Ar (330 kJ/mol). As a result, the PIS-A10 displayed relatively higher thermal oxidative stability than the others [35,36]. 139: ## excerpt 6 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=144-146 | keywords=ThermalProperties; anchors=PIS-O10; family=PIS-O10 144: 145: In our previous research, it has been confirmed that the thermal stability of polyimide resins containing siloxane structure can be improved by postcuring at a high temperature [37]. Taking the cured PIS-O10 resin as an example, it was postcured in an air atmosphere at 400, 420, and $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for different times, respectively. Its thermal properties and structural evolution of siloxane before and after postcuring were investigated. The $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ values of PIS-O10 as a function of postcuring conditions are depicted in Figure 9 and compared with that of PIS-O10 as cured at $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . It is found that the $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ values enhanced from 336 to $385^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for the PIS-O10 before and after postcuring at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $1\mathrm{h}$ , which showed only slight increase with the time extension. However, as the postcuring temperature enhanced to $420^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , PIS-O10 resin showed a significant increase in $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ values along with the postcuring time. The PIS-O10 resin after postcuring at $420^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $3\mathrm{h}$ gave the $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ value as high as $517^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which is $181^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ higher than that of as cured one. It is unexpected that the glass transition peak of tanδ even exceeding the testing temperature range of $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The improved thermal stability of PIS-O10 is supposed to relate to the oxidative crosslinking of siloxane. 146: ## excerpt 7 | block=b0004 | section=3.
