[POLYMER_ANCHOR] 身份标识: doi:10.3390/ma13173742_PIS-A0 cured polyimide_01 名称: PIS-A0 cured polyimide 别名: 聚合物分类编码: PI 聚合物分类名称: crosslinked polyimide resin 样本形态: sheet [RESULT_PROPERTY_HINTS] [PROPERTY_METHODS] [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=143-151 | keywords=ThermalProperties 143: # 3.4. The Effect of Postcuring Conditions on the Thermal Properties of Cured Polyimides 144: 145: In our previous research, it has been confirmed that the thermal stability of polyimide resins containing siloxane structure can be improved by postcuring at a high temperature [37]. Taking the cured PIS-O10 resin as an example, it was postcured in an air atmosphere at 400, 420, and $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for different times, respectively. Its thermal properties and structural evolution of siloxane before and after postcuring were investigated. The $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ values of PIS-O10 as a function of postcuring conditions are depicted in Figure 9 and compared with that of PIS-O10 as cured at $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . It is found that the $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ values enhanced from 336 to $385^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for the PIS-O10 before and after postcuring at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $1\mathrm{h}$ , which showed only slight increase with the time extension. However, as the postcuring temperature enhanced to $420^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , PIS-O10 resin showed a significant increase in $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ values along with the postcuring time. The PIS-O10 resin after postcuring at $420^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $3\mathrm{h}$ gave the $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ value as high as $517^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which is $181^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ higher than that of as cured one. It is unexpected that the glass transition peak of tanδ even exceeding the testing temperature range of $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The improved thermal stability of PIS-O10 is supposed to relate to the oxidative crosslinking of siloxane. 146: 147: The structure evolution of siloxane before and after postcuring at different temperature and time was investigated by XPS analysis. As shown in Figure 10, the Si 2p peak was centered at $102.04\mathrm{eV}$ for PIS-O10 as cured at $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $2\mathrm{h}$ , which shifted to the higher binding energy with the increase of postcuring temperature. It is noted that the binding energy of Si 2p for PIS-O10 after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was centered at $103.59\mathrm{eV}$ . It is well known that when an oxygen atom substitutes an alkyl group attached to the silicon atom, the binding energy of a siloxy unit will increase. Generally, the Si 2p spectra can be fitted with three components, which is the 101.90, 102.90 and $103.50\mathrm{eV}$ corresponding to the siloxy units of $\mathrm{D[(CH_3)_2SiO_{2/2}]}$ , $\mathrm{T[CH_3SiO_{3/2}]}$ and $\mathrm{Q[SiO_{4/2}]}$ , respectively [19]. The curve fitting results of the Si 2p peak for as cured PIS-O10 at $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ only gave the D and T siloxy units with the concentration of 23.7 and 76.3 at.% respectively. However, after PIS-O10 was postcured at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the Q siloxy units appeared because of the further siloxane oxidation. In the meantime, the increasing 148: 149: intensity of T siloxy units was combined with decreasing D siloxy units. There is more Q component combined with less D and T siloxy units with the increasing of postcuring temperature and extension of postcuring time for PIS-O10. The curve fitting results of Si 2p XPS listed in Table 5 show that PIS-O10 after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 1h gave the Q siloxy units with a concentration as high as 76.1 at.%, and that for D and T siloxy units, only 10.5 and 13.4 at.%, respectively. The results indicate that the siloxane structure in polyimides undergoes the oxidative crosslinking with the increase of postcuring temperature and time. After PIS-O10 was postcured at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , most of the siloxane structure is finally converted into inorganic silica structure, which leads to the enhancement of $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ . This can also well explain why polyimide resins with more siloxane content have higher amount of char residue as tested in the air. 150: 151: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ## excerpt 2 | block=b0004 | section=3. 166: 167: The inorganic silica-like domains formed after postcuring at elevated temperature can enhance the thermal stability of the polyimide resins with siloxane structure. Therefore, the postcuring effect on the thermal properties of siloxane-containing polyimide derived from different isomeric dianhydrides were investigated. From DMA curves of these polyimide resins after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , shown in Figure 11, it is found that the storage modulus revealed no obvious decrease even as the scanning temperature exceeding $460^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , suggesting their thermal stability extremely improved. In the tanδ curves, the strong subglass relaxation peaks owing to $2,2'$ -disubstitutes of benzidine moiety with $-\mathrm{CF}_3$ around $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ combined with broad glass transition peaks over $498^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ were detected. Moreover, it is noticed that the glass transition peak of tanδ even exceeding the testing temperature range of $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for PIS-T10 and PIS-O10 after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . 168: 169: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 170: Figure 11. DMA curves of the polyimide resins with different dianhydride structure after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $1\mathrm{h}$ . 171: 172: The significant enhancement of thermal stability for these polyimide resins is associated with the deep crosslinking of phenylethynyl group and further oxidative crosslinking of siloxane. The corresponding TGA data of polyimide resins with different isomeric dianhydrides after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ were listed in Table 6. These postcured resins exhibited better thermal oxidative stability with the $T_{5} > 573.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $T_{10} > 595.1^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which are 16.4-33.2 and 5.2-17.3 °C higher than those for as cured ones. 173: 174: The oxidative crosslinking state of siloxane for PIS-A10 and PIS-T10 resins after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ were also detected by XPS. As shown in Figure 12, the binding energy of Si 2p peaks for PIS-T10 and PIS-A10 were centered at 103.46 and $103.41\mathrm{eV}$ , respectively, which are slightly lower than that for PIS-O10 after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / 1\mathrm{h}$ (103.59 eV). Moreover, the curve fitting results of the Si 2p peaks 175: 176: for PIS-A10 and PIS-T10 after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 1 h gave the less Q siloxy units along with more D and T siloxy units as compared with that for PIS-O10 after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . It is suspected that the dramatic increase in $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ of PIS-O10 after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ correlated with the existence of ether groups, which promote the free radical oxidation crosslinking reaction of siloxane structure and thus further improve the $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ of the resin [35]. 177: 178: Table 6. The PIS-O10 resin after postcuring at  $420^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $3\mathrm{h}$  gave the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  value as high as  $517^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which is  $181^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  higher than that of as cured one. It is unexpected that the glass transition peak of tanδ even exceeding the testing temperature range of  $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  after postcuring at  $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The improved thermal stability of PIS-O10 is supposed to relate to the oxidative crosslinking of siloxane.
146: 
147: The structure evolution of siloxane before and after postcuring at different temperature and time was investigated by XPS analysis. As shown in Figure 10, the Si 2p peak was centered at  $102.04\mathrm{eV}$  for PIS-O10 as cured at  $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $2\mathrm{h}$ , which shifted to the higher binding energy with the increase of postcuring temperature. It is noted that the binding energy of Si 2p for PIS-O10 after postcuring at  $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  was centered at  $103.59\mathrm{eV}$ . It is well known that when an oxygen atom substitutes an alkyl group attached to the silicon atom, the binding energy of a siloxy unit will increase. Generally, the Si 2p spectra can be fitted with three components, which is the 101.90, 102.90 and  $103.50\mathrm{eV}$  corresponding to the siloxy units of  $\mathrm{D[(CH_3)_2SiO_{2/2}]}$ ,  $\mathrm{T[CH_3SiO_{3/2}]}$  and  $\mathrm{Q[SiO_{4/2}]}$ , respectively [19]. The curve fitting results of the Si 2p peak for as cured PIS-O10 at  $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  only gave the D and T siloxy units with the concentration of 23.7 and 76.3 at.% respectively. However, after PIS-O10 was postcured at  $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the Q siloxy units appeared because of the further siloxane oxidation. In the meantime, the increasing
148: 
149: intensity of T siloxy units was combined with decreasing D siloxy units. There is more Q component combined with less D and T siloxy units with the increasing of postcuring temperature and extension of postcuring time for PIS-O10. The curve fitting results of Si 2p XPS listed in Table 5 show that PIS-O10 after postcuring at  $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for 1h gave the Q siloxy units with a concentration as high as 76.1 at.%, and that for D and T siloxy units, only 10.5 and 13.4 at.%, respectively. The results indicate that the siloxane structure in polyimides undergoes the oxidative crosslinking with the increase of postcuring temperature and time. After PIS-O10 was postcured at  $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , most of the siloxane structure is finally converted into inorganic silica structure, which leads to the enhancement of  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ . This can also well explain why polyimide resins with more siloxane content have higher amount of char residue as tested in the air.

These postcured resins exhibited better thermal oxidative stability with the $T_{5} > 573.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $T_{10} > 595.1^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which are 16.4-33.2 and 5.2-17.3 °C higher than those for as cured ones. 173: 174: The oxidative crosslinking state of siloxane for PIS-A10 and PIS-T10 resins after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ were also detected by XPS. [POLYMER_ANCHOR] 身份标识: doi:10.3390/ma13173742_PIS-A30 cured polyimide_01 名称: PIS-A30 cured polyimide 别名: 聚合物分类编码: PI 聚合物分类名称: crosslinked polyimide resin containing siloxane structure 样本形态: sheet [RESULT_PROPERTY_HINTS] [PROPERTY_METHODS] [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=143-151 | keywords=ThermalProperties 143: # 3.4. The Effect of Postcuring Conditions on the Thermal Properties of Cured Polyimides 144: 145: In our previous research, it has been confirmed that the thermal stability of polyimide resins containing siloxane structure can be improved by postcuring at a high temperature [37]. Taking the cured PIS-O10 resin as an example, it was postcured in an air atmosphere at 400, 420, and $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for different times, respectively. Its thermal properties and structural evolution of siloxane before and after postcuring were investigated. The $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ values of PIS-O10 as a function of postcuring conditions are depicted in Figure 9 and compared with that of PIS-O10 as cured at $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . It is found that the $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ values enhanced from 336 to $385^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for the PIS-O10 before and after postcuring at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $1\mathrm{h}$ , which showed only slight increase with the time extension. However, as the postcuring temperature enhanced to $420^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , PIS-O10 resin showed a significant increase in $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ values along with the postcuring time. The PIS-O10 resin after postcuring at $420^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $3\mathrm{h}$ gave the $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ value as high as $517^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which is $181^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ higher than that of as cured one. It is unexpected that the glass transition peak of tanδ even exceeding the testing temperature range of $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The improved thermal stability of PIS-O10 is supposed to relate to the oxidative crosslinking of siloxane. 146: 147: The structure evolution of siloxane before and after postcuring at different temperature and time was investigated by XPS analysis. As shown in Figure 10, the Si 2p peak was centered at $102.04\mathrm{eV}$ for PIS-O10 as cured at $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $2\mathrm{h}$ , which shifted to the higher binding energy with the increase of postcuring temperature. It is noted that the binding energy of Si 2p for PIS-O10 after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was centered at $103.59\mathrm{eV}$ . It is well known that when an oxygen atom substitutes an alkyl group attached to the silicon atom, the binding energy of a siloxy unit will increase. Generally, the Si 2p spectra can be fitted with three components, which is the 101.90, 102.90 and $103.50\mathrm{eV}$ corresponding to the siloxy units of $\mathrm{D[(CH_3)_2SiO_{2/2}]}$ , $\mathrm{T[CH_3SiO_{3/2}]}$ and $\mathrm{Q[SiO_{4/2}]}$ , respectively [19]. The curve fitting results of the Si 2p peak for as cured PIS-O10 at $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ only gave the D and T siloxy units with the concentration of 23.7 and 76.3 at.% respectively. However, after PIS-O10 was postcured at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the Q siloxy units appeared because of the further siloxane oxidation. In the meantime, the increasing 148: 149: intensity of T siloxy units was combined with decreasing D siloxy units. There is more Q component combined with less D and T siloxy units with the increasing of postcuring temperature and extension of postcuring time for PIS-O10. The curve fitting results of Si 2p XPS listed in Table 5 show that PIS-O10 after postcuring at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 1h gave the Q siloxy units with a concentration as high as 76.1 at.%, and that for D and T siloxy units, only 10.5 and 13.4 at.%, respectively. The results indicate that the siloxane structure in polyimides undergoes the oxidative crosslinking with the increase of postcuring temperature and time. After PIS-O10 was postcured at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , most of the siloxane structure is finally converted into inorganic silica structure, which leads to the enhancement of $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ . This can also well explain why polyimide resins with more siloxane content have higher amount of char residue as tested in the air. 150: 151: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ## excerpt 2 | block=b0004 | section=3. After PIS-O10 was postcured at  $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , most of the siloxane structure is finally converted into inorganic silica structure, which leads to the enhancement of  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ . This can also well explain why polyimide resins with more siloxane content have higher amount of char residue as tested in the air.

## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=143-151 | keywords=ThermalProperties
143: # 3.4. The Effect of Postcuring Conditions on the Thermal Properties of Cured Polyimides
144: 
145: In our previous research, it has been confirmed that the thermal stability of polyimide resins containing siloxane structure can be improved by postcuring at a high temperature [37]. Taking the cured PIS-O10 resin as an example, it was postcured in an air atmosphere at 400, 420, and  $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for different times, respectively. Its thermal properties and structural evolution of siloxane before and after postcuring were investigated. The  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  values of PIS-O10 as a function of postcuring conditions are depicted in Figure 9 and compared with that of PIS-O10 as cured at  $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . It is found that the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  values enhanced from 336 to  $385^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for the PIS-O10 before and after postcuring at  $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{h}$ , which showed only slight increase with the time extension. However, as the postcuring temperature enhanced to  $420^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , PIS-O10 resin showed a significant increase in  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  values along with the postcuring time. The PIS-O10 resin after postcuring at  $420^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $3\mathrm{h}$  gave the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  value as high as  $517^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which is  $181^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  higher than that of as cured one. It is unexpected that the glass transition peak of tanδ even exceeding the testing temperature range of  $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  after postcuring at  $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The improved thermal stability of PIS-O10 is supposed to relate to the oxidative crosslinking of siloxane.
146: 
147: The structure evolution of siloxane before and after postcuring at different temperature and time was investigated by XPS analysis. As shown in Figure 10, the Si 2p peak was centered at  $102.04\mathrm{eV}$  for PIS-O10 as cured at  $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $2\mathrm{h}$ , which shifted to the higher binding energy with the increase of postcuring temperature. It is noted that the binding energy of Si 2p for PIS-O10 after postcuring at  $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  was centered at  $103.59\mathrm{eV}$ . It is well known that when an oxygen atom substitutes an alkyl group attached to the silicon atom, the binding energy of a siloxy unit will increase. Generally, the Si 2p spectra can be fitted with three components, which is the 101.90, 102.90 and  $103.50\mathrm{eV}$  corresponding to the siloxy units of  $\mathrm{D[(CH_3)_2SiO_{2/2}]}$ ,  $\mathrm{T[CH_3SiO_{3/2}]}$  and  $\mathrm{Q[SiO_{4/2}]}$ , respectively [19]. The curve fitting results of the Si 2p peak for as cured PIS-O10 at  $370^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  only gave the D and T siloxy units with the concentration of 23.7 and 76.3 at.% respectively. However, after PIS-O10 was postcured at  $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the Q siloxy units appeared because of the further siloxane oxidation. In the meantime, the increasing
148: 
149: intensity of T siloxy units was combined with decreasing D siloxy units. There is more Q component combined with less D and T siloxy units with the increasing of postcuring temperature and extension of postcuring time for PIS-O10. The curve fitting results of Si 2p XPS listed in Table 5 show that PIS-O10 after postcuring at  $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for 1h gave the Q siloxy units with a concentration as high as 76.1 at.%, and that for D and T siloxy units, only 10.5 and 13.4 at.%, respectively. The results indicate that the siloxane structure in polyimides undergoes the oxidative crosslinking with the increase of postcuring temperature and time. After PIS-O10 was postcured at  $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , most of the siloxane structure is finally converted into inorganic silica structure, which leads to the enhancement of  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ . This can also well explain why polyimide resins with more siloxane content have higher amount of char residue as tested in the air.

74: # 2.2. Characterization
75: 
76: IR spectra were taken on a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometer (Bruker Tensor II, Karlsruhe, Germany). Viscosity was measured with a Brookfield DV2THB viscometer in DMF at  $30^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The measurements were conducted as promptly as possible to avoid the depolymerization of PAAs. Surface roughness was measured by Dimension Icon atomic force microscopy (AFM) of Bruker (Dimension Icon, Karlsruhe, Germany). The glass transition temperature of the PI film was measured by NETZSCH (DMA 242E, Selb, Germany) with a heating rate of  $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C} \cdot \mathrm{min}^{-1}$  from  $30^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  to  $480^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  under nitrogen. Mikrometry DHG-050 was used to measure the thickness of the films. A UV-vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-3900H, Tokyo, Japan) was used to measure the optical transmission. The scan range was  $300 - 800\mathrm{nm}$ , and the scan rate of transmission measurement was  $600\mathrm{nm} \cdot \mathrm{min}^{-1}$ . The tensile strength and elongation at break of PI specimens with dimensions of  $30\mathrm{mm}$  in length and  $2\mathrm{mm}$  in width were measured using a CMT4103 universal testing instrument at room temperature and a testing speed of  $10\mathrm{mm}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ . The specimens were carefully cut from high-quality samples measuring  $5\mathrm{cm} \times 5\mathrm{cm}$ , which were free of any defects such as fine bubbles. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was executed in the range of  $5 - 80^{\circ}$  at a scanning speed of  $8^{\circ}) \cdot \mathrm{min}^{-1}$  on the Bruker (D8 Advance, Karlsruhe, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation ( $\lambda = 0.154\mathrm{nm}$ ) at  $40\mathrm{kV}$ .

## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=106-108 | keywords=RheologicalProperties; family=PAA
106: 
107: PAA serves as a crucial precursor in the production of PI films, and the quality of the PAA solution has a direct impact on the final performance of the PI films [27]. Unfortunately, PAA solutions are prone to instability upon storage and may undergo undesirable changes in viscosity [28]. The degradation of PAA can be attributed to two factors: firstly, the hydrolytic degradation of PAA due to the presence of trace water in the solvent, and secondly, the reversible formation of polymeric acid that causes chain length equilibrium in PAA [29,30]. For investigation of the stability of prepared PAA solutions, viscosity, and structure tests were carried out with a PAA solution stored with increasing time. It can be seen from the FT-IR spectra (Figure 2a) that the characteristic absorption band of the amide group gradually weakened, while the  $\mathrm{C = O}$  absorption peak at  $1714~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  enhances during 30 days of storage. However, when the storage time exceeded 50 days, the characteristic peaks of the amide group changed significantly. It can be concluded that the PAA solution was still available within 30 days of storage, but may deteriorate after 50 days. Figure 2b shows the changes in the viscosity of the PAA solution with increasing storage time. The viscosity of the PAA solution gradually decreased with storage, which also predicted that the polyimide solution was gradually deteriorating. Based on the experimental results, it is important to store newly prepared PAA solutions at a low temperature  $(0 - 10^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$  and in a sealed container, as the effective storage period of PAA solutions is about 30 days. As shown in Figure 2c, the virgin PAA solution is presented as a clear yellowish liquid. With increased storage time, the water vapor in the air will inevitably penetrate the storage
108:

## excerpt 2 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=112-114 | keywords=RheologicalProperties; family=PAA
112: 
113: The thickness of the synthesized PI film was tested by DHG-050 digital height gauge. Nine random sites on the film were selected for thickness testing, and the average value was taken as the thickness of the film. Film uniformity was evaluated as the variance of thickness values measured at nine randomly selected sites. As shown in Figure 3a, when PAA with a viscosity of  $62,300~\mathrm{cPs}$  was used for spin coating, the thickness of PI films gradually decreased with the increase in rotating speed. Uniform PI films with thicknesses ranging from 2.82 to  $34.32\mu \mathrm{m}$  can be obtained by adjusting the rotating speed during spin coating. The film thickness changed obviously in the speed range of  $500 - 3000\mathrm{rpm}$ . The prepared PI film exhibited excellent thickness uniformity, with an optimal uniformity of  $0.77\%$ . In comparison, commercially available films were also characterized for thickness and uniformity, as shown in Figure 3b. Compared to the commercially available films, the films prepared by this method with similar thickness showed significantly better uniformity. In addition, several fabrications were conducted with the same process parameters at  $500 - 5000\mathrm{rpm}$  to verify the repeatability of the film-forming process. The representative results are shown in Figure 3c. Compared to the results of the control group, the repeated experiments showed only slight variations, indicating that the process has excellent controllability.
114:

## excerpt 3 | block=b0004 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=138-140 | keywords=RheologicalProperties; family=PAA
138: 
139: The mechanical properties of the prepared PI films were characterized by a tensile testing machine. Figure 5a-c shows the tensile stress-strain curves of PI films under the influence of different factors. It can be observed that the prepared PI films exhibit excellent toughness. The excellent toughness of the prepared PI films can be partially attributed to the presence of a relatively large number of ether bonds in the molecular chain, which increases the number of allowed conformations and the flexibility of individual chains. As shown in Figure 5a, the tensile strength and elongation at break of the PI film increase with increasing film thickness. When the film thickness was  $26.8\mu \mathrm{m}$ , the tensile strength and elongation at break reached  $194.71\mathrm{MPa}$  and  $130.13\%$ , respectively. Additionally, the free-standing PI film that we prepared, with a thickness of only  $2.8\mu \mathrm{m}$ , also exhibited excellent mechanical properties, with a tensile strength of  $120.96\mathrm{MPa}$  and an elongation at break of  $28.66\%$ . The viscosity of the PAA precursor affected the thickness of the PI film, and therefore we hypothesize that it also has some influence on the mechanical properties of the film. As shown in Figure 5b, the mechanical properties of films prepared by spin-coating solutions with high viscosity  $(62,300~\mathrm{cP})$  and low viscosity  $(26,000~\mathrm{cP})$  were compared. It can be concluded that the difference in solution viscosity within a certain range has a negligible influence on the tensile strength and elongation at break of the film, indicating the potential to achieve thinner and lighter PI films with excellent mechanical properties. The mechanical properties of the films were greatly influenced by the substrate used for film formation. As depicted in Figure 5c, the films deposited on  $\mathrm{Si} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$  substrates exhibited superior toughness compared to those on glass substrates, possibly due to the lower surface roughness of  $\mathrm{Si} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ . Figure 5d shows the mechanical properties of PI films prepared in different studies [14,16,35-44]. Table 1 shows the structure, thickness, and corresponding mechanical properties of the PI films. It can be seen that the elongation at break of the PI film we prepared  $(26.8\mu \mathrm{m})$  is much higher than other films, and the tensile strength is also comparable. Meanwhile, the free-standing PI film we prepared with a thickness of only  $2.8\mu \mathrm{m}$  exhibited equally impressive in both strength and toughness. In addition, a comparison was made with other PI films of the same molecular structure in other studies [24,45,46], and the mechanical properties of the PI film we prepared exhibited significant superiority. Meanwhile, PI films with only the same diamine structure were also investigated [47-49], and the results also showed that the mechanical properties of the PI films we prepared were much better than others.

chain_input.text:
[POLYMER_ANCHOR]
身份标识: doi:10.1038/srep36977_MCS2_01
名称: MCS2
别名: 
聚合物分类编码: PI/PVdF MCS
聚合物分类名称: polyimide/polyvinylidene fluoride blend composite membrane
样本形态: Membrane

[RESULT_PROPERTY_HINTS]
RheologicalProperties

[PROPERTY_METHODS]

[PROPERTY_RESULTS]
## excerpt 1 | block=b0004 | section=Results and Discussion | lines=65-77 | keywords=RheologicalProperties; family=MCS, PI
65: 
66: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)
67: 
68: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
69: Figure 3. Impedance at  $1\mathrm{kHz}$  and open circuit voltage (OCV) of prototype cells with (a) MCS1 and with (b) the PE separator in  $1\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{LiPF}_6$  -EC/PC/DEC/VC. (c) Impedance spectra as a function of temperature of the prototype cells with the MCS1. The sharp increase in the interfacial resistance  $(R_{\mathrm{int}})$  is observed at temperature between 200 and  $210^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for electrode decomposition.
70: 
71: droplets were subjected to flow through the syringe capillary during electrospinning (Figure S2). Considering the molecular weight of the polymers, Flory-Huggins interaction parameters  $(\chi)$ , component ratios, and calculated viscosities of the droplets and other parts, the PI droplets were highly stretched compared with the PVdF parts within the mixture. This led to the formation of multiple ultrafine strings and outer shells with PI and PVdF polymers, respectively, as described in the Supporting Information. Therefore, it is believed that the intrinsic immiscibility  $(\chi_{\mathrm{PI - PVdF}} = 0.37)$  of the polymers can form a novel MCS membrane using single-nozzle electrospinning.
72: 
73: The thermal shrinkage test was conducted through storage at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{hr}$  to verify the effect of the MCS structure on thermal stability. Figure 2a,b shows photos of the PE separator and of the MCS1 before and after the thermal shrinkage test. The commercial PE separator significantly shrank because the melting temperature  $(T_{\mathrm{m}})$  of the PE is  $126.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Interestingly, no noticeable dimensional changes in MCS1 was found at the temperature of  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  even though the partially melted surface of the MCS1 separator after the test was observed due to PVdF  $(T_{\mathrm{m}} = 165^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , as shown in Fig. 2c. Thus, the thermally stable PI  $(T_{\mathrm{m}} = 388^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$  multicore fibrils that aligned along the PVdF shell could provide the dimensional and porous maintenance of the MCS separator at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
74: 
75: The thermal stability, including thermally induced shrinkage or elongation of the membrane, was also investigated by measuring the high temperature melt integrity (HTMI) using thermo-mechanical analysis (TMA). In general, there are three stages of dimensional changes: a short elongation during the initial stage, a slight shrinkage during the second stage, and then, another long elongation along the loading axis (third stage). The PE separator showed a greater elongation than the MCS1 separator during the initial stage, a steep shrinkage during the second stage, and then, a drastic elongation at approximately  $140^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  when the PE melted. However, a high dimensional stability  $(\sim 300^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$  was obtained except for an insignificant elongation during the initial stage and a mild shrinkage during the second stage.
76: 
77: The conductivity cells with either MCS1 or PE separators were assembled for an ionic resistance test by sandwiching the separator between the two blocking stainless steel electrodes (SUS/separator/SUS) as a function of temperature. For the PE separator, the ionic resistivity drastically increased at approximately  $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  because the PE separator melted. This type of behavior is the thermal shutdown behavior of the PE separator, which is similar to its melting properties. However, in the case of the MCS separator, no significant increase in the ionic resistance was observed up to  $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , with the exception of two small peaks were observed at approximately 90 and  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . In our previous study, the differential scanning calorimetry curve of a separator with only PVdF contained two major peaks, which corresponded to the melting points of swollen polymers on the surface of the fiber  $(90^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$  and the partially swollen PVdF in the core region of the fiber  $(135^{\circ}\mathrm{C})^{22}$ . In contrast to a previous study, there were no significant changes in the resistance and in the observation of a second peak at a higher temperature. Therefore, it is believed that the existence of multiple PI core fibrils along the fiber positively shifted the melting temperature of the inner region of PVdF, which regulated swelling and the thermal behavior of the separator and led to the thermal stability with respect to the maintainability of the dimension and resistance at high temperatures or during severe operating conditions.

chain_input.text:
[POLYMER_ANCHOR]
身份标识: doi:10.1038/srep36977_MCS1_01
名称: MCS1
别名: 
聚合物分类编码: PI/PVdF MCS
聚合物分类名称: polyimide/polyvinylidene fluoride blend composite membrane
样本形态: Membrane

[RESULT_PROPERTY_HINTS]
ElectricalDielectricProperties

[PROPERTY_METHODS]
## excerpt 1 | block=b0003 | section=Experimental procedures | lines=30-54 | keywords=ElectricalDielectricProperties; anchors=MCS1, PI/PVdF MCS; family=PI/PVdF MCS, MCS, PI, MCS1
30: # Experimental procedures
31: 
32: Preparation of a PI/PVdF mixed solution. The polymer solution was prepared from 6.5 wt.% poly (vinylidene fluoride) (PVdF, Atofina, Kynar 761) and 6.5 wt.% polyimide (PI, Ciba Specialty, Matrimid 5218) in a mixed solvent system containing dimethylacetamide (DMAc)/tetrahydrofuran (THF) (6/4, wt. ratio). The polymer materials were dried under vacuum at  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  to eliminate any moisture and volatile impurities before the polymer solution was prepared. HPLC-grade solvents (Merck) were used in the electrospinning experiments without any further purification. The polymer materials were added to the mixed solvents, and then, the mixture was stirred at  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  to prepare the homogeneously dispersed polymer solution. The PE separator (SKC provided, South Korea) was used as the reference material.
33: 
34: Preparation and post-treatments of the PI/PVdF MCS composite membranes. The nanofibrous PI/PVdF MCS composite membrane was prepared using the single nozzle electrospinning method that was described in detail in earlier studies[21,22]. An applied voltage of  $10.5\mathrm{kV}$ , a tip-grounded collector distance of  $14\mathrm{cm}$ , a needle size of  $30\mathrm{G}$ , and a solution feeding rate of  $30\mu\mathrm{l/min}$  were the parameters used. Post-processing (including hot pressing at  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , heat stretching at  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and then post-heat treatment at  $170^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) was on the electrospun membrane to control its thickness and porosity. The membrane with a thickness of approximately  $20\mu\mathrm{m}$  was obtained after post-processing. This post-processed membrane was dried in a vacuum oven at  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  to remove any remaining solvents and was then stored in a glove box filled with argon gas  $(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} < 1\mathrm{ppm})$  prior to use.
35: 
36: Characterization of the physical properties of the PI/PVdF MCS composite separator. The surface morphology of the PI/PVdF MCS separator was measured using a field-enhanced scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi, S-4200) under vacuum. All samples were gold-coated prior to SEM measurements. The porosity and electrolyte uptake measurements of the MCS1 separator and of the PE separator were measured by soaking the separators in an electrolyte solution of  $1\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{LiPF_6}$ -EC/PC/DEC/VC (35.4/17.2/45.1/2.3, wt.%) The mechanical properties of both separators were measured using a universal testing instrument (Instron, Model 4464) with a cross-head speed of  $10\mathrm{mm / min}$  at room temperature. Each sample was cut into  $6.0\mathrm{cm}\times 0.5\mathrm{cm}$  strips, and their thicknesses were measured using a dial gauge (Mitsutoyo, BWB836) prior to the measurements. This test was performed according to the ASTM standard D882-95a. Additionally, the thermal shrinkage tests of the MCS1 and PE separators ( $3\mathrm{cm}\times 3\mathrm{cm}$ ) were conducted by storing them in an oven at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{hr}$ . The dimensional changes were evaluated by measuring the dimensions of the resulting samples.
37: 
38: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
39: Figure 1. SEM images of the electrospun PI/PVdF MCS composite nanofibrous membrane, residual PI core fibrils after solvent extraction, and the schematic of the multicore-shell structure  $(\mathbf{a},\mathbf{d},\mathbf{g})$  MCS2, PI:PVdF = 2:1 wt. ratio,  $(\mathbf{b},\mathbf{e},\mathbf{h})$  MCS1, PI:PVdF = 1:1 wt. ratio, and  $(c,f,i)$  MCS0.5, PI:PVdF = 1:2 wt. ratio. The wrinkles along the electrospun fiber become severe, and the diameter of PI core fibrils tends to increase as the PI content is increased.
40: 
41: To investigate the high temperature melt integrity (HTMI) of MCS1 and PE separators, thermo-mechanical analysis (TMA, Perkin-Elmer, TMA-7) measurements were conducted. The length change as a function of temperature was monitored in the temperature range of  $50 - 400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The samples with dimensions of  $1\mathrm{cm} \times 0.5\mathrm{cm}$  were vacuum-dried prior to use. The heating rate was  $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ , and the weight of load was  $200\mathrm{g}$ . The static force was  $50\mathrm{mN}$ .
42: 
43: Cell preparation. Two types of cells (i.e., conductivity and prototype cells) for both MCS1 and PE separators were prepared. The conductivity cells were assembled by sandwiching the separators between the two blocking stainless steel electrodes. The polymer electrolyte was prepared by soaking the separator in an electrolyte solution of  $1\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{LiPF}_6$  -EC/PC/DEC/VC (35.4/17.2/45.1/2.3 wt.%) at room temperature in a glove box filled with argon gas. The prototype cell was prepared by assembling three components, i.e., the polyelectrolyte, a mesocarbon microbead (MCMB) anode (SKC supplied, Korea) and a  $\mathrm{LiCoO_2}$  cathode (SKC supplied, Korea) in a vacuum-sealed aluminum-plastic pouch. The cell size was  $3\mathrm{cm}\times 4\mathrm{cm}$ , and its theoretical capacity was  $141\mathrm{mAh / g}$ . The entire assembling process was performed in a glove box filled with argon gas  $(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} < 1\mathrm{ppm})$ .
44: 
45: Electrochemical Characterization. A multichannel potentiostat equipped with impedance modules (BioLogic) was used to measure electrochemical impedance at  $1\mathrm{kHz}$  in the  $100\mathrm{mHz} - 1\mathrm{MHz}$  frequency range with an AC voltage of  $10\mathrm{mV}$ . The conductivity cells were heated at a rate of  $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$  from 40 to  $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . After reaching the desired temperature, a  $5\mathrm{min}$  rest period was applied before the impedance measurements were recorded every  $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
46: 
47: The prototype cells were charged to  $50\%$  SOC and then were heated at a rate of  $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$  from 40 to  $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The rest time was  $5\mathrm{min}$  for every  $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the impedance was measured from  $100\mathrm{mHz}$  to  $1\mathrm{MHz}$  for 150 seconds, along with the OCV for 10 seconds and the impedance at  $1\mathrm{kHz}$  for 10 seconds.
48: 
49: The temperature dependence of the discharge capacity of the prototype cells was investigated, as follows. The cells were charged at C/10 and then were discharged at 1C with cut-off voltages of 2.75 and  $4.2\mathrm{V}$  at intervals of  $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  in the temperature range of  $0 - 80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Additionally, the cycle performance of prototype cells was examined using a battery cycler (WBCS3000, WonATech Co.) at room temperature. The cell performance was galvanostatically evaluated with cut-off voltages of 2.75 and  $4.2\mathrm{V}$  at a 1C/1C rate.

The network and pore structures were maintained. (d) The differential HTMI of the MCS1 separator and the PE separator. The inset is the original HTMI. Note the following three stages of dimensional change: i) short elongation, ii) slight shrinkage and iii) complete elongation. 65:

67: 
68: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
69: Figure 3. Impedance at  $1\mathrm{kHz}$  and open circuit voltage (OCV) of prototype cells with (a) MCS1 and with (b) the PE separator in  $1\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{LiPF}_6$  -EC/PC/DEC/VC. (c) Impedance spectra as a function of temperature of the prototype cells with the MCS1. The sharp increase in the interfacial resistance  $(R_{\mathrm{int}})$  is observed at temperature between 200 and  $210^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for electrode decomposition.
70: 
71: droplets were subjected to flow through the syringe capillary during electrospinning (Figure S2). Considering the molecular weight of the polymers, Flory-Huggins interaction parameters  $(\chi)$ , component ratios, and calculated viscosities of the droplets and other parts, the PI droplets were highly stretched compared with the PVdF parts within the mixture. This led to the formation of multiple ultrafine strings and outer shells with PI and PVdF polymers, respectively, as described in the Supporting Information. Therefore, it is believed that the intrinsic immiscibility  $(\chi_{\mathrm{PI - PVdF}} = 0.37)$  of the polymers can form a novel MCS membrane using single-nozzle electrospinning.
72: 
73: The thermal shrinkage test was conducted through storage at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{hr}$  to verify the effect of the MCS structure on thermal stability. Figure 2a,b shows photos of the PE separator and of the MCS1 before and after the thermal shrinkage test. The commercial PE separator significantly shrank because the melting temperature  $(T_{\mathrm{m}})$  of the PE is  $126.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Interestingly, no noticeable dimensional changes in MCS1 was found at the temperature of  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  even though the partially melted surface of the MCS1 separator after the test was observed due to PVdF  $(T_{\mathrm{m}} = 165^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , as shown in Fig. 2c. Thus, the thermally stable PI  $(T_{\mathrm{m}} = 388^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$  multicore fibrils that aligned along the PVdF shell could provide the dimensional and porous maintenance of the MCS separator at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
74: 
75: The thermal stability, including thermally induced shrinkage or elongation of the membrane, was also investigated by measuring the high temperature melt integrity (HTMI) using thermo-mechanical analysis (TMA). In general, there are three stages of dimensional changes: a short elongation during the initial stage, a slight shrinkage during the second stage, and then, another long elongation along the loading axis (third stage). The PE separator showed a greater elongation than the MCS1 separator during the initial stage, a steep shrinkage during the second stage, and then, a drastic elongation at approximately  $140^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  when the PE melted. However, a high dimensional stability  $(\sim 300^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$  was obtained except for an insignificant elongation during the initial stage and a mild shrinkage during the second stage.
76: 
77: The conductivity cells with either MCS1 or PE separators were assembled for an ionic resistance test by sandwiching the separator between the two blocking stainless steel electrodes (SUS/separator/SUS) as a function of temperature. For the PE separator, the ionic resistivity drastically increased at approximately  $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  because the PE separator melted. This type of behavior is the thermal shutdown behavior of the PE separator, which is similar to its melting properties. However, in the case of the MCS separator, no significant increase in the ionic resistance was observed up to  $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , with the exception of two small peaks were observed at approximately 90 and  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . In our previous study, the differential scanning calorimetry curve of a separator with only PVdF contained two major peaks, which corresponded to the melting points of swollen polymers on the surface of the fiber  $(90^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$  and the partially swollen PVdF in the core region of the fiber  $(135^{\circ}\mathrm{C})^{22}$ . In contrast to a previous study, there were no significant changes in the resistance and in the observation of a second peak at a higher temperature. Therefore, it is believed that the existence of multiple PI core fibrils along the fiber positively shifted the melting temperature of the inner region of PVdF, which regulated swelling and the thermal behavior of the separator and led to the thermal stability with respect to the maintainability of the dimension and resistance at high temperatures or during severe operating conditions.

88: Figure 4. Temperature dependence of the discharge capacity in the prototype cells with (a) the PE separator and with (b) the MCS1 separator in  $1\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{LiPF}_6$  -EC/PC/DEC/VC. (c) C-rate performance of prototype cells with the MCS1 composite nanofibrous membrane. (d) Long-term cycle performance of the prototype cells with the PE separator and MCS1 in  $1\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{LiPF}_6$  -EC/PC/DEC/VC at a charge/discharge rate of  $1.0\mathrm{C} / 1.0\mathrm{C}$ . (e) The average capacity decay rate comparison of other PI/PVdf composite Li-ion battery separators (single core-shell, co-electrospun) and two other separators of different materials (polyaniline/polyimide composite with hierarchical 3D micro/nano-architecture, PANI/PI_3D, and partially oxidized polyacrylonitrile, Oxidized PAN). The long-term cycle performance for both single core-shell and co-electrospun was conducted at a charge/discharge rate of  $0.5\mathrm{C} / 0.5\mathrm{C}$ . The average capacity decay was calculated by dividing the difference of start and end capacities by the total cycle number.
89: 
90: The thermal behavior of open circuit voltage (OCV) and the impedance at  $1\mathrm{kHz}$  were measured in the temperature range of  $40 - 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  to investigate the thermal stability of the MCS1 and PE separator in a LIB cell with  $\mathrm{LiCoO}_2$  as the cathode and mesocarbon microbeads as the anode. Both cells were charged to the  $50\%$  state of charge for each measurement. The LIB cell with the PE separator showed lower thermal stability than the cell with the MCS1 separator. The OCV of the LIB cell with the PE separator gradually decreased at  $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and was approximately zero at  $140^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , as shown in Fig. 3a. Additionally, the AC impedance at  $1\mathrm{kHz}$  steeply increased at  $130^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . As expected from the ionic resistance test in a conductivity cell, the melting of the PE separator was responsible for the two outbreaks, which corresponded to the sharp increase in ionic resistance at approximately  $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  (Fig. 2e). In the cell with the MCS1 separator, the AC impedance sharply increased at  $220^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and OCV steeply decreased at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and reached a value of approximately  $0\mathrm{V}$  at  $\sim 220^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  (Fig. 3b). The outbreak temperatures measured in the cell were lower than those in the ionic resistance cell due to the lower thermal stability of the  $\mathrm{LiCoO}_2$  cathode. It is well known that  $\mathrm{LiCoO}_2$  in LIBs thermally decomposed through  $\mathrm{O}_2$  release at approximately
91: 
92: $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the electrolyte solution degraded the electrode surface[27-29]. Thus, this side reaction could have caused the sharp increase in the AC impedance of the cell with the MCS1 separator.
93: 
94: Figure 3c shows the impedance spectra of LIB cells with the separators as a function of temperature. The bulk resistance  $(\mathrm{R_b})$  of the MCS1 separator with the electrolyte solution was approximately constant up to  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  but slightly increased at  $210^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . However, the interfacial resistance  $(\mathrm{R_{int}})$  between the MCS1 separator and the electrode was only a few tens of ohms until  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and then, sharply increased at  $210^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Based on the fact that there was no significant increase in ionic resistance of the conductivity cell up to  $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  (Fig. 2e), an unstable interface was formed via electrode decomposition, which contributed to the sharp increase in the AC impedance at  $1\mathrm{kHz}$  and to the steep drop in the OCV (Fig. 3b). These results confirmed that the MCS1 separator had a high thermal stability in the LIB at temperatures greater than  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Additionally, the thermal stability in the LIB cell was lower than in the conductivity cell. This was attributed to electrode decomposition and/or electrolyte solution degradation at the interface between the electrode and polymer electrolytes. The thermal failure of the MCS1 separator was not a factor.
95: 
96: The discharge capacities of LIB cells with both the MCS1 separator and the PE separator in  $1\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{LiPF}_6$  -EC/PC/ DEC/VC (35.4/17.2/45.1/2.3, wt.%) were measured in the  $20 - 80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  temperature region. In Fig. 4a,b, higher discharge capacities were found in the cell with MCS1 separator than the cell with the PE separator in the operating temperature range. A higher electrolyte uptake of  $427\%$  was found for the MCS1 separator compared with the value of  $203\%$  for the PE separator. This difference was due to the interaction between the PVdF component on the surface and the solvent molecules of the electrolyte solution[18], as summarized in Table S1. Considering the ionic resistance and electrolyte uptake, the control of the structure and morphology of the MCS1 separator affects the LIB performance. However, the difference in the discharge capacities becomes indefinite at the high temperature region because the cell temperature increase is due to the external heat source. The c-rate performance test on the two cells for 5 cycles at seven different c-rates was performed (Fig. 4c). The prototype cell with the MCS1 separator always had a higher capacity for all c-rates compared with the PE separator cell. Additionally, the capacity difference became more significant at high charge rates. The capacity difference between the cells gradually began to increase at 1C and was clearly observed at the 10C charge/discharge rate. The enormous differences were responsible for the internal heat that was generated within the cell. The internal heat generation became significant at high discharge rates due to the ohmic drop and polarization effects[29-31]. Therefore, the MCS1 separator possessed superior performance compared with the PE separator in terms of the internal and external thermal management.

## excerpt 1 | block=b0003 | section=Experimental procedures | lines=30-54 | keywords=ElectricalDielectricProperties; anchors=PE separator; family=PE, PE separator
30: # Experimental procedures
31: 
32: Preparation of a PI/PVdF mixed solution. The polymer solution was prepared from 6.5 wt.% poly (vinylidene fluoride) (PVdF, Atofina, Kynar 761) and 6.5 wt.% polyimide (PI, Ciba Specialty, Matrimid 5218) in a mixed solvent system containing dimethylacetamide (DMAc)/tetrahydrofuran (THF) (6/4, wt. ratio). The polymer materials were dried under vacuum at  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  to eliminate any moisture and volatile impurities before the polymer solution was prepared. HPLC-grade solvents (Merck) were used in the electrospinning experiments without any further purification. The polymer materials were added to the mixed solvents, and then, the mixture was stirred at  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  to prepare the homogeneously dispersed polymer solution. The PE separator (SKC provided, South Korea) was used as the reference material.
33: 
34: Preparation and post-treatments of the PI/PVdF MCS composite membranes. The nanofibrous PI/PVdF MCS composite membrane was prepared using the single nozzle electrospinning method that was described in detail in earlier studies[21,22]. An applied voltage of  $10.5\mathrm{kV}$ , a tip-grounded collector distance of  $14\mathrm{cm}$ , a needle size of  $30\mathrm{G}$ , and a solution feeding rate of  $30\mu\mathrm{l/min}$  were the parameters used. Post-processing (including hot pressing at  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , heat stretching at  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and then post-heat treatment at  $170^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) was on the electrospun membrane to control its thickness and porosity. The membrane with a thickness of approximately  $20\mu\mathrm{m}$  was obtained after post-processing. This post-processed membrane was dried in a vacuum oven at  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  to remove any remaining solvents and was then stored in a glove box filled with argon gas  $(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} < 1\mathrm{ppm})$  prior to use.
35: 
36: Characterization of the physical properties of the PI/PVdF MCS composite separator. The surface morphology of the PI/PVdF MCS separator was measured using a field-enhanced scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi, S-4200) under vacuum. All samples were gold-coated prior to SEM measurements. The porosity and electrolyte uptake measurements of the MCS1 separator and of the PE separator were measured by soaking the separators in an electrolyte solution of  $1\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{LiPF_6}$ -EC/PC/DEC/VC (35.4/17.2/45.1/2.3, wt.%) The mechanical properties of both separators were measured using a universal testing instrument (Instron, Model 4464) with a cross-head speed of  $10\mathrm{mm / min}$  at room temperature. Each sample was cut into  $6.0\mathrm{cm}\times 0.5\mathrm{cm}$  strips, and their thicknesses were measured using a dial gauge (Mitsutoyo, BWB836) prior to the measurements. This test was performed according to the ASTM standard D882-95a. Additionally, the thermal shrinkage tests of the MCS1 and PE separators ( $3\mathrm{cm}\times 3\mathrm{cm}$ ) were conducted by storing them in an oven at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{hr}$ . The dimensional changes were evaluated by measuring the dimensions of the resulting samples.

The wrinkles along the electrospun fiber become severe, and the diameter of PI core fibrils tends to increase as the PI content is increased.

The length change as a function of temperature was monitored in the temperature range of  $50 - 400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The samples with dimensions of  $1\mathrm{cm} \times 0.5\mathrm{cm}$  were vacuum-dried prior to use. The heating rate was  $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ , and the weight of load was  $200\mathrm{g}$ . The static force was  $50\mathrm{mN}$ .

The prototype cell was prepared by assembling three components, i.e., the polyelectrolyte, a mesocarbon microbead (MCMB) anode (SKC supplied, Korea) and a  $\mathrm{LiCoO_2}$  cathode (SKC supplied, Korea) in a vacuum-sealed aluminum-plastic pouch. The cell size was  $3\mathrm{cm}\times 4\mathrm{cm}$ , and its theoretical capacity was  $141\mathrm{mAh / g}$ . 90: The thermal behavior of open circuit voltage (OCV) and the impedance at  $1\mathrm{kHz}$  were measured in the temperature range of  $40 - 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  to investigate the thermal stability of the MCS1 and PE separator in a LIB cell with  $\mathrm{LiCoO}_2$  as the cathode and mesocarbon microbeads as the anode. Both cells were charged to the  $50\%$  state of charge for each measurement. The LIB cell with the PE separator showed lower thermal stability than the cell with the MCS1 separator. The OCV of the LIB cell with the PE separator gradually decreased at  $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and was approximately zero at  $140^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , as shown in Fig. 3a. Additionally, the AC impedance at  $1\mathrm{kHz}$  steeply increased at  $130^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . As expected from the ionic resistance test in a conductivity cell, the melting of the PE separator was responsible for the two outbreaks, which corresponded to the sharp increase in ionic resistance at approximately  $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  (Fig. 2e). In the cell with the MCS1 separator, the AC impedance sharply increased at  $220^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and OCV steeply decreased at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and reached a value of approximately  $0\mathrm{V}$  at  $\sim 220^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  (Fig. 3b). The outbreak temperatures measured in the cell were lower than those in the ionic resistance cell due to the lower thermal stability of the  $\mathrm{LiCoO}_2$  cathode. Thermal conductivity measurements were carried out at different temperatures (from room temperature to $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) to detect the heat insulation performance of the specimen using a heat flux method (Netzsch, HFM436 Lambda). Results and Discussions | lines=193-195 | keywords=PhysicalProperties, ThermalProperties; anchors=Polyimide aerogel; family=PI, Polyimide aerogel, PPDA 193: 194: The thermal conductivities $(\lambda)$ of composite samples at different temperatures (from 100 to $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) were determined using a periodic heat flux method (see Figure 8). Heat transport through a porous solid can occur through a series of radiative, gaseous, and solid conduction processes.[36] Radiative thermal conductivities are likely to be negligible at room temperature. Heat conductivity in solids is determined by the density and conducting properties of the material, while gaseous conductivity depends on the porosity and pore size of the material. Different formulations in polyimide aerogels have an obvious influence on the thermal conductivities of the PI composites. Thermal conductivity for impregnated GF/PI composites at room temperature increases from 0.023 to $0.029\mathrm{Wm}^{-1}\mathrm{K}^{-1}$ as their PPDA content increases. Conversely, Figure S7, Supporting Information, shows that the introduction of fiber does not significantly improve the thermal conductivity of aerogels. Conversely, when the content of PPDA in aerogels is high (e.g., PI-PPDA-75 and PI-PPDA-100), the as-prepared composites have a lower thermal conductivity than the corresponding pure aerogel. This is because the pore sizes of the composites are smaller than the mean free paths of oxygen and nitrogen $(\approx 70~\mathrm{nm})$ in air, which can significantly decrease the effective thermal conductivity in the gaseous phase. Results and Discussions | lines=85-97 | keywords=PhysicalProperties, ProcessingProperties; family=PI 85: 86: Figure S1, Supporting Information, shows a picture of typical aerogels prepared using different molar ratios of ODA and PPDA in cylindrical molds of the same size. These PI aerogels differ in color depending on their formulation, with sample colors changing from light yellow to orange yellow to dark yellow as the amount of PPDA present increases to $100\%$ . The change in color reflects the different colors and densities of the parent diamines, with PPDA having an intense brown color and a high density, while ODA is a white crystalline solid with a relatively low density. Heating these aerogels to $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ results in all the aerogels darkening and shrinking to much smaller volumes, as shown in Figure S1, Supporting Information, thus demonstrating the 87: 88: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 89: Figure 1. Schematic illustration of the synthesizing process. 90: 91: In view of the severe shrinkage, the physical properties of these carbonized aerogels are not analyzed further. The density and volume shrinkage of the aerogel samples are shown in Table 1. Supercritical drying effectively prevented contraction of the aerogels during the initial drying phases, however, some shrinkage did occur during the gelation and solvent exchange phases. Introduction of PPDA into the polymer backbone is expected to result in the aerogels exhibiting reduced shrinkage, as the PPDA would be expected to make the polymer backbone more rigid. However, this proved not to be the case, with a PI aerogel prepared with ODA shrunk by $16.5\%$ during processing, while an aerogel containing $100\%$ PPDA shrunk by as much as $57.9\%$ . It is possible that noncoplanarity may induce less shrinkage during gelation of pure ODA oligomers because fewer interactions occur between polymer chains. This contrasts with the structures of oligomers obtained from PPDA which exhibit a greater degree of planarity, which leads to more contraction during processing. 92: 93: Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired to identify the chemical structures of the aerogels (see Figure 2) whose spectra reveal the presence of characteristic bands for polyimides at $1370~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ ( $\upsilon$ imide C-N), $1715~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ (symmetric $\upsilon$ imide C=O) and $1775~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ (asymmetric $\upsilon$ imide C=O). A peak at $1499~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ was assigned to the stretching vibration of the amide $(-CONH-)$ with no band found to be present at $1860~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ that would be expected if any unreacted anhydride is present. 94: 95: The pore structure of PI aerogels containing different molar ratios of diamines were investigated by examining their SEM images, nitrogen sorption isotherms, and BJH pore size distributions (see Figure 3). The SEM images (see Figure 3a-d) of the microstructure of the aerogels prepared using different 96: 97: Table 1. Textural characteristics of as-prepared pure aerogels. ## excerpt 2 | block=b0004 | section=3. 141: 142: a)BET specific surface area; b)Total pore volume at $P / P_0 = 0.995$ ; c)Average pore diameter. 143: 144: less shrinkage, with the densities of these aerogel composites ranging from 0.143 to $0.177\mathrm{gcm}^{-3}$ , as shown in Table 2. The density of the GF/PI-100 composite is significantly lower than that of pure PI aerogel, despite the incorporation of glass fiber. The proposed molecular structure of the composites is shown in Figure S4, Supporting Information, which is similar to the structure of pure aerogels, thus indicating that the fiber has little effect on the gross chemical structure of these PI aerogels. 145: 146: Figure 5a-d shows the microstructure of GF/PI, which reveals that all of the porous glass fiber networks are effectively filled with PI aerogels, thus indicating that PI aerogels are easily impregnated into fibrous porous glass fiber to form fiber/aerogel composites. The fiber felt acts as a skeletal support to shape and reduce shrinkage of the aerogel during the preparation process, which results in improved mechanical strength. These evenly distributed aerogels effectively reduce the pore size of the fiber mats, thereby improving the thermal insulation properties of the composites. The pure aerogels formed in the composites exhibit randomly interconnected 3D porous networks composed of nanometer-sized strands, or spheroidal particles as pure aerogels (see Figure 3). TGA curves for the composites under nitrogen and air atmospheres are shown in Figure S5, Supporting Information, with the excellent 147: 148: thermal stability of the composites due to the heat-resistance of the glass fibers and the PI aerogels. Figure 5d,e shows the adsorption-desorption curves and pore size distributions of the composites. It could be seen that the composites exhibit lower specific surface areas and smaller pore volumes. This is because the glass fibers exhibit lower contact angles (Figure S6, Supporting Information) and larger surface energy (Table S1, Supporting Information) as well as rough surface facilitates the preferential growth of the aerogel, with pores between the fibers limiting the continued aggregation of aerogels, leading to formation of discontinuous particles. 149: 150: Figure 6a-d shows the pyrolyzed morphologies of the aerogel composites after treatment at $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . As shown in Figure S5, Supporting Information, little weight loss for aerogel composites occurs at $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in a nitrogen atmosphere, suggesting that short-term use at this temperature is possible. However, when heated, pyrolysis of the aerogel results in significant shrinkage which creates voids within a less dense aerogel matrix. The glass fiber helps maintain the 3D network of the composite by binding the residual aerogels together, which explains the lower shrinkage levels when these impregnated composites are heated at high temperatures. The inset shown in Figure 6a-d shows the magnified SEM images of a PI aerogel treated at high temperature, which reveal the presence of grape-like aggregates of primary PI nanoparticles which are progressively converted into thread-like structures. The structures of these composites treated at high temperature are dominated by the presence of macrospores between fibers that are formed during the pyrolysis process, which results from pyrolysis and contraction of the PI aerogels. 151: 152: The mechanical behavior of the GF/PI composites was examined by carrying out compression tests along the $z$ -direction, with the effect of fiber enhancement on compression curves for pure PI aerogels and CGF/PI as shown in Figure 7a,c. Young's modulus of these samples were measured from the initial slopes of the stress-strain curves, which are plotted in 153: 154: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) 155: 156: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ## excerpt 3 | block=b0004 | section=3. The mechanical properties of these PI aerogels are dependent on the amount of PPDA present. Stress increases linearly to maximum elastic strain levels of around $5\%$ , with further increases in strain from $5 - 40\%$ during the yield stage resulting in limited augmentation of compression stress that leads to strain levels exceeding $50\%$ . This behavior is characteristic of an initial elastic stage being formed through elastic compression of an aerogel networks, followed by an irreversible yield stage that is followed by a final densification stage that results in permanent collapse of the aerogels and separation of fibers from the aerogel matrix. Thermal conductivity $(\lambda)$ values of as-prepared composites at different temperatures from room temperature to $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . 191: 192: shown in Figure 7d, reveals that the compression performance of these composites is improved dramatically in the presence of glass fiber and increasing amounts of PPDA. For example, compared with PI aerogels derived from $100\%$ ODA that gives a Young's modulus of $3.6\mathrm{MPa}$ , PI aerogels containing $100\%$ PPDA show an order of magnitude growth of $42.7\mathrm{MPa}$ . Furthermore, when impregnated with glass fiber, the Young's modulus of GF/PI-100 increased significantly to $113.5\mathrm{MPa}$ . The Young's modulus of GF/PI-100 is the highest value reported for PI aerogels to date, when compared on a same-density basis (Table S2, Supporting Information), with this increase in mechanical strength assigned to the synergistic effect of the aerogels and fibers. Aerogels with high surface area fill in the pores of the GF resulting in more effective dispersion of external stress forces, with fibers acting as a support framework to dissipate fracture energy during the fibers/aerogel stripping process. 193: 194: The thermal conductivities $(\lambda)$ of composite samples at different temperatures (from 100 to $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) were determined using a periodic heat flux method (see Figure 8). Heat transport through a porous solid can occur through a series of radiative, gaseous, and solid conduction processes.[36] Radiative thermal conductivities are likely to be negligible at room temperature. Heat conductivity in solids is determined by the density and conducting properties of the material, while gaseous conductivity depends on the porosity and pore size of the material. Different formulations in polyimide aerogels have an obvious influence on the thermal conductivities of the PI composites. Thermal conductivity for impregnated GF/PI composites at room temperature increases from 0.023 to $0.029\mathrm{Wm}^{-1}\mathrm{K}^{-1}$ as their PPDA content increases. Conversely, when the content of PPDA in aerogels is high (e.g., PI-PPDA-75 and PI-PPDA-100), the as-prepared composites have a lower thermal conductivity than the corresponding pure aerogel. This is because the pore sizes of the composites are smaller than the mean free paths of oxygen and nitrogen $(\approx 70~\mathrm{nm})$ in air, which can significantly decrease the effective thermal conductivity in the gaseous phase. Alternatively, the 195: 196: presence of aerogels can considerably enhance grain boundary scattering, resulting in a decrease in the solid thermal conductivity of the composite. Increasing temperature results in the PI aerogels turning black, with their thermal conductivity at $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ $(0.082\mathrm{Wm}^{-1}\mathrm{K}^{-1})$ significantly greater than that of at room temperature $(0.057\mathrm{Wm}^{-1}\mathrm{K}^{-1})$ . However, both these thermal conductivity values are relatively low, indicating that these as-prepared composites exhibit good insulating properties.

This is because the pore sizes of the composites are smaller than the mean free paths of oxygen and nitrogen $(\approx 70~\mathrm{nm})$ in air, which can significantly decrease the effective thermal conductivity in the gaseous phase. Furthermore, when impregnated with glass fiber, the Young's modulus of GF/PI-100 increased significantly to $113.5\mathrm{MPa}$ . The Young's modulus of GF/PI-100 is the highest value reported for PI aerogels to date, when compared on a same-density basis (Table S2, Supporting Information), with this increase in mechanical strength assigned to the synergistic effect of the aerogels and fibers. Heat transport through a porous solid can occur through a series of radiative, gaseous, and solid conduction processes.[36] Radiative thermal conductivities are likely to be negligible at room temperature. Heat conductivity in solids is determined by the density and conducting properties of the material, while gaseous conductivity depends on the porosity and pore size of the material. Different formulations in polyimide aerogels have an obvious influence on the thermal conductivities of the PI composites. Thermal conductivity for impregnated GF/PI composites at room temperature increases from 0.023 to $0.029\mathrm{Wm}^{-1}\mathrm{K}^{-1}$ as their PPDA content increases. Conversely, Figure S7, Supporting Information, shows that the introduction of fiber does not significantly improve the thermal conductivity of aerogels. Conversely, when the content of PPDA in aerogels is high (e.g., PI-PPDA-75 and PI-PPDA-100), the as-prepared composites have a lower thermal conductivity than the corresponding pure aerogel. This is because the pore sizes of the composites are smaller than the mean free paths of oxygen and nitrogen $(\approx 70~\mathrm{nm})$ in air, which can significantly decrease the effective thermal conductivity in the gaseous phase. Alternatively, the 195: 196: presence of aerogels can considerably enhance grain boundary scattering, resulting in a decrease in the solid thermal conductivity of the composite. Increasing temperature results in the PI aerogels turning black, with their thermal conductivity at $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ $(0.082\mathrm{Wm}^{-1}\mathrm{K}^{-1})$ significantly greater than that of at room temperature $(0.057\mathrm{Wm}^{-1}\mathrm{K}^{-1})$ . However, both these thermal conductivity values are relatively low, indicating that these as-prepared composites exhibit good insulating properties.

Introduction of PPDA into the polymer backbone is expected to result in the aerogels exhibiting reduced shrinkage, as the PPDA would be expected to make the polymer backbone more rigid. However, this proved not to be the case, with a PI aerogel prepared with ODA shrunk by $16.5\%$ during processing, while an aerogel containing $100\%$ PPDA shrunk by as much as $57.9\%$ .
