chain_input.text:
[POLYMER_ANCHOR] 身份标识: doi:10.3390/nano12162745_PI-1_01 名称: PI-1 别名: 聚合物分类编码: PI 聚合物分类名称: preimidized negative auto-photosensitive polyimide homopolymer resin 样本形态: Solid [RESULT_PROPERTY_HINTS] OpticalProperties, ProcessingProperties, RheologicalProperties, ThermalProperties [PROPERTY_METHODS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0005 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=64-77 | keywords=OpticalProperties, RheologicalProperties; family=PI 64: # 2.2. Measurements 65: 66: Inherent viscosities of the PI resins were measured using an Ubbelohde viscometer (Pingxuan Scientific Instrument Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) with a $0.5\mathrm{g}\mathrm{dL}^{-1}$ NMP solution at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Absolute viscosity of the PI solutions was measured using a DV-II+ Pro viscometer (Brookfield, Ametek, MA, USA) at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The number average molecular weight $(M_{n})$ and weight average molecular weight $(M_w)$ of the PI resins were measured using a gel permeation chromatography (GPC) system (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The solubility of the PI resins was investigated by mixing $1.0\mathrm{g}$ of the PI resins and $9.0\mathrm{g}$ of the solvent tested $(10\mathrm{wt}\%$ solid content) and then stirred for $24\mathrm{h}$ at room temperature. The solubility was determined visually as three grades: completely soluble $(++)$ , partially soluble $(+)$ , and insoluble $(-)$ , wherein complete soluble indicates a homogenous and clean state without phase separation, precipitation or gel formation, and insoluble indicates no change in the appearance of the resin. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra of the PI NFMs were recorded on a Iraffinity-1S FT-IR spectrometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and the derivative TGA (DTG) of the PI NFMs were performed on a Q50 thermal analysis system (New Castle, Delaware, USA) at a heating rate of $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ in nitrogen. Thermo-mechanical analysis (TMA) of the PIs was recorded on a TMA402F3 thermal analysis system (NETZSCH, Selb, Germany) by using films instead of fibrous membranes. The PI films with a thickness of $25\pm 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ were prepared according to our previous work [25]. The thermal scanning mode ranges from 50 to $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at a heating rate of $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ in nitrogen atmosphere. The coefficients of linear thermal expansion (CTE) values of composite films were recorded in the range of $50 - 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectra of the PI NFMs were recorded on a Hitachi U-3210 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the PI NFMs was conducted on a Rigaku D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Tokyo, Japan). The micro-morphologies of the PI patterns were detected via a JSM-6700F (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) 67: 68: field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). The CIE (Comission Internationale del' Eclairage, Vienna, Austria) Lab optical parameters of the PI NFMs were measured using an X-rite color i7 spectrophotometer (Michigan, USA). For the color parameters, $L^{*}$ is the lightness, where 100 means white and 0 implies black. A positive $a^{*}$ means a red color, and a negative one indicates a green color. A positive $b^{*}$ means a yellow color, and a negative one indicates a blue color. The whiteness values of the PI NFMs were calculated as Equation (1), where WI standards for whiteness index, $L^{*}$ standards for the index of lightness, $a^{*}$ and $b^{*}$ stand for chromaticity coefficient. 69: 70: $$ 71: \mathrm {W I} = 1 0 0 - \left[ \left(1 0 0 - L ^ {*}\right) ^ {2} + a ^ {* 2} + b ^ {* 2} \right] ^ {1 / 2} \tag {1} 72: $$ 73: 74: Effects of the structural modification on the photocrosslinking behaviors of the PIs were evaluated as follows. The tested PI solution with a solid content of $30\mathrm{wt}\%$ was spin-coated onto potassium bromide (KBr) sheets $(1.0\mathrm{cm} \times 1.0\mathrm{cm} \times 2\mathrm{mm})$ at $700\mathrm{rpm}$ for $30\mathrm{s}$ using a spin-coater (MS-B100, Mikasa Co. Ltd., Hiroshima, Japan). Then, the KBr sheets were prebaked at $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $0.5\mathrm{h}$ on a digital hotplate (NA-1A, As-One Co., Ltd., Osaka, Japan). The thickness of the obtained PI coatings was $5 \pm 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ . The KBr sheets were exposed with a UV light source (IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China, power density: $400\mathrm{mW/cm}^2$ ). The distance between the UV source and the PI samples was $1.0 \pm 0.1\mathrm{cm}$ . The UV exposure time was set to be $0.5, 1.0, 2.0$ and $3.0\mathrm{min}$ . After exposure, the ATR-FTIR spectra of the PI samples were detected. 75: 76: The photolithographic patterning procedures of the PI NFMs were investigated as follows. The PI NFM with a thickness of $20.0 \pm 1.0 \mu \mathrm{m}$ on the aluminum substrate was covered with a photomask containing the "CUGB" pattern together with the corresponding numbers (line width with the unit of a micrometer). Then, the PI NMF/mask system was exposed for 60 s with a high-pressure mercury lamp equipped with a UV-LED system (Model: IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China) with the main ultraviolet wavelength of i-line (365 nm), g-line (405 nm), and h-line (436 nm). Then, the exposed PI NFM was developed in DMF for 5 s, after which the sample was immersed in butyl acetate for 10 s. At last, the PI NFM was baked at $120^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 60 min. The photolithography results were observed by SEM measurement. The thickness of the pattern was measured with SEM measurement or Dektak XTL Stylus Profilometer (Tucson, AZ, USA). 77: ## excerpt 2 | block=b0005 | section=2. 97: ## excerpt 3 | block=b0005 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=98-102 | keywords=OpticalProperties, RheologicalProperties; family=PI 98: # 2.4. Fabrication of PI NFMs via Electrospinning 99: 100: The PI and SPI NFMs were fabricated via the standard electrospinning procedure by using the PI solutions as the starting materials. For comparison, the same solid content of $30\mathrm{wt}\%$ was used in the current work so as to maintain the similar thickness of the derived NFMs as accurately as possible. The electrospinning parameters, including the voltage $(15\mathrm{kV})$ , the inner diameters of the spinnerets $(0.21\mathrm{mm})$ , the distance between the spinneret to the alumina foil collector $(15\mathrm{cm})$ , the speed of the rotating drum collector $(1000\mathrm{rpm})$ , and the temperature $(20\pm 2^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ and relative humidity $(30\pm 5\%)$ , were also controlled to be the same. The detailed electrospinning fabrication could be illustrated by the preparation of SPI-4 NFM. The dried SPI-4 resin obtained in Section 2.3 was dissolved in DMAc at room temperature with the solid content of $30\mathrm{wt}\%$ . A yellow solution with the absolute viscosity of $3377\mathrm{mPa}$ s was then obtained. The SPI-4 solution was filled into a $5\mathrm{mL}$ syringe and squeezed out through a spinneret by a syringe pump at a speed of $0.2\mathrm{mL/h}$ . The voltage was applied between the syringe and the rolling drum collector. Ultrathin SPI-4 fibers or beads deposited on the aluminum foil substrates. After the electrospinning, the free-standing SPI-4 NFM was obtained by being peeled from the aluminum substrate and then dried in an air-circulated oven at $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $1\mathrm{h}$ to remove the absorbed moisture and the residual solvent. 101: 102: The other NFMs were prepared according to a similar procedure. The free-standing PI NFMs were used to test the FTIR, thermal properties, optical reflectance, and CIE Lab parameters. The PI NFMs on the aluminum substrates were used to evaluate the photolithography patterning properties. [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=107-109 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=PI-1; family=PI, PI-1 107: 108: The standard PI-1 (BTDA-TMMDA) represents a type of auto-sensitive negative PSPI developed in the 1980s, which is intrinsically sensitive to the emitting wavelength of a high-pressure mercury lamp $(365\sim 436\mathrm{nm})$ without any additional photoactive compounds [26]. In such PSPI, the benzophenone units in the BTDA moiety will be excited by the UV exposure to form the triplet, which will capture the hydrogen atoms in the methyl substituents ortho to the amino groups in the TMMDA moiety. This will lead to the intra- and intermolecular photocrosslinking reactions, making the UV-exposed parts insoluble in the developer (DMF or CPA) [27]. Thus, negative patterns to the photomask will be obtained. In view of the several intrinsic defects of the pristine PI (BTDA-TMMDA) system, such as the relatively low photo-efficiency, low optical transmittance to the UV light sources, the high coefficient of linear thermal expansion (CTE), and so on, various modifications via incorporation of the third or more monomers have been reported in the past decades [28,29]. Most of the above modification works are to improve the comprehensive properties of the 109: ## excerpt 2 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=116-118 | keywords=RheologicalProperties; anchors=PI-1; family=PI, PI-1 116: 117: First, the inherent viscosities $([\eta]_{\mathrm{inh}})$ and molecular weights of the PI and SPI resins were detected, and the results are shown in Table 2. The copolymer PI (SPI-2~SPI-6) resins showed the $(\eta)_{\mathrm{inh}}$ values in the range of $0.47\sim 0.78\mathrm{dLg}^{-1}$ , the number of average molecular weights $(M_n)$ in the range of $(2.87\sim 4.70)\times 10^{4}\mathrm{g mol}^{-1}$ , and the weight average molecular weights $(M_w)$ in the range of $(5.13\sim 8.83)\times 10^{4}\mathrm{g mol}^{-1}$ , respectively. These values are all correspondingly lower than those of the pristine PI-1 (BTDA-TMMDA) system $((\eta)_{\mathrm{inh}} = 1.00\mathrm{dLg}^{-1}, M_n = 5.06\times 10^4\mathrm{g mol}^{-1})$ , indicating that incorporation of the TSN monomer decreased the molecular weights of the polymers. This might be due to the decreased reactivity of the TSN diamine due to the existence of the electron-withdrawing sulfone groups. Nevertheless, the current co-polyimides possessed acceptable molecular weights for the following NFM fabrications. 118: ## excerpt 3 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=179-181 | keywords=ProcessingProperties; anchors=PI-1; family=PI, PI-1 179: 180: The thermal stability of the PI NFMs was then evaluated by TGA measurements, and the results are listed in Table 3. It can be clearly detected from the TGA and derivative TGA (DTG) plots of the SPI NFMs shown in Figure 8 that all the polymers showed good stability before $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , after which all the SPI NFMs gradually decomposed and revealed the $5\%$ weight loss temperatures $(T_{5\%})$ in the range of $507.6\sim 527.8^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the $10\%$ weight loss temperatures $(T_{10\%})$ in the range of $558.3\sim 568.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the residual weight ratios at $750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ $(R_{\mathrm{w750}})$ of $64.0\sim 67.0\%$ . All the thermal data of the SPI NFMs were a bit lower than those of the pristine PI-1 NFM when comparing the corresponding thermal data shown in Table 3. This is mainly due to the relatively low thermal stability of sulfone groups at elevated temperatures [31]. The SPI-6 NFM with the highest sulfone contents in the molecular structure showed the lowest thermal data among the polymers. In addition, according to the DTG plots, all the SPI NFMs showed two-stage thermal decomposition behaviors during the test. The first stage occurred around $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which might be due to the decomposition of methyl side chains or sulfone units, and the second stage was recorded around $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which could be ascribed to the decomposition of the main chains of the PIs. 181: ## excerpt 4 | block=b0006 | section=3. First, the UV-Vis reflectance spectra of the SPI NFMs are shown in Figure 10. [POLYMER_ANCHOR] 身份标识: doi:10.3390/nano12162745_SPI-6_01 名称: SPI-6 别名: 聚合物分类编码: SPI 聚合物分类名称: preimidized negative auto-photosensitive polyimide copolymer resin 样本形态: Solid [RESULT_PROPERTY_HINTS] ProcessingProperties, RheologicalProperties, ThermalProperties [PROPERTY_METHODS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0005 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=64-77 | keywords=RheologicalProperties 64: # 2.2. Measurements 65: 66: Inherent viscosities of the PI resins were measured using an Ubbelohde viscometer (Pingxuan Scientific Instrument Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) with a $0.5\mathrm{g}\mathrm{dL}^{-1}$ NMP solution at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Absolute viscosity of the PI solutions was measured using a DV-II+ Pro viscometer (Brookfield, Ametek, MA, USA) at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The number average molecular weight $(M_{n})$ and weight average molecular weight $(M_w)$ of the PI resins were measured using a gel permeation chromatography (GPC) system (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The solubility of the PI resins was investigated by mixing $1.0\mathrm{g}$ of the PI resins and $9.0\mathrm{g}$ of the solvent tested $(10\mathrm{wt}\%$ solid content) and then stirred for $24\mathrm{h}$ at room temperature. The solubility was determined visually as three grades: completely soluble $(++)$ , partially soluble $(+)$ , and insoluble $(-)$ , wherein complete soluble indicates a homogenous and clean state without phase separation, precipitation or gel formation, and insoluble indicates no change in the appearance of the resin. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra of the PI NFMs were recorded on a Iraffinity-1S FT-IR spectrometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and the derivative TGA (DTG) of the PI NFMs were performed on a Q50 thermal analysis system (New Castle, Delaware, USA) at a heating rate of $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ in nitrogen. Thermo-mechanical analysis (TMA) of the PIs was recorded on a TMA402F3 thermal analysis system (NETZSCH, Selb, Germany) by using films instead of fibrous membranes. The PI films with a thickness of $25\pm 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ were prepared according to our previous work [25]. The thermal scanning mode ranges from 50 to $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at a heating rate of $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ in nitrogen atmosphere. The coefficients of linear thermal expansion (CTE) values of composite films were recorded in the range of $50 - 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectra of the PI NFMs were recorded on a Hitachi U-3210 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the PI NFMs was conducted on a Rigaku D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Tokyo, Japan). The micro-morphologies of the PI patterns were detected via a JSM-6700F (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) 67: 68: field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). The CIE (Comission Internationale del' Eclairage, Vienna, Austria) Lab optical parameters of the PI NFMs were measured using an X-rite color i7 spectrophotometer (Michigan, USA). For the color parameters, $L^{*}$ is the lightness, where 100 means white and 0 implies black. A positive $a^{*}$ means a red color, and a negative one indicates a green color. A positive $b^{*}$ means a yellow color, and a negative one indicates a blue color. The whiteness values of the PI NFMs were calculated as Equation (1), where WI standards for whiteness index, $L^{*}$ standards for the index of lightness, $a^{*}$ and $b^{*}$ stand for chromaticity coefficient. 69: 70: $$ 71: \mathrm {W I} = 1 0 0 - \left[ \left(1 0 0 - L ^ {*}\right) ^ {2} + a ^ {* 2} + b ^ {* 2} \right] ^ {1 / 2} \tag {1} 72: $$ 73: 74: Effects of the structural modification on the photocrosslinking behaviors of the PIs were evaluated as follows. The tested PI solution with a solid content of $30\mathrm{wt}\%$ was spin-coated onto potassium bromide (KBr) sheets $(1.0\mathrm{cm} \times 1.0\mathrm{cm} \times 2\mathrm{mm})$ at $700\mathrm{rpm}$ for $30\mathrm{s}$ using a spin-coater (MS-B100, Mikasa Co. Ltd., Hiroshima, Japan). Then, the KBr sheets were prebaked at $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $0.5\mathrm{h}$ on a digital hotplate (NA-1A, As-One Co., Ltd., Osaka, Japan). The thickness of the obtained PI coatings was $5 \pm 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ . The KBr sheets were exposed with a UV light source (IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China, power density: $400\mathrm{mW/cm}^2$ ). The distance between the UV source and the PI samples was $1.0 \pm 0.1\mathrm{cm}$ . The UV exposure time was set to be $0.5, 1.0, 2.0$ and $3.0\mathrm{min}$ . After exposure, the ATR-FTIR spectra of the PI samples were detected. 75: 76: The photolithographic patterning procedures of the PI NFMs were investigated as follows. The PI NFM with a thickness of $20.0 \pm 1.0 \mu \mathrm{m}$ on the aluminum substrate was covered with a photomask containing the "CUGB" pattern together with the corresponding numbers (line width with the unit of a micrometer). Then, the PI NMF/mask system was exposed for 60 s with a high-pressure mercury lamp equipped with a UV-LED system (Model: IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China) with the main ultraviolet wavelength of i-line (365 nm), g-line (405 nm), and h-line (436 nm). Then, the exposed PI NFM was developed in DMF for 5 s, after which the sample was immersed in butyl acetate for 10 s. At last, the PI NFM was baked at $120^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 60 min. The photolithography results were observed by SEM measurement. The thickness of the pattern was measured with SEM measurement or Dektak XTL Stylus Profilometer (Tucson, AZ, USA). 77: ## excerpt 2 | block=b0005 | section=2. 97: ## excerpt 3 | block=b0005 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=98-102 | keywords=RheologicalProperties; family=SPI 98: # 2.4. Fabrication of PI NFMs via Electrospinning 99: 100: The PI and SPI NFMs were fabricated via the standard electrospinning procedure by using the PI solutions as the starting materials. For comparison, the same solid content of $30\mathrm{wt}\%$ was used in the current work so as to maintain the similar thickness of the derived NFMs as accurately as possible. The electrospinning parameters, including the voltage $(15\mathrm{kV})$ , the inner diameters of the spinnerets $(0.21\mathrm{mm})$ , the distance between the spinneret to the alumina foil collector $(15\mathrm{cm})$ , the speed of the rotating drum collector $(1000\mathrm{rpm})$ , and the temperature $(20\pm 2^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ and relative humidity $(30\pm 5\%)$ , were also controlled to be the same. The detailed electrospinning fabrication could be illustrated by the preparation of SPI-4 NFM. The dried SPI-4 resin obtained in Section 2.3 was dissolved in DMAc at room temperature with the solid content of $30\mathrm{wt}\%$ . A yellow solution with the absolute viscosity of $3377\mathrm{mPa}$ s was then obtained. The SPI-4 solution was filled into a $5\mathrm{mL}$ syringe and squeezed out through a spinneret by a syringe pump at a speed of $0.2\mathrm{mL/h}$ . The voltage was applied between the syringe and the rolling drum collector. Ultrathin SPI-4 fibers or beads deposited on the aluminum foil substrates. After the electrospinning, the free-standing SPI-4 NFM was obtained by being peeled from the aluminum substrate and then dried in an air-circulated oven at $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $1\mathrm{h}$ to remove the absorbed moisture and the residual solvent. 101: 102: The other NFMs were prepared according to a similar procedure. The free-standing PI NFMs were used to test the FTIR, thermal properties, optical reflectance, and CIE Lab parameters. The PI NFMs on the aluminum substrates were used to evaluate the photolithography patterning properties. [PROPERTY_RESULTS] ## excerpt 1 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=116-118 | keywords=RheologicalProperties; anchors=SPI-6; family=SPI, SPI-6 116: 117: First, the inherent viscosities $([\eta]_{\mathrm{inh}})$ and molecular weights of the PI and SPI resins were detected, and the results are shown in Table 2. The copolymer PI (SPI-2~SPI-6) resins showed the $(\eta)_{\mathrm{inh}}$ values in the range of $0.47\sim 0.78\mathrm{dLg}^{-1}$ , the number of average molecular weights $(M_n)$ in the range of $(2.87\sim 4.70)\times 10^{4}\mathrm{g mol}^{-1}$ , and the weight average molecular weights $(M_w)$ in the range of $(5.13\sim 8.83)\times 10^{4}\mathrm{g mol}^{-1}$ , respectively. These values are all correspondingly lower than those of the pristine PI-1 (BTDA-TMMDA) system $((\eta)_{\mathrm{inh}} = 1.00\mathrm{dLg}^{-1}, M_n = 5.06\times 10^4\mathrm{g mol}^{-1})$ , indicating that incorporation of the TSN monomer decreased the molecular weights of the polymers. This might be due to the decreased reactivity of the TSN diamine due to the existence of the electron-withdrawing sulfone groups. Nevertheless, the current co-polyimides possessed acceptable molecular weights for the following NFM fabrications. 118: ## excerpt 2 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=179-181 | keywords=ProcessingProperties; anchors=SPI-6; family=SPI, SPI-6 179: 180: The thermal stability of the PI NFMs was then evaluated by TGA measurements, and the results are listed in Table 3. It can be clearly detected from the TGA and derivative TGA (DTG) plots of the SPI NFMs shown in Figure 8 that all the polymers showed good stability before $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , after which all the SPI NFMs gradually decomposed and revealed the $5\%$ weight loss temperatures $(T_{5\%})$ in the range of $507.6\sim 527.8^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the $10\%$ weight loss temperatures $(T_{10\%})$ in the range of $558.3\sim 568.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the residual weight ratios at $750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ $(R_{\mathrm{w750}})$ of $64.0\sim 67.0\%$ . All the thermal data of the SPI NFMs were a bit lower than those of the pristine PI-1 NFM when comparing the corresponding thermal data shown in Table 3. This is mainly due to the relatively low thermal stability of sulfone groups at elevated temperatures [31]. The SPI-6 NFM with the highest sulfone contents in the molecular structure showed the lowest thermal data among the polymers. In addition, according to the DTG plots, all the SPI NFMs showed two-stage thermal decomposition behaviors during the test. The first stage occurred around $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which might be due to the decomposition of methyl side chains or sulfone units, and the second stage was recorded around $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which could be ascribed to the decomposition of the main chains of the PIs. 181: ## excerpt 3 | block=b0006 | section=3. The whiteness values of the PI NFMs were calculated as Equation (1), where WI standards for whiteness index,  $L^{*}$  standards for the index of lightness,  $a^{*}$  and  $b^{*}$  stand for chromaticity coefficient.
69: 
70: $$
71: \mathrm {W I} = 1 0 0 - \left[ \left(1 0 0 - L ^ {*}\right) ^ {2} + a ^ {* 2} + b ^ {* 2} \right] ^ {1 / 2} \tag {1}
72: $$
73: 
74: Effects of the structural modification on the photocrosslinking behaviors of the PIs were evaluated as follows. The tested PI solution with a solid content of  $30\mathrm{wt}\%$  was spin-coated onto potassium bromide (KBr) sheets  $(1.0\mathrm{cm} \times 1.0\mathrm{cm} \times 2\mathrm{mm})$  at  $700\mathrm{rpm}$  for  $30\mathrm{s}$  using a spin-coater (MS-B100, Mikasa Co. Ltd., Hiroshima, Japan). Then, the KBr sheets were prebaked at  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $0.5\mathrm{h}$  on a digital hotplate (NA-1A, As-One Co., Ltd., Osaka, Japan). The thickness of the obtained PI coatings was  $5 \pm 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ . The KBr sheets were exposed with a UV light source (IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China, power density:  $400\mathrm{mW/cm}^2$ ). The distance between the UV source and the PI samples was  $1.0 \pm 0.1\mathrm{cm}$ . The UV exposure time was set to be  $0.5, 1.0, 2.0$  and  $3.0\mathrm{min}$ . After exposure, the ATR-FTIR spectra of the PI samples were detected.
75: 
76: The photolithographic patterning procedures of the PI NFMs were investigated as follows. The PI NFM with a thickness of  $20.0 \pm 1.0 \mu \mathrm{m}$  on the aluminum substrate was covered with a photomask containing the "CUGB" pattern together with the corresponding numbers (line width with the unit of a micrometer). Then, the PI NMF/mask system was exposed for 60 s with a high-pressure mercury lamp equipped with a UV-LED system (Model: IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China) with the main ultraviolet wavelength of i-line (365 nm), g-line (405 nm), and h-line (436 nm). Then, the exposed PI NFM was developed in DMF for 5 s, after which the sample was immersed in butyl acetate for 10 s. At last, the PI NFM was baked at  $120^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$  for 60 min. The photolithography results were observed by SEM measurement. The thickness of the pattern was measured with SEM measurement or Dektak XTL Stylus Profilometer (Tucson, AZ, USA).

## excerpt 2 | block=b0005 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=98-102 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=PI NFM; family=PI NFM, PI, NFM
98: # 2.4. Fabrication of PI NFMs via Electrospinning
99: 
100: The PI and SPI NFMs were fabricated via the standard electrospinning procedure by using the PI solutions as the starting materials. For comparison, the same solid content of  $30\mathrm{wt}\%$  was used in the current work so as to maintain the similar thickness of the derived NFMs as accurately as possible. The electrospinning parameters, including the voltage  $(15\mathrm{kV})$ , the inner diameters of the spinnerets  $(0.21\mathrm{mm})$ , the distance between the spinneret to the alumina foil collector  $(15\mathrm{cm})$ , the speed of the rotating drum collector  $(1000\mathrm{rpm})$ , and the temperature  $(20\pm 2^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$  and relative humidity  $(30\pm 5\%)$ , were also controlled to be the same. The detailed electrospinning fabrication could be illustrated by the preparation of SPI-4 NFM. The dried SPI-4 resin obtained in Section 2.3 was dissolved in DMAc at room temperature with the solid content of  $30\mathrm{wt}\%$ . A yellow solution with the absolute viscosity of  $3377\mathrm{mPa}$  s was then obtained. The SPI-4 solution was filled into a  $5\mathrm{mL}$  syringe and squeezed out through a spinneret by a syringe pump at a speed of  $0.2\mathrm{mL/h}$ . The voltage was applied between the syringe and the rolling drum collector. Ultrathin SPI-4 fibers or beads deposited on the aluminum foil substrates. After the electrospinning, the free-standing SPI-4 NFM was obtained by being peeled from the aluminum substrate and then dried in an air-circulated oven at  $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{h}$  to remove the absorbed moisture and the residual solvent.
101: 
102: The other NFMs were prepared according to a similar procedure. The free-standing PI NFMs were used to test the FTIR, thermal properties, optical reflectance, and CIE Lab parameters. The PI NFMs on the aluminum substrates were used to evaluate the photolithography patterning properties.

[PROPERTY_RESULTS]
## excerpt 1 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=187-197 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=PI NFM; family=PI NFM, PI, NFM, PI-1
187: 
188: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
189: Figure 8. TGA and DTG curves of SPI NFMs.
190: 
191: In addition to the TGA measurements for the PI NFMs, the structurally analogous PI films were also prepared for the TMA measurements in order to reveal the glass transition temperatures  $(T_{\mathrm{g}})$  and linear coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) of the polymers. Flexible and tough SPI films with good optical transparency and pale-yellow colors were
192: 
193: obtained by the standard casting procedure from SPI solutions. Effects of the incorporation of TSN units on the glass transition behaviors and the high-temperature dimensional stability of the SPIs were studied. As could be seen from the TMA plots of the PI films shown in Figure 9, all the PI films showed thermally shrinking behaviors at elevated temperatures. For the high-temperature-resistant polymers containing rigid-rod units, this unusual shrinkage beyond the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  is usually considered to be an indication of the orderly arrangement of the molecular chain segments in the polymers. After the ordered arrangements, the polymers will show the usual dimensional expansion with increasing temperatures. The turning points of this dimensional change are usually recorded as the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  values of the polymers. According to the records, the PI films showed the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  values higher than  $330^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . It meant that the incorporation of the TSN components did not deteriorate the high-temperature stability of the pristine PI-1. According to the CTE values of the polymers, incorporation of the TSN units slightly improved the dimensional stability of the PI-1 film at elevated temperatures. The CTE values of the polymers gradually decreased with the increasing contents of TSN in the polymers. The SPI-6 film showed the lowest CTE value of  $50.9 \times 10^{-6} / \mathrm{K}$  in the temperature range of  $50 \sim 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which is lower than that of PI-1 (CTE = 56.5 × 10⁻⁶ / K). This might be due to the ring-fused dibenzothiophene sulfone structure of TSN. The highly conjugated and fused nature of the TSN units efficiently prohibited the free motion of the molecular chain segments at high temperatures. In summary, the high- $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  and low-CTE features of the SPIs are all beneficial for their applications in advanced optoelectronic fabrications.

195: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
196: Figure 9. TMA curves of PI and SPI films. (Insert: locally enlarged TMA curves for clear demonstration with the same X- and Y-axis captions).

## excerpt 2 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=199-201 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=PI NFM; family=PI NFM, PI, NFM, PI-1
199: 
200: The optical properties of the PI NFMs, including the optical reflectance, CIE Lab color parameters, photocrosslinking behaviors upon UV exposure, and photoimageable abilities via photolithography were systemically evaluated, and the related data are summarized in Table 4. First, the UV-Vis reflectance spectra of the SPI NFMs are shown in Figure 10. The SPI NFMs showed the optical reflectance values at the wavelength of  $365\mathrm{nm}$  ( $R_{365}$ ) and  $436\mathrm{nm}$  ( $R_{436}$ ) of  $6.1\sim24.4\%$  and  $66.4\sim84.0\%$ , respectively. These values were all correspondingly lower than those of the pristine PI-1 ( $R_{365} = 34.2\%$ ;  $R_{436} = 85.3\%$ ). This meant that more UV lights were absorbed by the SPI NFMs than that of PI-1. In addition, the optical reflectances of the PI NFMs decreased with the increasing TSN contents in the polymers.
201:

## excerpt 4 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=214-216 | keywords=SensingProperties; anchors=PI NFM; family=PI NFM, NFM
214: 
215: Secondly, the photocrosslinking behaviors of the SPIs upon UV exposure at different times  $(0\sim 3\mathrm{min})$  were investigated, and the results are shown in Figure 11. The investigation used the characteristic absorption strength of benzophenone carbonyl groups at the wavenumber of  $1674~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  in FTIR spectra as the indicator justifying the degree of photocrosslinking reactions. This is mainly due to the fact that once the carbonyl groups are excited by the UV light, the structure will change from  $>C = O$  to  $>\dot{C}$ -OH (triplet). Thus, the absorption strength at  $1674~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  in the FTIR spectra will decrease. If the absorption strength was 0, the photocrosslinking reaction was thought to be finished. The UV exposure times were set to be 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 min, respectively. It can be clearly observed from Figure 11a-e that all the copolymers could undergo the UV excitation reaction upon exposure, and all the photoreactions finished within 3 min according to the photocrosslinking mechanism shown in Figure 11f. It demonstrates that the SPI coatings showed good sensitivity to the emitting wavelengths  $(365\sim 436~\mathrm{nm})$  of the high-pressure mercury lamp sources. This is quite beneficial for the following photolithography processing of the SPI NFMs.

64: # 2.2. Measurements
65: 
66: Inherent viscosities of the PI resins were measured using an Ubbelohde viscometer (Pingxuan Scientific Instrument Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) with a  $0.5\mathrm{g}\mathrm{dL}^{-1}$  NMP solution at  $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Absolute viscosity of the PI solutions was measured using a DV-II+ Pro viscometer (Brookfield, Ametek, MA, USA) at  $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The number average molecular weight  $(M_{n})$  and weight average molecular weight  $(M_w)$  of the PI resins were measured using a gel permeation chromatography (GPC) system (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The solubility of the PI resins was investigated by mixing  $1.0\mathrm{g}$  of the PI resins and  $9.0\mathrm{g}$  of the solvent tested  $(10\mathrm{wt}\%$  solid content) and then stirred for  $24\mathrm{h}$  at room temperature. The solubility was determined visually as three grades: completely soluble  $(++)$ , partially soluble  $(+)$ , and insoluble  $(-)$ , wherein complete soluble indicates a homogenous and clean state without phase separation, precipitation or gel formation, and insoluble indicates no change in the appearance of the resin. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra of the PI NFMs were recorded on a Iraffinity-1S FT-IR spectrometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and the derivative TGA (DTG) of the PI NFMs were performed on a Q50 thermal analysis system (New Castle, Delaware, USA) at a heating rate of  $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$  in nitrogen. Thermo-mechanical analysis (TMA) of the PIs was recorded on a TMA402F3 thermal analysis system (NETZSCH, Selb, Germany) by using films instead of fibrous membranes. The PI films with a thickness of  $25\pm 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$  were prepared according to our previous work [25]. The thermal scanning mode ranges from 50 to  $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  at a heating rate of  $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$  in nitrogen atmosphere. The coefficients of linear thermal expansion (CTE) values of composite films were recorded in the range of  $50 - 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Ltd., Hiroshima, Japan). Then, the KBr sheets were prebaked at  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $0.5\mathrm{h}$  on a digital hotplate (NA-1A, As-One Co., Ltd., Osaka, Japan). The thickness of the obtained PI coatings was  $5 \pm 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ . The KBr sheets were exposed with a UV light source (IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China, power density:  $400\mathrm{mW/cm}^2$ ). The distance between the UV source and the PI samples was  $1.0 \pm 0.1\mathrm{cm}$ . The UV exposure time was set to be  $0.5, 1.0, 2.0$  and  $3.0\mathrm{min}$ . After exposure, the ATR-FTIR spectra of the PI samples were detected.
75: 
76: The photolithographic patterning procedures of the PI NFMs were investigated as follows. The PI NFM with a thickness of  $20.0 \pm 1.0 \mu \mathrm{m}$  on the aluminum substrate was covered with a photomask containing the "CUGB" pattern together with the corresponding numbers (line width with the unit of a micrometer). Then, the PI NMF/mask system was exposed for 60 s with a high-pressure mercury lamp equipped with a UV-LED system (Model: IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China) with the main ultraviolet wavelength of i-line (365 nm), g-line (405 nm), and h-line (436 nm). Then, the exposed PI NFM was developed in DMF for 5 s, after which the sample was immersed in butyl acetate for 10 s. At last, the PI NFM was baked at  $120^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$  for 60 min. The photolithography results were observed by SEM measurement. The thickness of the pattern was measured with SEM measurement or Dektak XTL Stylus Profilometer (Tucson, AZ, USA).

## excerpt 2 | block=b0005 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=98-102 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=SPI NFM; family=SPI NFM, SPI, NFM
98: # 2.4. Fabrication of PI NFMs via Electrospinning
99: 
100: The PI and SPI NFMs were fabricated via the standard electrospinning procedure by using the PI solutions as the starting materials. For comparison, the same solid content of  $30\mathrm{wt}\%$  was used in the current work so as to maintain the similar thickness of the derived NFMs as accurately as possible. The electrospinning parameters, including the voltage  $(15\mathrm{kV})$ , the inner diameters of the spinnerets  $(0.21\mathrm{mm})$ , the distance between the spinneret to the alumina foil collector  $(15\mathrm{cm})$ , the speed of the rotating drum collector  $(1000\mathrm{rpm})$ , and the temperature  $(20\pm 2^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$  and relative humidity  $(30\pm 5\%)$ , were also controlled to be the same. The detailed electrospinning fabrication could be illustrated by the preparation of SPI-4 NFM. The dried SPI-4 resin obtained in Section 2.3 was dissolved in DMAc at room temperature with the solid content of  $30\mathrm{wt}\%$ . A yellow solution with the absolute viscosity of  $3377\mathrm{mPa}$  s was then obtained. The SPI-4 solution was filled into a  $5\mathrm{mL}$  syringe and squeezed out through a spinneret by a syringe pump at a speed of  $0.2\mathrm{mL/h}$ . The voltage was applied between the syringe and the rolling drum collector. Ultrathin SPI-4 fibers or beads deposited on the aluminum foil substrates. After the electrospinning, the free-standing SPI-4 NFM was obtained by being peeled from the aluminum substrate and then dried in an air-circulated oven at  $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{h}$  to remove the absorbed moisture and the residual solvent.
101: 
102: The other NFMs were prepared according to a similar procedure. The free-standing PI NFMs were used to test the FTIR, thermal properties, optical reflectance, and CIE Lab parameters. The PI NFMs on the aluminum substrates were used to evaluate the photolithography patterning properties.

[PROPERTY_RESULTS]
## excerpt 1 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=187-197 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=SPI NFM; family=SPI NFM, SPI, NFM
187: 
188: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
189: Figure 8. TGA and DTG curves of SPI NFMs.
190: 
191: In addition to the TGA measurements for the PI NFMs, the structurally analogous PI films were also prepared for the TMA measurements in order to reveal the glass transition temperatures  $(T_{\mathrm{g}})$  and linear coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) of the polymers. Flexible and tough SPI films with good optical transparency and pale-yellow colors were
192: 
193: obtained by the standard casting procedure from SPI solutions. Effects of the incorporation of TSN units on the glass transition behaviors and the high-temperature dimensional stability of the SPIs were studied. As could be seen from the TMA plots of the PI films shown in Figure 9, all the PI films showed thermally shrinking behaviors at elevated temperatures. For the high-temperature-resistant polymers containing rigid-rod units, this unusual shrinkage beyond the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  is usually considered to be an indication of the orderly arrangement of the molecular chain segments in the polymers. After the ordered arrangements, the polymers will show the usual dimensional expansion with increasing temperatures. The turning points of this dimensional change are usually recorded as the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  values of the polymers. According to the records, the PI films showed the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  values higher than  $330^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . It meant that the incorporation of the TSN components did not deteriorate the high-temperature stability of the pristine PI-1. According to the CTE values of the polymers, incorporation of the TSN units slightly improved the dimensional stability of the PI-1 film at elevated temperatures. The CTE values of the polymers gradually decreased with the increasing contents of TSN in the polymers. The SPI-6 film showed the lowest CTE value of  $50.9 \times 10^{-6} / \mathrm{K}$  in the temperature range of  $50 \sim 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which is lower than that of PI-1 (CTE = 56.5 × 10⁻⁶ / K). This might be due to the ring-fused dibenzothiophene sulfone structure of TSN. The highly conjugated and fused nature of the TSN units efficiently prohibited the free motion of the molecular chain segments at high temperatures. In summary, the high- $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  and low-CTE features of the SPIs are all beneficial for their applications in advanced optoelectronic fabrications.

## excerpt 2 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=199-201 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=SPI NFM; family=SPI NFM, SPI, NFM
199: 
200: The optical properties of the PI NFMs, including the optical reflectance, CIE Lab color parameters, photocrosslinking behaviors upon UV exposure, and photoimageable abilities via photolithography were systemically evaluated, and the related data are summarized in Table 4. First, the UV-Vis reflectance spectra of the SPI NFMs are shown in Figure 10. The SPI NFMs showed the optical reflectance values at the wavelength of  $365\mathrm{nm}$  ( $R_{365}$ ) and  $436\mathrm{nm}$  ( $R_{436}$ ) of  $6.1\sim24.4\%$  and  $66.4\sim84.0\%$ , respectively. These values were all correspondingly lower than those of the pristine PI-1 ( $R_{365} = 34.2\%$ ;  $R_{436} = 85.3\%$ ). This meant that more UV lights were absorbed by the SPI NFMs than that of PI-1. In addition, the optical reflectances of the PI NFMs decreased with the increasing TSN contents in the polymers.
201:

## excerpt 4 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=214-216 | keywords=SensingProperties; anchors=SPI NFM; family=SPI NFM, SPI, NFM
214: 
215: Secondly, the photocrosslinking behaviors of the SPIs upon UV exposure at different times  $(0\sim 3\mathrm{min})$  were investigated, and the results are shown in Figure 11. The investigation used the characteristic absorption strength of benzophenone carbonyl groups at the wavenumber of  $1674~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  in FTIR spectra as the indicator justifying the degree of photocrosslinking reactions. This is mainly due to the fact that once the carbonyl groups are excited by the UV light, the structure will change from  $>C = O$  to  $>\dot{C}$ -OH (triplet). Thus, the absorption strength at  $1674~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  in the FTIR spectra will decrease. If the absorption strength was 0, the photocrosslinking reaction was thought to be finished. The UV exposure times were set to be 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 min, respectively. It can be clearly observed from Figure 11a-e that all the copolymers could undergo the UV excitation reaction upon exposure, and all the photoreactions finished within 3 min according to the photocrosslinking mechanism shown in Figure 11f. It demonstrates that the SPI coatings showed good sensitivity to the emitting wavelengths  $(365\sim 436~\mathrm{nm})$  of the high-pressure mercury lamp sources. This is quite beneficial for the following photolithography processing of the SPI NFMs.

64: # 2.2. Measurements
65: 
66: Inherent viscosities of the PI resins were measured using an Ubbelohde viscometer (Pingxuan Scientific Instrument Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) with a  $0.5\mathrm{g}\mathrm{dL}^{-1}$  NMP solution at  $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Absolute viscosity of the PI solutions was measured using a DV-II+ Pro viscometer (Brookfield, Ametek, MA, USA) at  $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The number average molecular weight  $(M_{n})$  and weight average molecular weight  $(M_w)$  of the PI resins were measured using a gel permeation chromatography (GPC) system (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The solubility of the PI resins was investigated by mixing  $1.0\mathrm{g}$  of the PI resins and  $9.0\mathrm{g}$  of the solvent tested  $(10\mathrm{wt}\%$  solid content) and then stirred for  $24\mathrm{h}$  at room temperature. The solubility was determined visually as three grades: completely soluble  $(++)$ , partially soluble  $(+)$ , and insoluble  $(-)$ , wherein complete soluble indicates a homogenous and clean state without phase separation, precipitation or gel formation, and insoluble indicates no change in the appearance of the resin. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra of the PI NFMs were recorded on a Iraffinity-1S FT-IR spectrometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and the derivative TGA (DTG) of the PI NFMs were performed on a Q50 thermal analysis system (New Castle, Delaware, USA) at a heating rate of  $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$  in nitrogen. Thermo-mechanical analysis (TMA) of the PIs was recorded on a TMA402F3 thermal analysis system (NETZSCH, Selb, Germany) by using films instead of fibrous membranes. The PI films with a thickness of  $25\pm 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$  were prepared according to our previous work [25]. The thermal scanning mode ranges from 50 to  $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  at a heating rate of  $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$  in nitrogen atmosphere. The coefficients of linear thermal expansion (CTE) values of composite films were recorded in the range of  $50 - 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectra of the PI NFMs were recorded on a Hitachi U-3210 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the PI NFMs was conducted on a Rigaku D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Tokyo, Japan). The micro-morphologies of the PI patterns were detected via a JSM-6700F (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan)
67: 
68: field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). The CIE (Comission Internationale del' Eclairage, Vienna, Austria) Lab optical parameters of the PI NFMs were measured using an X-rite color i7 spectrophotometer (Michigan, USA). For the color parameters,  $L^{*}$  is the lightness, where 100 means white and 0 implies black. A positive  $a^{*}$  means a red color, and a negative one indicates a green color. A positive  $b^{*}$  means a yellow color, and a negative one indicates a blue color. The whiteness values of the PI NFMs were calculated as Equation (1), where WI standards for whiteness index,  $L^{*}$  standards for the index of lightness,  $a^{*}$  and  $b^{*}$  stand for chromaticity coefficient.
69: 
70: $$
71: \mathrm {W I} = 1 0 0 - \left[ \left(1 0 0 - L ^ {*}\right) ^ {2} + a ^ {* 2} + b ^ {* 2} \right] ^ {1 / 2} \tag {1}
72: $$
73: 
74: Effects of the structural modification on the photocrosslinking behaviors of the PIs were evaluated as follows. The tested PI solution with a solid content of  $30\mathrm{wt}\%$  was spin-coated onto potassium bromide (KBr) sheets  $(1.0\mathrm{cm} \times 1.0\mathrm{cm} \times 2\mathrm{mm})$  at  $700\mathrm{rpm}$  for  $30\mathrm{s}$  using a spin-coater (MS-B100, Mikasa Co. Ltd., Hiroshima, Japan). Then, the KBr sheets were prebaked at  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $0.5\mathrm{h}$  on a digital hotplate (NA-1A, As-One Co., Ltd., Osaka, Japan). The thickness of the obtained PI coatings was  $5 \pm 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ . The KBr sheets were exposed with a UV light source (IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China, power density:  $400\mathrm{mW/cm}^2$ ). The distance between the UV source and the PI samples was  $1.0 \pm 0.1\mathrm{cm}$ . The UV exposure time was set to be  $0.5, 1.0, 2.0$  and  $3.0\mathrm{min}$ . After exposure, the ATR-FTIR spectra of the PI samples were detected.
75: 
76: The photolithographic patterning procedures of the PI NFMs were investigated as follows. The PI NFM with a thickness of  $20.0 \pm 1.0 \mu \mathrm{m}$  on the aluminum substrate was covered with a photomask containing the "CUGB" pattern together with the corresponding numbers (line width with the unit of a micrometer). Then, the PI NMF/mask system was exposed for 60 s with a high-pressure mercury lamp equipped with a UV-LED system (Model: IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China) with the main ultraviolet wavelength of i-line (365 nm), g-line (405 nm), and h-line (436 nm). Then, the exposed PI NFM was developed in DMF for 5 s, after which the sample was immersed in butyl acetate for 10 s. At last, the PI NFM was baked at  $120^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$  for 60 min. The photolithography results were observed by SEM measurement. The thickness of the pattern was measured with SEM measurement or Dektak XTL Stylus Profilometer (Tucson, AZ, USA).

76: The photolithographic patterning procedures of the PI NFMs were investigated as follows. The PI NFM with a thickness of  $20.0 \pm 1.0 \mu \mathrm{m}$  on the aluminum substrate was covered with a photomask containing the "CUGB" pattern together with the corresponding numbers (line width with the unit of a micrometer). Then, the PI NMF/mask system was exposed for 60 s with a high-pressure mercury lamp equipped with a UV-LED system (Model: IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China) with the main ultraviolet wavelength of i-line (365 nm), g-line (405 nm), and h-line (436 nm). Then, the exposed PI NFM was developed in DMF for 5 s, after which the sample was immersed in butyl acetate for 10 s. At last, the PI NFM was baked at  $120^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$  for 60 min. The photolithography results were observed by SEM measurement. The thickness of the pattern was measured with SEM measurement or Dektak XTL Stylus Profilometer (Tucson, AZ, USA).

## excerpt 2 | block=b0005 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=98-102 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=SPI NFM; family=SPI NFM, SPI, NFM
98: # 2.4. Fabrication of PI NFMs via Electrospinning
99: 
100: The PI and SPI NFMs were fabricated via the standard electrospinning procedure by using the PI solutions as the starting materials. For comparison, the same solid content of  $30\mathrm{wt}\%$  was used in the current work so as to maintain the similar thickness of the derived NFMs as accurately as possible. The electrospinning parameters, including the voltage  $(15\mathrm{kV})$ , the inner diameters of the spinnerets  $(0.21\mathrm{mm})$ , the distance between the spinneret to the alumina foil collector  $(15\mathrm{cm})$ , the speed of the rotating drum collector  $(1000\mathrm{rpm})$ , and the temperature  $(20\pm 2^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$  and relative humidity  $(30\pm 5\%)$ , were also controlled to be the same. The detailed electrospinning fabrication could be illustrated by the preparation of SPI-4 NFM. The dried SPI-4 resin obtained in Section 2.3 was dissolved in DMAc at room temperature with the solid content of  $30\mathrm{wt}\%$ . A yellow solution with the absolute viscosity of  $3377\mathrm{mPa}$  s was then obtained. The SPI-4 solution was filled into a  $5\mathrm{mL}$  syringe and squeezed out through a spinneret by a syringe pump at a speed of  $0.2\mathrm{mL/h}$ . The voltage was applied between the syringe and the rolling drum collector. Ultrathin SPI-4 fibers or beads deposited on the aluminum foil substrates. After the electrospinning, the free-standing SPI-4 NFM was obtained by being peeled from the aluminum substrate and then dried in an air-circulated oven at  $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{h}$  to remove the absorbed moisture and the residual solvent.
101: 
102: The other NFMs were prepared according to a similar procedure. The free-standing PI NFMs were used to test the FTIR, thermal properties, optical reflectance, and CIE Lab parameters. The PI NFMs on the aluminum substrates were used to evaluate the photolithography patterning properties.

[PROPERTY_RESULTS]
## excerpt 1 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=187-197 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=SPI NFM; family=SPI NFM, SPI, NFM
187: 
188: ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
189: Figure 8. TGA and DTG curves of SPI NFMs.
190: 
191: In addition to the TGA measurements for the PI NFMs, the structurally analogous PI films were also prepared for the TMA measurements in order to reveal the glass transition temperatures  $(T_{\mathrm{g}})$  and linear coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) of the polymers. Flexible and tough SPI films with good optical transparency and pale-yellow colors were
192: 
193: obtained by the standard casting procedure from SPI solutions. Effects of the incorporation of TSN units on the glass transition behaviors and the high-temperature dimensional stability of the SPIs were studied. As could be seen from the TMA plots of the PI films shown in Figure 9, all the PI films showed thermally shrinking behaviors at elevated temperatures. For the high-temperature-resistant polymers containing rigid-rod units, this unusual shrinkage beyond the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  is usually considered to be an indication of the orderly arrangement of the molecular chain segments in the polymers. After the ordered arrangements, the polymers will show the usual dimensional expansion with increasing temperatures. The turning points of this dimensional change are usually recorded as the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  values of the polymers. According to the records, the PI films showed the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  values higher than  $330^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . It meant that the incorporation of the TSN components did not deteriorate the high-temperature stability of the pristine PI-1. According to the CTE values of the polymers, incorporation of the TSN units slightly improved the dimensional stability of the PI-1 film at elevated temperatures. The CTE values of the polymers gradually decreased with the increasing contents of TSN in the polymers. The SPI-6 film showed the lowest CTE value of  $50.9 \times 10^{-6} / \mathrm{K}$  in the temperature range of  $50 \sim 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which is lower than that of PI-1 (CTE = 56.5 × 10⁻⁶ / K). This might be due to the ring-fused dibenzothiophene sulfone structure of TSN. The highly conjugated and fused nature of the TSN units efficiently prohibited the free motion of the molecular chain segments at high temperatures. In summary, the high- $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  and low-CTE features of the SPIs are all beneficial for their applications in advanced optoelectronic fabrications.

## excerpt 2 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=199-201 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=SPI NFM; family=SPI NFM, SPI, NFM
199: 
200: The optical properties of the PI NFMs, including the optical reflectance, CIE Lab color parameters, photocrosslinking behaviors upon UV exposure, and photoimageable abilities via photolithography were systemically evaluated, and the related data are summarized in Table 4. First, the UV-Vis reflectance spectra of the SPI NFMs are shown in Figure 10. The SPI NFMs showed the optical reflectance values at the wavelength of  $365\mathrm{nm}$  ( $R_{365}$ ) and  $436\mathrm{nm}$  ( $R_{436}$ ) of  $6.1\sim24.4\%$  and  $66.4\sim84.0\%$ , respectively. These values were all correspondingly lower than those of the pristine PI-1 ( $R_{365} = 34.2\%$ ;  $R_{436} = 85.3\%$ ). This meant that more UV lights were absorbed by the SPI NFMs than that of PI-1. In addition, the optical reflectances of the PI NFMs decreased with the increasing TSN contents in the polymers.
201:

## excerpt 4 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=214-216 | keywords=SensingProperties; anchors=SPI NFM; family=SPI NFM, SPI, NFM
214: 
215: Secondly, the photocrosslinking behaviors of the SPIs upon UV exposure at different times  $(0\sim 3\mathrm{min})$  were investigated, and the results are shown in Figure 11. The investigation used the characteristic absorption strength of benzophenone carbonyl groups at the wavenumber of  $1674~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  in FTIR spectra as the indicator justifying the degree of photocrosslinking reactions. This is mainly due to the fact that once the carbonyl groups are excited by the UV light, the structure will change from  $>C = O$  to  $>\dot{C}$ -OH (triplet). Thus, the absorption strength at  $1674~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  in the FTIR spectra will decrease. If the absorption strength was 0, the photocrosslinking reaction was thought to be finished. The UV exposure times were set to be 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 min, respectively. It can be clearly observed from Figure 11a-e that all the copolymers could undergo the UV excitation reaction upon exposure, and all the photoreactions finished within 3 min according to the photocrosslinking mechanism shown in Figure 11f. It demonstrates that the SPI coatings showed good sensitivity to the emitting wavelengths  $(365\sim 436~\mathrm{nm})$  of the high-pressure mercury lamp sources. This is quite beneficial for the following photolithography processing of the SPI NFMs.

## excerpt 1 | block=b0005 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=64-77 | keywords=OpticalProperties; family=NFM
64: # 2.2. Measurements
65: 
66: Inherent viscosities of the PI resins were measured using an Ubbelohde viscometer (Pingxuan Scientific Instrument Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) with a  $0.5\mathrm{g}\mathrm{dL}^{-1}$  NMP solution at  $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Absolute viscosity of the PI solutions was measured using a DV-II+ Pro viscometer (Brookfield, Ametek, MA, USA) at  $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The number average molecular weight  $(M_{n})$  and weight average molecular weight  $(M_w)$  of the PI resins were measured using a gel permeation chromatography (GPC) system (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The solubility of the PI resins was investigated by mixing  $1.0\mathrm{g}$  of the PI resins and  $9.0\mathrm{g}$  of the solvent tested  $(10\mathrm{wt}\%$  solid content) and then stirred for  $24\mathrm{h}$  at room temperature. The solubility was determined visually as three grades: completely soluble  $(++)$ , partially soluble  $(+)$ , and insoluble  $(-)$ , wherein complete soluble indicates a homogenous and clean state without phase separation, precipitation or gel formation, and insoluble indicates no change in the appearance of the resin. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra of the PI NFMs were recorded on a Iraffinity-1S FT-IR spectrometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and the derivative TGA (DTG) of the PI NFMs were performed on a Q50 thermal analysis system (New Castle, Delaware, USA) at a heating rate of  $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$  in nitrogen. Thermo-mechanical analysis (TMA) of the PIs was recorded on a TMA402F3 thermal analysis system (NETZSCH, Selb, Germany) by using films instead of fibrous membranes. The PI films with a thickness of  $25\pm 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$  were prepared according to our previous work [25]. The thermal scanning mode ranges from 50 to  $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  at a heating rate of  $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$  in nitrogen atmosphere. The coefficients of linear thermal expansion (CTE) values of composite films were recorded in the range of  $50 - 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectra of the PI NFMs were recorded on a Hitachi U-3210 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the PI NFMs was conducted on a Rigaku D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Tokyo, Japan). The micro-morphologies of the PI patterns were detected via a JSM-6700F (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan)
67: 
68: field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). The CIE (Comission Internationale del' Eclairage, Vienna, Austria) Lab optical parameters of the PI NFMs were measured using an X-rite color i7 spectrophotometer (Michigan, USA). For the color parameters,  $L^{*}$  is the lightness, where 100 means white and 0 implies black. A positive  $a^{*}$  means a red color, and a negative one indicates a green color. A positive  $b^{*}$  means a yellow color, and a negative one indicates a blue color. The whiteness values of the PI NFMs were calculated as Equation (1), where WI standards for whiteness index,  $L^{*}$  standards for the index of lightness,  $a^{*}$  and  $b^{*}$  stand for chromaticity coefficient.
69: 
70: $$
71: \mathrm {W I} = 1 0 0 - \left[ \left(1 0 0 - L ^ {*}\right) ^ {2} + a ^ {* 2} + b ^ {* 2} \right] ^ {1 / 2} \tag {1}
72: $$
73: 
74: Effects of the structural modification on the photocrosslinking behaviors of the PIs were evaluated as follows. The tested PI solution with a solid content of  $30\mathrm{wt}\%$  was spin-coated onto potassium bromide (KBr) sheets  $(1.0\mathrm{cm} \times 1.0\mathrm{cm} \times 2\mathrm{mm})$  at  $700\mathrm{rpm}$  for  $30\mathrm{s}$  using a spin-coater (MS-B100, Mikasa Co. Ltd., Hiroshima, Japan). Then, the KBr sheets were prebaked at  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $0.5\mathrm{h}$  on a digital hotplate (NA-1A, As-One Co., Ltd., Osaka, Japan). The thickness of the obtained PI coatings was  $5 \pm 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ . The KBr sheets were exposed with a UV light source (IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China, power density:  $400\mathrm{mW/cm}^2$ ). The distance between the UV source and the PI samples was  $1.0 \pm 0.1\mathrm{cm}$ . The UV exposure time was set to be  $0.5, 1.0, 2.0$  and  $3.0\mathrm{min}$ . After exposure, the ATR-FTIR spectra of the PI samples were detected.
75: 
76: The photolithographic patterning procedures of the PI NFMs were investigated as follows. The PI NFM with a thickness of  $20.0 \pm 1.0 \mu \mathrm{m}$  on the aluminum substrate was covered with a photomask containing the "CUGB" pattern together with the corresponding numbers (line width with the unit of a micrometer). Then, the PI NMF/mask system was exposed for 60 s with a high-pressure mercury lamp equipped with a UV-LED system (Model: IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China) with the main ultraviolet wavelength of i-line (365 nm), g-line (405 nm), and h-line (436 nm). Then, the exposed PI NFM was developed in DMF for 5 s, after which the sample was immersed in butyl acetate for 10 s. At last, the PI NFM was baked at  $120^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$  for 60 min. The photolithography results were observed by SEM measurement. The thickness of the pattern was measured with SEM measurement or Dektak XTL Stylus Profilometer (Tucson, AZ, USA).

100: The PI and SPI NFMs were fabricated via the standard electrospinning procedure by using the PI solutions as the starting materials. For comparison, the same solid content of  $30\mathrm{wt}\%$  was used in the current work so as to maintain the similar thickness of the derived NFMs as accurately as possible. The electrospinning parameters, including the voltage  $(15\mathrm{kV})$ , the inner diameters of the spinnerets  $(0.21\mathrm{mm})$ , the distance between the spinneret to the alumina foil collector  $(15\mathrm{cm})$ , the speed of the rotating drum collector  $(1000\mathrm{rpm})$ , and the temperature  $(20\pm 2^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$  and relative humidity  $(30\pm 5\%)$ , were also controlled to be the same. The detailed electrospinning fabrication could be illustrated by the preparation of SPI-4 NFM. The dried SPI-4 resin obtained in Section 2.3 was dissolved in DMAc at room temperature with the solid content of  $30\mathrm{wt}\%$ . A yellow solution with the absolute viscosity of  $3377\mathrm{mPa}$  s was then obtained. The SPI-4 solution was filled into a  $5\mathrm{mL}$  syringe and squeezed out through a spinneret by a syringe pump at a speed of  $0.2\mathrm{mL/h}$ . The voltage was applied between the syringe and the rolling drum collector. Ultrathin SPI-4 fibers or beads deposited on the aluminum foil substrates. After the electrospinning, the free-standing SPI-4 NFM was obtained by being peeled from the aluminum substrate and then dried in an air-circulated oven at  $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{h}$  to remove the absorbed moisture and the residual solvent.
101: 
102: The other NFMs were prepared according to a similar procedure. The free-standing PI NFMs were used to test the FTIR, thermal properties, optical reflectance, and CIE Lab parameters. The PI NFMs on the aluminum substrates were used to evaluate the photolithography patterning properties.

[PROPERTY_RESULTS]
## excerpt 1 | block=b0006 | section=3. Results and Discussion | lines=190-194 | keywords=OpticalProperties; anchors=PI film, PI-1 film; family=PI film, PI, PI-1 film, PI-1
190: 
191: In addition to the TGA measurements for the PI NFMs, the structurally analogous PI films were also prepared for the TMA measurements in order to reveal the glass transition temperatures  $(T_{\mathrm{g}})$  and linear coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) of the polymers. Flexible and tough SPI films with good optical transparency and pale-yellow colors were
192: 
193: obtained by the standard casting procedure from SPI solutions. Effects of the incorporation of TSN units on the glass transition behaviors and the high-temperature dimensional stability of the SPIs were studied. As could be seen from the TMA plots of the PI films shown in Figure 9, all the PI films showed thermally shrinking behaviors at elevated temperatures. For the high-temperature-resistant polymers containing rigid-rod units, this unusual shrinkage beyond the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  is usually considered to be an indication of the orderly arrangement of the molecular chain segments in the polymers. After the ordered arrangements, the polymers will show the usual dimensional expansion with increasing temperatures. The turning points of this dimensional change are usually recorded as the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  values of the polymers. According to the records, the PI films showed the  $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  values higher than  $330^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . It meant that the incorporation of the TSN components did not deteriorate the high-temperature stability of the pristine PI-1. According to the CTE values of the polymers, incorporation of the TSN units slightly improved the dimensional stability of the PI-1 film at elevated temperatures. The CTE values of the polymers gradually decreased with the increasing contents of TSN in the polymers. The SPI-6 film showed the lowest CTE value of  $50.9 \times 10^{-6} / \mathrm{K}$  in the temperature range of  $50 \sim 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which is lower than that of PI-1 (CTE = 56.5 × 10⁻⁶ / K). This might be due to the ring-fused dibenzothiophene sulfone structure of TSN. The highly conjugated and fused nature of the TSN units efficiently prohibited the free motion of the molecular chain segments at high temperatures. In summary, the high- $T_{\mathrm{g}}$  and low-CTE features of the SPIs are all beneficial for their applications in advanced optoelectronic fabrications.

## excerpt 1 | block=b0005 | section=2. Materials and Methods | lines=64-77 | keywords=OpticalProperties
64: # 2.2. Measurements
65: 
66: Inherent viscosities of the PI resins were measured using an Ubbelohde viscometer (Pingxuan Scientific Instrument Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) with a  $0.5\mathrm{g}\mathrm{dL}^{-1}$  NMP solution at  $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Absolute viscosity of the PI solutions was measured using a DV-II+ Pro viscometer (Brookfield, Ametek, MA, USA) at  $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The number average molecular weight  $(M_{n})$  and weight average molecular weight  $(M_w)$  of the PI resins were measured using a gel permeation chromatography (GPC) system (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The solubility of the PI resins was investigated by mixing  $1.0\mathrm{g}$  of the PI resins and  $9.0\mathrm{g}$  of the solvent tested  $(10\mathrm{wt}\%$  solid content) and then stirred for  $24\mathrm{h}$  at room temperature. The solubility was determined visually as three grades: completely soluble  $(++)$ , partially soluble  $(+)$ , and insoluble  $(-)$ , wherein complete soluble indicates a homogenous and clean state without phase separation, precipitation or gel formation, and insoluble indicates no change in the appearance of the resin. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra of the PI NFMs were recorded on a Iraffinity-1S FT-IR spectrometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and the derivative TGA (DTG) of the PI NFMs were performed on a Q50 thermal analysis system (New Castle, Delaware, USA) at a heating rate of  $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$  in nitrogen. Thermo-mechanical analysis (TMA) of the PIs was recorded on a TMA402F3 thermal analysis system (NETZSCH, Selb, Germany) by using films instead of fibrous membranes. The PI films with a thickness of  $25\pm 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$  were prepared according to our previous work [25]. The thermal scanning mode ranges from 50 to  $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  at a heating rate of  $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$  in nitrogen atmosphere. The coefficients of linear thermal expansion (CTE) values of composite films were recorded in the range of  $50 - 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectra of the PI NFMs were recorded on a Hitachi U-3210 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the PI NFMs was conducted on a Rigaku D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Tokyo, Japan). The micro-morphologies of the PI patterns were detected via a JSM-6700F (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan)
67: 
68: field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). The CIE (Comission Internationale del' Eclairage, Vienna, Austria) Lab optical parameters of the PI NFMs were measured using an X-rite color i7 spectrophotometer (Michigan, USA). For the color parameters,  $L^{*}$  is the lightness, where 100 means white and 0 implies black. A positive  $a^{*}$  means a red color, and a negative one indicates a green color. A positive  $b^{*}$  means a yellow color, and a negative one indicates a blue color. The whiteness values of the PI NFMs were calculated as Equation (1), where WI standards for whiteness index,  $L^{*}$  standards for the index of lightness,  $a^{*}$  and  $b^{*}$  stand for chromaticity coefficient.
69: 
70: $$
71: \mathrm {W I} = 1 0 0 - \left[ \left(1 0 0 - L ^ {*}\right) ^ {2} + a ^ {* 2} + b ^ {* 2} \right] ^ {1 / 2} \tag {1}
72: $$
73: 
74: Effects of the structural modification on the photocrosslinking behaviors of the PIs were evaluated as follows. The tested PI solution with a solid content of  $30\mathrm{wt}\%$  was spin-coated onto potassium bromide (KBr) sheets  $(1.0\mathrm{cm} \times 1.0\mathrm{cm} \times 2\mathrm{mm})$  at  $700\mathrm{rpm}$  for  $30\mathrm{s}$  using a spin-coater (MS-B100, Mikasa Co. Ltd., Hiroshima, Japan). Then, the KBr sheets were prebaked at  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $0.5\mathrm{h}$  on a digital hotplate (NA-1A, As-One Co., Ltd., Osaka, Japan). The thickness of the obtained PI coatings was  $5 \pm 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ . The KBr sheets were exposed with a UV light source (IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China, power density:  $400\mathrm{mW/cm}^2$ ). The distance between the UV source and the PI samples was  $1.0 \pm 0.1\mathrm{cm}$ . The UV exposure time was set to be  $0.5, 1.0, 2.0$  and  $3.0\mathrm{min}$ . After exposure, the ATR-FTIR spectra of the PI samples were detected.
75: 
76: The photolithographic patterning procedures of the PI NFMs were investigated as follows. The PI NFM with a thickness of  $20.0 \pm 1.0 \mu \mathrm{m}$  on the aluminum substrate was covered with a photomask containing the "CUGB" pattern together with the corresponding numbers (line width with the unit of a micrometer). Then, the PI NMF/mask system was exposed for 60 s with a high-pressure mercury lamp equipped with a UV-LED system (Model: IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China) with the main ultraviolet wavelength of i-line (365 nm), g-line (405 nm), and h-line (436 nm). Then, the exposed PI NFM was developed in DMF for 5 s, after which the sample was immersed in butyl acetate for 10 s. At last, the PI NFM was baked at  $120^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$  for 60 min. The photolithography results were observed by SEM measurement. The thickness of the pattern was measured with SEM measurement or Dektak XTL Stylus Profilometer (Tucson, AZ, USA).
77:

Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the PI NFMs was conducted on a Rigaku D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Tokyo, Japan). The micro-morphologies of the PI patterns were detected via a JSM-6700F (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan)
67: 
68: field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). The CIE (Comission Internationale del' Eclairage, Vienna, Austria) Lab optical parameters of the PI NFMs were measured using an X-rite color i7 spectrophotometer (Michigan, USA). For the color parameters,  $L^{*}$  is the lightness, where 100 means white and 0 implies black. A positive  $a^{*}$  means a red color, and a negative one indicates a green color. A positive  $b^{*}$  means a yellow color, and a negative one indicates a blue color. The whiteness values of the PI NFMs were calculated as Equation (1), where WI standards for whiteness index,  $L^{*}$  standards for the index of lightness,  $a^{*}$  and  $b^{*}$  stand for chromaticity coefficient.
69: 
70: $$
71: \mathrm {W I} = 1 0 0 - \left[ \left(1 0 0 - L ^ {*}\right) ^ {2} + a ^ {* 2} + b ^ {* 2} \right] ^ {1 / 2} \tag {1}
72: $$
73: 
74: Effects of the structural modification on the photocrosslinking behaviors of the PIs were evaluated as follows. The tested PI solution with a solid content of  $30\mathrm{wt}\%$  was spin-coated onto potassium bromide (KBr) sheets  $(1.0\mathrm{cm} \times 1.0\mathrm{cm} \times 2\mathrm{mm})$  at  $700\mathrm{rpm}$  for  $30\mathrm{s}$  using a spin-coater (MS-B100, Mikasa Co. Ltd., Hiroshima, Japan). Then, the KBr sheets were prebaked at  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $0.5\mathrm{h}$  on a digital hotplate (NA-1A, As-One Co., Ltd., Osaka, Japan). The thickness of the obtained PI coatings was  $5 \pm 0.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ . The KBr sheets were exposed with a UV light source (IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China, power density:  $400\mathrm{mW/cm}^2$ ). The distance between the UV source and the PI samples was  $1.0 \pm 0.1\mathrm{cm}$ . The UV exposure time was set to be  $0.5, 1.0, 2.0$  and  $3.0\mathrm{min}$ . After exposure, the ATR-FTIR spectra of the PI samples were detected.
75: 
76: The photolithographic patterning procedures of the PI NFMs were investigated as follows. The PI NFM with a thickness of  $20.0 \pm 1.0 \mu \mathrm{m}$  on the aluminum substrate was covered with a photomask containing the "CUGB" pattern together with the corresponding numbers (line width with the unit of a micrometer). Then, the PI NMF/mask system was exposed for 60 s with a high-pressure mercury lamp equipped with a UV-LED system (Model: IMS-811A-0606, IUVOT Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, Changzhou, China) with the main ultraviolet wavelength of i-line (365 nm), g-line (405 nm), and h-line (436 nm). Then, the exposed PI NFM was developed in DMF for 5 s, after which the sample was immersed in butyl acetate for 10 s. At last, the PI NFM was baked at  $120^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$  for 60 min. The photolithography results were observed by SEM measurement. The thickness of the pattern was measured with SEM measurement or Dektak XTL Stylus Profilometer (Tucson, AZ, USA).
